speeches.speech: Let fame, that all hunt after in their lives, Live register'd upon our brazen tombs And then grace us in the disgrace of death; When, spite of cormorant devouring Time, The endeavor of this present breath may buy That honour which shall bate his scythe's keen edge And make us heirs of all eternity. Therefore, brave conquerors,--for so you are, That war against your own affections And the huge army of the world's desires,-- Our late edict shall strongly stand in force: Navarre shall be the wonder of the world; Our court shall be a little Academe, Still and contemplative in living art. You three, Biron, Dumain, and Longaville, Have sworn for three years' term to live with me My fellow-scholars, and to keep those statutes That are recorded in this schedule here: Your oaths are pass'd; and now subscribe your names, That his own hand may strike his honour down That violates the smallest branch herein: If you are arm'd to do as sworn to do, Subscribe to your deep oaths, and keep it too.
lines: Let fame, that all hunt after in their lives,
Live register'd upon our brazen tombs
And then grace us in the disgrace of death;
When, spite of cormorant devouring Time,
The endeavor of this present breath may buy
That honour which shall bate his scythe's keen edge
And make us heirs of all eternity.
Therefore, brave conquerors,--for so you are,
That war against your own affections
And the huge army of the world's desires,--
Our late edict shall strongly stand in force:
Navarre shall be the wonder of the world;
Our court shall be a little Academe,
Still and contemplative in living art.
You three, Biron, Dumain, and Longaville,
Have sworn for three years' term to live with me
My fellow-scholars, and to keep those statutes
That are recorded in this schedule here:
Your oaths are pass'd; and now subscribe your names,
That his own hand may strike his honour down
That violates the smallest branch herein:
If you are arm'd to do as sworn to do,
Subscribe to your deep oaths, and keep it too.

speeches.speech: I am resolved; 'tis but a three years' fast: The mind shall banquet, though the body pine: Fat paunches have lean pates, and dainty bits Make rich the ribs, but bankrupt quite the wits.
lines: I am resolved; 'tis but a three years' fast:
The mind shall banquet, though the body pine:
Fat paunches have lean pates, and dainty bits
Make rich the ribs, but bankrupt quite the wits.

speeches.speech: My loving lord, Dumain is mortified: The grosser manner of these world's delights He throws upon the gross world's baser slaves: To love, to wealth, to pomp, I pine and die; With all these living in philosophy.
lines: My loving lord, Dumain is mortified:
The grosser manner of these world's delights
He throws upon the gross world's baser slaves:
To love, to wealth, to pomp, I pine and die;
With all these living in philosophy.

speeches.speech: I can but say their protestation over; So much, dear liege, I have already sworn, That is, to live and study here three years. But there are other strict observances; As, not to see a woman in that term, Which I hope well is not enrolled there; And one day in a week to touch no food And but one meal on every day beside, The which I hope is not enrolled there; And then, to sleep but three hours in the night, And not be seen to wink of all the day-- When I was wont to think no harm all night And make a dark night too of half the day-- Which I hope well is not enrolled there: O, these are barren tasks, too hard to keep, Not to see ladies, study, fast, not sleep!
lines: I can but say their protestation over;
So much, dear liege, I have already sworn,
That is, to live and study here three years.
But there are other strict observances;
As, not to see a woman in that term,
Which I hope well is not enrolled there;
And one day in a week to touch no food
And but one meal on every day beside,
The which I hope is not enrolled there;
And then, to sleep but three hours in the night,
And not be seen to wink of all the day--
When I was wont to think no harm all night
And make a dark night too of half the day--
Which I hope well is not enrolled there:
O, these are barren tasks, too hard to keep,
Not to see ladies, study, fast, not sleep!

speeches.speech: Your oath is pass'd to pass away from these.
lines: Your oath is pass'd to pass away from these.

speeches.speech: Let me say no, my liege, an if you please: I only swore to study with your grace And stay here in your court for three years' space.
lines: Let me say no, my liege, an if you please:
I only swore to study with your grace
And stay here in your court for three years' space.

speeches.speech: You swore to that, Biron, and to the rest.
lines: You swore to that, Biron, and to the rest.

speeches.speech: By yea and nay, sir, then I swore in jest. What is the end of study? let me know.
lines: By yea and nay, sir, then I swore in jest.
What is the end of study? let me know.

speeches.speech: Why, that to know, which else we should not know.
lines: Why, that to know, which else we should not know.

speeches.speech: Things hid and barr'd, you mean, from common sense?
lines: Things hid and barr'd, you mean, from common sense?

speeches.speech: Ay, that is study's godlike recompense.
lines: Ay, that is study's godlike recompense.

speeches.speech: Come on, then; I will swear to study so, To know the thing I am forbid to know: As thus,--to study where I well may dine, When I to feast expressly am forbid; Or study where to meet some mistress fine, When mistresses from common sense are hid; Or, having sworn too hard a keeping oath, Study to break it and not break my troth. If study's gain be thus and this be so, Study knows that which yet it doth not know: Swear me to this, and I will ne'er say no.
lines: Come on, then; I will swear to study so,
To know the thing I am forbid to know:
As thus,--to study where I well may dine,
When I to feast expressly am forbid;
Or study where to meet some mistress fine,
When mistresses from common sense are hid;
Or, having sworn too hard a keeping oath,
Study to break it and not break my troth.
If study's gain be thus and this be so,
Study knows that which yet it doth not know:
Swear me to this, and I will ne'er say no.

speeches.speech: These be the stops that hinder study quite And train our intellects to vain delight.
lines: These be the stops that hinder study quite
And train our intellects to vain delight.

speeches.speech: Why, all delights are vain; but that most vain, Which with pain purchased doth inherit pain: As, painfully to pore upon a book To seek the light of truth; while truth the while Doth falsely blind the eyesight of his look: Light seeking light doth light of light beguile: So, ere you find where light in darkness lies, Your light grows dark by losing of your eyes. Study me how to please the eye indeed By fixing it upon a fairer eye, Who dazzling so, that eye shall be his heed And give him light that it was blinded by. Study is like the heaven's glorious sun That will not be deep-search'd with saucy looks: Small have continual plodders ever won Save base authority from others' books These earthly godfathers of heaven's lights That give a name to every fixed star Have no more profit of their shining nights Than those that walk and wot not what they are. Too much to know is to know nought but fame; And every godfather can give a name.
lines: Why, all delights are vain; but that most vain,
Which with pain purchased doth inherit pain:
As, painfully to pore upon a book
To seek the light of truth; while truth the while
Doth falsely blind the eyesight of his look:
Light seeking light doth light of light beguile:
So, ere you find where light in darkness lies,
Your light grows dark by losing of your eyes.
Study me how to please the eye indeed
By fixing it upon a fairer eye,
Who dazzling so, that eye shall be his heed
And give him light that it was blinded by.
Study is like the heaven's glorious sun
That will not be deep-search'd with saucy looks:
Small have continual plodders ever won
Save base authority from others' books
These earthly godfathers of heaven's lights
That give a name to every fixed star
Have no more profit of their shining nights
Than those that walk and wot not what they are.
Too much to know is to know nought but fame;
And every godfather can give a name.

speeches.speech: How well he's read, to reason against reading!
lines: How well he's read, to reason against reading!

speeches.speech: Proceeded well, to stop all good proceeding!
lines: Proceeded well, to stop all good proceeding!

speeches.speech: He weeds the corn and still lets grow the weeding.
lines: He weeds the corn and still lets grow the weeding.

speeches.speech: The spring is near when green geese are a-breeding.
lines: The spring is near when green geese are a-breeding.

speeches.speech: How follows that?
lines: How follows that?

speeches.speech: Fit in his place and time.
lines: Fit in his place and time.

speeches.speech: In reason nothing.
lines: In reason nothing.

speeches.speech: Something then in rhyme.
lines: Something then in rhyme.

speeches.speech: Biron is like an envious sneaping frost, That bites the first-born infants of the spring.
lines: Biron is like an envious sneaping frost,
That bites the first-born infants of the spring.

speeches.speech: Well, say I am; why should proud summer boast Before the birds have any cause to sing? Why should I joy in any abortive birth? At Christmas I no more desire a rose Than wish a snow in May's new-fangled mirth; But like of each thing that in season grows. So you, to study now it is too late, Climb o'er the house to unlock the little gate.
lines: Well, say I am; why should proud summer boast
Before the birds have any cause to sing?
Why should I joy in any abortive birth?
At Christmas I no more desire a rose
Than wish a snow in May's new-fangled mirth;
But like of each thing that in season grows.
So you, to study now it is too late,
Climb o'er the house to unlock the little gate.

speeches.speech: Well, sit you out: go home, Biron: adieu.
lines: Well, sit you out: go home, Biron: adieu.

speeches.speech: No, my good lord; I have sworn to stay with you: And though I have for barbarism spoke more Than for that angel knowledge you can say, Yet confident I'll keep what I have swore And bide the penance of each three years' day. Give me the paper; let me read the same; And to the strict'st decrees I'll write my name.
lines: No, my good lord; I have sworn to stay with you:
And though I have for barbarism spoke more
Than for that angel knowledge you can say,
Yet confident I'll keep what I have swore
And bide the penance of each three years' day.
Give me the paper; let me read the same;
And to the strict'st decrees I'll write my name.

speeches.speech: How well this yielding rescues thee from shame!
lines: How well this yielding rescues thee from shame!

speeches.speech: [Reads] 'Item, That no woman shall come within a mile of my court:' Hath this been proclaimed?
lines: [Reads] 'Item, That no woman shall come within a
mile of my court:' Hath this been proclaimed?

speeches.speech: Four days ago.
lines: Four days ago.

speeches.speech: Let's see the penalty. [Reads] 'On pain of losing her tongue.' Who devised this penalty?
lines: Let's see the penalty.
[Reads]
'On pain of losing her tongue.' Who devised this penalty?

speeches.speech: Marry, that did I.
lines: Marry, that did I.

speeches.speech: Sweet lord, and why?
lines: Sweet lord, and why?

speeches.speech: To fright them hence with that dread penalty.
lines: To fright them hence with that dread penalty.

speeches.speech: A dangerous law against gentility! [Reads] 'Item, If any man be seen to talk with a woman within the term of three years, he shall endure such public shame as the rest of the court can possibly devise.' This article, my liege, yourself must break; For well you know here comes in embassy The French king's daughter with yourself to speak-- A maid of grace and complete majesty-- About surrender up of Aquitaine To her decrepit, sick and bedrid father: Therefore this article is made in vain, Or vainly comes the admired princess hither.
lines: A dangerous law against gentility!
[Reads]
'Item, If any man be seen to talk with a woman
within the term of three years, he shall endure such
public shame as the rest of the court can possibly devise.'
This article, my liege, yourself must break;
For well you know here comes in embassy
The French king's daughter with yourself to speak--
A maid of grace and complete majesty--
About surrender up of Aquitaine
To her decrepit, sick and bedrid father:
Therefore this article is made in vain,
Or vainly comes the admired princess hither.

speeches.speech: What say you, lords? Why, this was quite forgot.
lines: What say you, lords? Why, this was quite forgot.

speeches.speech: So study evermore is overshot: While it doth study to have what it would It doth forget to do the thing it should, And when it hath the thing it hunteth most, 'Tis won as towns with fire, so won, so lost.
lines: So study evermore is overshot:
While it doth study to have what it would
It doth forget to do the thing it should,
And when it hath the thing it hunteth most,
'Tis won as towns with fire, so won, so lost.

speeches.speech: We must of force dispense with this decree; She must lie here on mere necessity.
lines: We must of force dispense with this decree;
She must lie here on mere necessity.

speeches.speech: Necessity will make us all forsworn Three thousand times within this three years' space; For every man with his affects is born, Not by might master'd but by special grace: If I break faith, this word shall speak for me; I am forsworn on 'mere necessity.' So to the laws at large I write my name: [Subscribes] And he that breaks them in the least degree Stands in attainder of eternal shame: Suggestions are to other as to me; But I believe, although I seem so loath, I am the last that will last keep his oath. But is there no quick recreation granted?
lines: Necessity will make us all forsworn
Three thousand times within this three years' space;
For every man with his affects is born,
Not by might master'd but by special grace:
If I break faith, this word shall speak for me;
I am forsworn on 'mere necessity.'
So to the laws at large I write my name:
[Subscribes]
And he that breaks them in the least degree
Stands in attainder of eternal shame:
Suggestions are to other as to me;
But I believe, although I seem so loath,
I am the last that will last keep his oath.
But is there no quick recreation granted?

speeches.speech: Ay, that there is. Our court, you know, is haunted With a refined traveller of Spain; A man in all the world's new fashion planted, That hath a mint of phrases in his brain; One whom the music of his own vain tongue Doth ravish like enchanting harmony; A man of complements, whom right and wrong Have chose as umpire of their mutiny: This child of fancy, that Armado hight, For interim to our studies shall relate In high-born words the worth of many a knight From tawny Spain lost in the world's debate. How you delight, my lords, I know not, I; But, I protest, I love to hear him lie And I will use him for my minstrelsy.
lines: Ay, that there is. Our court, you know, is haunted
With a refined traveller of Spain;
A man in all the world's new fashion planted,
That hath a mint of phrases in his brain;
One whom the music of his own vain tongue
Doth ravish like enchanting harmony;
A man of complements, whom right and wrong
Have chose as umpire of their mutiny:
This child of fancy, that Armado hight,
For interim to our studies shall relate
In high-born words the worth of many a knight
From tawny Spain lost in the world's debate.
How you delight, my lords, I know not, I;
But, I protest, I love to hear him lie
And I will use him for my minstrelsy.

speeches.speech: Armado is a most illustrious wight, A man of fire-new words, fashion's own knight.
lines: Armado is a most illustrious wight,
A man of fire-new words, fashion's own knight.

speeches.speech: Costard the swain and he shall be our sport; And so to study, three years is but short.
lines: Costard the swain and he shall be our sport;
And so to study, three years is but short.

speeches.speech: Which is the duke's own person?
lines: Which is the duke's own person?

speeches.speech: This, fellow: what wouldst?
lines: This, fellow: what wouldst?

speeches.speech: I myself reprehend his own person, for I am his grace's tharborough: but I would see his own person in flesh and blood.
lines: I myself reprehend his own person, for I am his
grace's tharborough: but I would see his own person
in flesh and blood.

speeches.speech: This is he.
lines: This is he.

speeches.speech: Signior Arme--Arme--commends you. There's villany abroad: this letter will tell you more.
lines: Signior Arme--Arme--commends you. There's villany
abroad: this letter will tell you more.

speeches.speech: Sir, the contempts thereof are as touching me.
lines: Sir, the contempts thereof are as touching me.

speeches.speech: A letter from the magnificent Armado.
lines: A letter from the magnificent Armado.

speeches.speech: How low soever the matter, I hope in God for high words.
lines: How low soever the matter, I hope in God for high words.

speeches.speech: A high hope for a low heaven: God grant us patience!
lines: A high hope for a low heaven: God grant us patience!

speeches.speech: To hear? or forbear laughing?
lines: To hear? or forbear laughing?

speeches.speech: To hear meekly, sir, and to laugh moderately; or to forbear both.
lines: To hear meekly, sir, and to laugh moderately; or to
forbear both.

speeches.speech: Well, sir, be it as the style shall give us cause to climb in the merriness.
lines: Well, sir, be it as the style shall give us cause to
climb in the merriness.

speeches.speech: The matter is to me, sir, as concerning Jaquenetta. The manner of it is, I was taken with the manner.
lines: The matter is to me, sir, as concerning Jaquenetta.
The manner of it is, I was taken with the manner.

speeches.speech: In what manner?
lines: In what manner?

speeches.speech: In manner and form following, sir; all those three: I was seen with her in the manor-house, sitting with her upon the form, and taken following her into the park; which, put together, is in manner and form following. Now, sir, for the manner,--it is the manner of a man to speak to a woman: for the form,-- in some form.
lines: In manner and form following, sir; all those three:
I was seen with her in the manor-house, sitting with
her upon the form, and taken following her into the
park; which, put together, is in manner and form
following. Now, sir, for the manner,--it is the
manner of a man to speak to a woman: for the form,--
in some form.

speeches.speech: For the following, sir?
lines: For the following, sir?

speeches.speech: As it shall follow in my correction: and God defend the right!
lines: As it shall follow in my correction: and God defend
the right!

speeches.speech: Will you hear this letter with attention?
lines: Will you hear this letter with attention?

speeches.speech: As we would hear an oracle.
lines: As we would hear an oracle.

speeches.speech: Such is the simplicity of man to hearken after the flesh.
lines: Such is the simplicity of man to hearken after the flesh.

speeches.speech: [Reads] 'Great deputy, the welkin's vicegerent and sole dominator of Navarre, my soul's earth's god, and body's fostering patron.'
lines: [Reads] 'Great deputy, the welkin's vicegerent and
sole dominator of Navarre, my soul's earth's god,
and body's fostering patron.'

speeches.speech: Not a word of Costard yet.
lines: Not a word of Costard yet.

speeches.speech: [Reads] 'So it is,'--
lines: [Reads] 'So it is,'--

speeches.speech: It may be so: but if he say it is so, he is, in telling true, but so.
lines: It may be so: but if he say it is so, he is, in
telling true, but so.

speeches.speech: Peace!
lines: Peace!

speeches.speech: Be to me and every man that dares not fight!
lines: Be to me and every man that dares not fight!

speeches.speech: No words!
lines: No words!

speeches.speech: Of other men's secrets, I beseech you.
lines: Of other men's secrets, I beseech you.

speeches.speech: [Reads] 'So it is, besieged with sable-coloured melancholy, I did commend the black-oppressing humour to the most wholesome physic of thy health-giving air; and, as I am a gentleman, betook myself to walk. The time when. About the sixth hour; when beasts most graze, birds best peck, and men sit down to that nourishment which is called supper: so much for the time when. Now for the ground which; which, I mean, I walked upon: it is y-cleped thy park. Then for the place where; where, I mean, I did encounter that obscene and preposterous event, that draweth from my snow-white pen the ebon-coloured ink, which here thou viewest, beholdest, surveyest, or seest; but to the place where; it standeth north-north-east and by east from the west corner of thy curious- knotted garden: there did I see that low-spirited swain, that base minnow of thy mirth,'--
lines: [Reads] 'So it is, besieged with sable-coloured
melancholy, I did commend the black-oppressing humour
to the most wholesome physic of thy health-giving
air; and, as I am a gentleman, betook myself to
walk. The time when. About the sixth hour; when
beasts most graze, birds best peck, and men sit down
to that nourishment which is called supper: so much
for the time when. Now for the ground which; which,
I mean, I walked upon: it is y-cleped thy park. Then
for the place where; where, I mean, I did encounter
that obscene and preposterous event, that draweth
from my snow-white pen the ebon-coloured ink, which
here thou viewest, beholdest, surveyest, or seest;
but to the place where; it standeth north-north-east
and by east from the west corner of thy curious-
knotted garden: there did I see that low-spirited
swain, that base minnow of thy mirth,'--

speeches.speech: Me?
lines: Me?

speeches.speech: [Reads] 'that unlettered small-knowing soul,'--
lines: [Reads] 'that unlettered small-knowing soul,'--

speeches.speech: Me?
lines: Me?

speeches.speech: [Reads] 'that shallow vassal,'--
lines: [Reads] 'that shallow vassal,'--

speeches.speech: Still me?
lines: Still me?

speeches.speech: [Reads] 'which, as I remember, hight Costard,'--
lines: [Reads] 'which, as I remember, hight Costard,'--

speeches.speech: O, me!
lines: O, me!

speeches.speech: [Reads] 'sorted and consorted, contrary to thy established proclaimed edict and continent canon, which with,--O, with--but with this I passion to say wherewith,--
lines: [Reads] 'sorted and consorted, contrary to thy
established proclaimed edict and continent canon,
which with,--O, with--but with this I passion to say
wherewith,--

speeches.speech: With a wench.
lines: With a wench.

speeches.speech: [Reads] 'with a child of our grandmother Eve, a female; or, for thy more sweet understanding, a woman. Him I, as my ever-esteemed duty pricks me on, have sent to thee, to receive the meed of punishment, by thy sweet grace's officer, Anthony Dull; a man of good repute, carriage, bearing, and estimation.'
lines: [Reads] 'with a child of our grandmother Eve, a
female; or, for thy more sweet understanding, a
woman. Him I, as my ever-esteemed duty pricks me on,
have sent to thee, to receive the meed of
punishment, by thy sweet grace's officer, Anthony
Dull; a man of good repute, carriage, bearing, and
estimation.'

speeches.speech: 'Me, an't shall please you; I am Anthony Dull.
lines: 'Me, an't shall please you; I am Anthony Dull.

speeches.speech: [Reads] 'For Jaquenetta,--so is the weaker vessel called which I apprehended with the aforesaid swain,--I keep her as a vessel of the law's fury; and shall, at the least of thy sweet notice, bring her to trial. Thine, in all compliments of devoted and heart-burning heat of duty. DON ADRIANO DE ARMADO.'
lines: [Reads] 'For Jaquenetta,--so is the weaker vessel
called which I apprehended with the aforesaid
swain,--I keep her as a vessel of the law's fury;
and shall, at the least of thy sweet notice, bring
her to trial. Thine, in all compliments of devoted
and heart-burning heat of duty.
DON ADRIANO DE ARMADO.'

speeches.speech: This is not so well as I looked for, but the best that ever I heard.
lines: This is not so well as I looked for, but the best
that ever I heard.

speeches.speech: Ay, the best for the worst. But, sirrah, what say you to this?
lines: Ay, the best for the worst. But, sirrah, what say
you to this?

speeches.speech: Sir, I confess the wench.
lines: Sir, I confess the wench.

speeches.speech: Did you hear the proclamation?
lines: Did you hear the proclamation?

speeches.speech: I do confess much of the hearing it but little of the marking of it.
lines: I do confess much of the hearing it but little of
the marking of it.

speeches.speech: It was proclaimed a year's imprisonment, to be taken with a wench.
lines: It was proclaimed a year's imprisonment, to be taken
with a wench.

speeches.speech: I was taken with none, sir: I was taken with a damsel.
lines: I was taken with none, sir: I was taken with a damsel.

speeches.speech: Well, it was proclaimed 'damsel.'
lines: Well, it was proclaimed 'damsel.'

speeches.speech: This was no damsel, neither, sir; she was a virgin.
lines: This was no damsel, neither, sir; she was a virgin.

speeches.speech: It is so varied, too; for it was proclaimed 'virgin.'
lines: It is so varied, too; for it was proclaimed 'virgin.'

speeches.speech: If it were, I deny her virginity: I was taken with a maid.
lines: If it were, I deny her virginity: I was taken with a maid.

speeches.speech: This maid will not serve your turn, sir.
lines: This maid will not serve your turn, sir.

speeches.speech: This maid will serve my turn, sir.
lines: This maid will serve my turn, sir.

speeches.speech: Sir, I will pronounce your sentence: you shall fast a week with bran and water.
lines: Sir, I will pronounce your sentence: you shall fast
a week with bran and water.

speeches.speech: I had rather pray a month with mutton and porridge.
lines: I had rather pray a month with mutton and porridge.

speeches.speech: And Don Armado shall be your keeper. My Lord Biron, see him deliver'd o'er: And go we, lords, to put in practise that Which each to other hath so strongly sworn.
lines: And Don Armado shall be your keeper.
My Lord Biron, see him deliver'd o'er:
And go we, lords, to put in practise that
Which each to other hath so strongly sworn.

speeches.speech: I'll lay my head to any good man's hat, These oaths and laws will prove an idle scorn. Sirrah, come on.
lines: I'll lay my head to any good man's hat,
These oaths and laws will prove an idle scorn.
Sirrah, come on.

speeches.speech: I suffer for the truth, sir; for true it is, I was taken with Jaquenetta, and Jaquenetta is a true girl; and therefore welcome the sour cup of prosperity! Affliction may one day smile again; and till then, sit thee down, sorrow!
lines: I suffer for the truth, sir; for true it is, I was
taken with Jaquenetta, and Jaquenetta is a true
girl; and therefore welcome the sour cup of
prosperity! Affliction may one day smile again; and
till then, sit thee down, sorrow!

speeches.speech: Boy, what sign is it when a man of great spirit grows melancholy?
lines: Boy, what sign is it when a man of great spirit
grows melancholy?

speeches.speech: A great sign, sir, that he will look sad.
lines: A great sign, sir, that he will look sad.

speeches.speech: Why, sadness is one and the self-same thing, dear imp.
lines: Why, sadness is one and the self-same thing, dear imp.

speeches.speech: No, no; O Lord, sir, no.
lines: No, no; O Lord, sir, no.

speeches.speech: How canst thou part sadness and melancholy, my tender juvenal?
lines: How canst thou part sadness and melancholy, my
tender juvenal?

speeches.speech: By a familiar demonstration of the working, my tough senior.
lines: By a familiar demonstration of the working, my tough senior.

speeches.speech: Why tough senior? why tough senior?
lines: Why tough senior? why tough senior?

speeches.speech: Why tender juvenal? why tender juvenal?
lines: Why tender juvenal? why tender juvenal?

speeches.speech: And I, tough senior, as an appertinent title to your old time, which we may name tough.

speeches.speech: Pretty and apt.
lines: Pretty and apt.

speeches.speech: How mean you, sir? I pretty, and my saying apt? or I apt, and my saying pretty?
lines: How mean you, sir? I pretty, and my saying apt? or

speeches.speech: Thou pretty, because little.
lines: Thou pretty, because little.

speeches.speech: Little pretty, because little. Wherefore apt?
lines: Little pretty, because little. Wherefore apt?

speeches.speech: And therefore apt, because quick.
lines: And therefore apt, because quick.

speeches.speech: Speak you this in my praise, master?
lines: Speak you this in my praise, master?

speeches.speech: In thy condign praise.
lines: In thy condign praise.

speeches.speech: I will praise an eel with the same praise.
lines: I will praise an eel with the same praise.

speeches.speech: What, that an eel is ingenious?
lines: What, that an eel is ingenious?

speeches.speech: That an eel is quick.
lines: That an eel is quick.

speeches.speech: I do say thou art quick in answers: thou heatest my blood.
lines: I do say thou art quick in answers: thou heatest my blood.

speeches.speech: I am answered, sir.
lines: I am answered, sir.

speeches.speech: I love not to be crossed.
lines: I love not to be crossed.

speeches.speech: [Aside] He speaks the mere contrary; crosses love not him.
lines: [Aside] He speaks the mere contrary; crosses love not him.

speeches.speech: I have promised to study three years with the duke.
lines: I have promised to study three years with the duke.

speeches.speech: You may do it in an hour, sir.
lines: You may do it in an hour, sir.

speeches.speech: Impossible.
lines: Impossible.

speeches.speech: How many is one thrice told?
lines: How many is one thrice told?

speeches.speech: I am ill at reckoning; it fitteth the spirit of a tapster.
lines: I am ill at reckoning; it fitteth the spirit of a tapster.

speeches.speech: You are a gentleman and a gamester, sir.
lines: You are a gentleman and a gamester, sir.

speeches.speech: I confess both: they are both the varnish of a complete man.
lines: I confess both: they are both the varnish of a

speeches.speech: Then, I am sure, you know how much the gross sum of deuce-ace amounts to.
lines: Then, I am sure, you know how much the gross sum of

speeches.speech: It doth amount to one more than two.

speeches.speech: Which the base vulgar do call three.

speeches.speech: True.
lines: True.

speeches.speech: Why, sir, is this such a piece of study? Now here is three studied, ere ye'll thrice wink: and how easy it is to put 'years' to the word 'three,' and study three years in two words, the dancing horse will tell you.

speeches.speech: To prove you a cipher.

speeches.speech: I will hereupon confess I am in love: and as it is base for a soldier to love, so am I in love with a base wench. If drawing my sword against the humour of affection would deliver me from the reprobate thought of it, I would take Desire prisoner, and ransom him to any French courtier for a new-devised courtesy. I think scorn to sigh: methinks I should outswear Cupid. Comfort, me, boy: what great men have been in love?

speeches.speech: Hercules, master.

speeches.speech: Most sweet Hercules! More authority, dear boy, name more; and, sweet my child, let them be men of good repute and carriage.

speeches.speech: Samson, master: he was a man of good carriage, great carriage, for he carried the town-gates on his back like a porter: and he was in love.

speeches.speech: O well-knit Samson! strong-jointed Samson! I do excel thee in my rapier as much as thou didst me in carrying gates. I am in love too. Who was Samson's love, my dear Moth?

lines: Of what complexion?

speeches.speech: Of all the four, or the three, or the two, or one of the four.
lines: Of all the four, or the three, or the two, or one of the four.

speeches.speech: Tell me precisely of what complexion.
lines: Tell me precisely of what complexion.

speeches.speech: Of the sea-water green, sir.
lines: Of the sea-water green, sir.

speeches.speech: Is that one of the four complexions?
lines: Is that one of the four complexions?

speeches.speech: As I have read, sir; and the best of them too.
lines: As I have read, sir; and the best of them too.

speeches.speech: Green indeed is the colour of lovers; but to have a love of that colour, methinks Samson had small reason for it. He surely affected her for her wit.

lines: It was so, sir; for she had a green wit.

speeches.speech: My love is most immaculate white and red.
lines: My love is most immaculate white and red.

speeches.speech: Most maculate thoughts, master, are masked under such colours.
lines: Most maculate thoughts, master, are masked under

speeches.speech: Define, define, well-educated infant.
lines: Define, define, well-educated infant.

speeches.speech: My father's wit and my mother's tongue, assist me!
lines: My father's wit and my mother's tongue, assist me!

speeches.speech: Sweet invocation of a child; most pretty and pathetical!
lines: Sweet invocation of a child; most pretty and

speeches.speech: If she be made of white and red, Her faults will ne'er be known, For blushing cheeks by faults are bred And fears by pale white shown: Then if she fear, or be to blame, By this you shall not know, For still her cheeks possess the same Which native she doth owe. A dangerous rhyme, master, against the reason of white and red.

speeches.speech: Is there not a ballad, boy, of the King and the Beggar?
lines: Is there not a ballad, boy, of the King and the Beggar?

speeches.speech: The world was very guilty of such a ballad some three ages since: but I think now 'tis not to be found; or, if it were, it would neither serve for the writing nor the tune.

speeches.speech: I will have that subject newly writ o'er, that I may example my digression by some mighty precedent. Boy, I do love that country girl that I took in the park with the rational hind Costard: she deserves well.

speeches.speech: [Aside] To be whipped; and yet a better love than my master.
lines: [Aside] To be whipped; and yet a better love than

speeches.speech: Sing, boy; my spirit grows heavy in love.
lines: Sing, boy; my spirit grows heavy in love.

speeches.speech: And that's great marvel, loving a light wench.
lines: And that's great marvel, loving a light wench.

speeches.speech: I say, sing.
lines: I say, sing.

speeches.speech: Forbear till this company be past.
lines: Forbear till this company be past.

speeches.speech: Sir, the duke's pleasure is, that you keep Costard safe: and you must suffer him to take no delight nor no penance; but a' must fast three days a week. For this damsel, I must keep her at the park: she is allowed for the day-woman. Fare you well.

speeches.speech: I do betray myself with blushing. Maid!
lines: I do betray myself with blushing. Maid!

speeches.speech: Man?
lines: Man?

lines: I will visit thee at the lodge.

lines: I will tell thee wonders.

lines: With that face?

lines: Come, Jaquenetta, away!

lines: Villain, thou shalt fast for thy offences ere thou

speeches.speech: Well, sir, I hope, when I do it, I shall do it on a full stomach.
lines: Well, sir, I hope, when I do it, I shall do it on a

speeches.speech: Thou shalt be heavily punished.
lines: Thou shalt be heavily punished.

speeches.speech: I am more bound to you than your fellows, for they are but lightly rewarded.

speeches.speech: Take away this villain; shut him up.
lines: Take away this villain; shut him up.

lines: Come, you transgressing slave; away!

speeches.speech: Let me not be pent up, sir: I will fast, being loose.
lines: Let me not be pent up, sir: I will fast, being loose.

speeches.speech: No, sir; that were fast and loose: thou shalt to prison.
lines: No, sir; that were fast and loose: thou shalt to prison.

speeches.speech: Well, if ever I do see the merry days of desolation that I have seen, some shall see.

speeches.speech: What shall some see?
lines: What shall some see?

speeches.speech: Nay, nothing, Master Moth, but what they look upon. It is not for prisoners to be too silent in their words; and therefore I will say nothing: I thank God I have as little patience as another man; and therefore I can be quiet.

speeches.speech: I do affect the very ground, which is base, where her shoe, which is baser, guided by her foot, which is basest, doth tread. I shall be forsworn, which is a great argument of falsehood, if I love. And how can that be true love which is falsely attempted? Love is a familiar; Love is a devil: there is no evil angel but Love. Yet was Samson so tempted, and he had an excellent strength; yet was Solomon so seduced, and he had a very good wit. Cupid's butt-shaft is too hard for Hercules' club; and therefore too much odds for a Spaniard's rapier. The first and second cause will not serve my turn; the passado he respects not, the duello he regards not: his disgrace is to be called boy; but his glory is to subdue men. Adieu, valour! rust rapier! be still, drum! for your manager is in love; yea, he loveth. Assist me, some extemporal god of rhyme, for I am sure I shall turn sonnet. Devise, wit; write, pen; for I am for whole volumes in folio.

speeches.speech: Now, madam, summon up your dearest spirits: Consider who the king your father sends, To whom he sends, and what's his embassy: Yourself, held precious in the world's esteem, To parley with the sole inheritor Of all perfections that a man may owe, Matchless Navarre; the plea of no less weight Than Aquitaine, a dowry for a queen. Be now as prodigal of all dear grace As Nature was in making graces dear When she did starve the general world beside And prodigally gave them all to you.
lines: Now, madam, summon up your dearest spirits:
Consider who the king your father sends,
To whom he sends, and what's his embassy:
Yourself, held precious in the world's esteem,
To parley with the sole inheritor
Of all perfections that a man may owe,
Matchless Navarre; the plea of no less weight
Than Aquitaine, a dowry for a queen.
Be now as prodigal of all dear grace
As Nature was in making graces dear
When she did starve the general world beside
And prodigally gave them all to you.

speeches.speech: Good Lord Boyet, my beauty, though but mean, Needs not the painted flourish of your praise: Beauty is bought by judgement of the eye, Not utter'd by base sale of chapmen's tongues: I am less proud to hear you tell my worth Than you much willing to be counted wise In spending your wit in the praise of mine. But now to task the tasker: good Boyet, You are not ignorant, all-telling fame Doth noise abroad, Navarre hath made a vow, Till painful study shall outwear three years, No woman may approach his silent court: Therefore to's seemeth it a needful course, Before we enter his forbidden gates, To know his pleasure; and in that behalf, Bold of your worthiness, we single you As our best-moving fair solicitor. Tell him, the daughter of the King of France, On serious business, craving quick dispatch, Importunes personal conference with his grace: Haste, signify so much; while we attend, Like humble-visaged suitors, his high will.
lines: Good Lord Boyet, my beauty, though but mean,
Needs not the painted flourish of your praise:
Beauty is bought by judgement of the eye,
Not utter'd by base sale of chapmen's tongues:
I am less proud to hear you tell my worth
Than you much willing to be counted wise
In spending your wit in the praise of mine.
But now to task the tasker: good Boyet,
You are not ignorant, all-telling fame
Doth noise abroad, Navarre hath made a vow,
Till painful study shall outwear three years,
No woman may approach his silent court:
Therefore to's seemeth it a needful course,
Before we enter his forbidden gates,
To know his pleasure; and in that behalf,
Bold of your worthiness, we single you
As our best-moving fair solicitor.
Tell him, the daughter of the King of France,
On serious business, craving quick dispatch,
Importunes personal conference with his grace:
Haste, signify so much; while we attend,
Like humble-visaged suitors, his high will.

speeches.speech: Proud of employment, willingly I go.
lines: Proud of employment, willingly I go.

speeches.speech: All pride is willing pride, and yours is so. [Exit BOYET] Who are the votaries, my loving lords, That are vow-fellows with this virtuous duke?
lines: All pride is willing pride, and yours is so.
[Exit BOYET]
Who are the votaries, my loving lords,
That are vow-fellows with this virtuous duke?

speeches.speech: Lord Longaville is one.
lines: Lord Longaville is one.

speeches.speech: Know you the man?
lines: Know you the man?

speeches.speech: I know him, madam: at a marriage-feast, Between Lord Perigort and the beauteous heir Of Jaques Falconbridge, solemnized In Normandy, saw I this Longaville: A man of sovereign parts he is esteem'd; Well fitted in arts, glorious in arms: Nothing becomes him ill that he would well. The only soil of his fair virtue's gloss, If virtue's gloss will stain with any soil, Is a sharp wit matched with too blunt a will; Whose edge hath power to cut, whose will still wills It should none spare that come within his power.
lines: I know him, madam: at a marriage-feast,
Between Lord Perigort and the beauteous heir
Of Jaques Falconbridge, solemnized
In Normandy, saw I this Longaville:
A man of sovereign parts he is esteem'd;
Well fitted in arts, glorious in arms:
Nothing becomes him ill that he would well.
The only soil of his fair virtue's gloss,
If virtue's gloss will stain with any soil,
Is a sharp wit matched with too blunt a will;
Whose edge hath power to cut, whose will still wills
It should none spare that come within his power.

speeches.speech: Some merry mocking lord, belike; is't so?
lines: Some merry mocking lord, belike; is't so?

speeches.speech: They say so most that most his humours know.
lines: They say so most that most his humours know.

speeches.speech: Such short-lived wits do wither as they grow. Who are the rest?
lines: Such short-lived wits do wither as they grow.

speeches.speech: The young Dumain, a well-accomplished youth, Of all that virtue love for virtue loved: Most power to do most harm, least knowing ill; For he hath wit to make an ill shape good, And shape to win grace though he had no wit. I saw him at the Duke Alencon's once; And much too little of that good I saw Is my report to his great worthiness.

Of all that virtue love for virtue loved:

speeches.speech: Another of these students at that time Was there with him, if I have heard a truth. Biron they call him; but a merrier man, Within the limit of becoming mirth, I never spent an hour's talk withal: His eye begets occasion for his wit; For every object that the one doth catch The other turns to a mirth-moving jest, Which his fair tongue, conceit's expositor, Delivers in such apt and gracious words That aged ears play truant at his tales And younger hearings are quite ravished; So sweet and voluble is his discourse.
lines: Another of these students at that time
Was there with him, if I have heard a truth.
Biron they call him; but a merrier man,
Within the limit of becoming mirth,

speeches.speech: God bless my ladies! are they all in love, That every one her own hath garnished With such bedecking ornaments of praise?

speeches.speech: Here comes Boyet.
lines: Here comes Boyet.

speeches.speech: Now, what admittance, lord?
lines: Now, what admittance, lord?

speeches.speech: Navarre had notice of your fair approach; And he and his competitors in oath Were all address'd to meet you, gentle lady, Before I came. Marry, thus much I have learnt: He rather means to lodge you in the field, Like one that comes here to besiege his court, Than seek a dispensation for his oath, To let you enter his unpeopled house. Here comes Navarre. [Enter FERDINAND, LONGAVILLE, DUMAIN, BIRON, and] Attendants]

And he and his competitors in oath
Were all address'd to meet you, gentle lady,
Before I came. Marry, thus much I have learnt:
He rather means to lodge you in the field,

speeches.speech: Fair princess, welcome to the court of Navarre.
lines: Fair princess, welcome to the court of Navarre.

speeches.speech: 'Fair' I give you back again; and 'welcome' I have not yet: the roof of this court is too high to be yours; and welcome to the wide fields too base to be mine.

speeches.speech: You shall be welcome, madam, to my court.

speeches.speech: I will be welcome, then: conduct me thither.
lines: I will be welcome, then: conduct me thither.

speeches.speech: Hear me, dear lady; I have sworn an oath.
lines: Hear me, dear lady; I have sworn an oath.

speeches.speech: Our Lady help my lord! he'll be forsworn.
lines: Our Lady help my lord! he'll be forsworn.

speeches.speech: Not for the world, fair madam, by my will.
lines: Not for the world, fair madam, by my will.

speeches.speech: Why, will shall break it; will and nothing else.
lines: Why, will shall break it; will and nothing else.

speeches.speech: Your ladyship is ignorant what it is.

speeches.speech: Were my lord so, his ignorance were wise, Where now his knowledge must prove ignorance. I hear your grace hath sworn out house-keeping: Tis deadly sin to keep that oath, my lord, And sin to break it. But pardon me. I am too sudden-bold: To teach a teacher ill beseemeth me. Vouchsafe to read the purpose of my coming, And suddenly resolve me in my suit.

speeches.speech: You will the sooner, that I were away; For you'll prove perjured if you make me stay.
lines: You will the sooner, that I were away;

speeches.speech: Did not I dance with you in Brabant once?
lines: Did not I dance with you in Brabant once?

speeches.speech: Did not I dance with you in Brabant once?
lines: Did not I dance with you in Brabant once?

lines: I know you did.

speeches.speech: How needless was it then to ask the question!
lines: How needless was it then to ask the question!

speeches.speech: You must not be so quick.
lines: You must not be so quick.

speeches.speech: 'Tis 'long of you that spur me with such questions.
lines: 'Tis 'long of you that spur me with such questions.
