lines: Not till it leave the rider in the mire.

speeches.speech: What time o' day?
lines: What time o' day?

speeches.speech: The hour that fools should ask.
lines: The hour that fools should ask.

speeches.speech: Now fair befall your mask!
lines: Now fair befall your mask!

speeches.speech: Fair fall the face it covers!
lines: Fair fall the face it covers!

speeches.speech: And send you many lovers!
lines: And send you many lovers!

speeches.speech: Amen, so you be none.
lines: Amen, so you be none.

speeches.speech: Nay, then will I be gone.
lines: Nay, then will I be gone.

speeches.speech: Madam, your father here doth intimate The payment of a hundred thousand crowns; Being but the one half of an entire sum Disbursed by my father in his wars. But say that he or we, as neither have, Received that sum, yet there remains unpaid A hundred thousand more; in surety of the which, One part of Aquitaine is bound to us, Although not valued to the money's worth. If then the king your father will restore But that one half which is unsatisfied, We will give up our right in Aquitaine, And hold fair friendship with his majesty. But that, it seems, he little purposeth, For here he doth demand to have repaid A hundred thousand crowns; and not demands, On payment of a hundred thousand crowns, To have his title live in Aquitaine; Which we much rather had depart withal And have the money by our father lent Than Aquitaine so gelded as it is. Dear Princess, were not his requests so far From reason's yielding, your fair self should make A yielding 'gainst some reason in my breast And go well satisfied to France again.
lines: Madam, your father here doth intimate
The payment of a hundred thousand crowns;
Being but the one half of an entire sum
Disbursed by my father in his wars.
But say that he or we, as neither have,
Received that sum, yet there remains unpaid
A hundred thousand more; in surety of the which,
One part of Aquitaine is bound to us,
Although not valued to the money's worth.
If then the king your father will restore
But that one half which is unsatisfied,
We will give up our right in Aquitaine,
And hold fair friendship with his majesty.
But that, it seems, he little purposeth,
For here he doth demand to have repaid
A hundred thousand crowns; and not demands,
On payment of a hundred thousand crowns,
To have his title live in Aquitaine;
Which we much rather had depart withal
And have the money by our father lent
Than Aquitaine so gelded as it is.
Dear Princess, were not his requests so far
From reason's yielding, your fair self should make
A yielding 'gainst some reason in my breast
And go well satisfied to France again.

speeches.speech: You do the king my father too much wrong And wrong the reputation of your name, In so unseeming to confess receipt Of that which hath so faithfully been paid.
lines: You do the king my father too much wrong
And wrong the reputation of your name,
In so unseeming to confess receipt
Of that which hath so faithfully been paid.

speeches.speech: I do protest I never heard of it; And if you prove it, I'll repay it back Or yield up Aquitaine.
lines: I do protest I never heard of it;
And if you prove it, I'll repay it back
Or yield up Aquitaine.

speeches.speech: We arrest your word. Boyet, you can produce acquittances For such a sum from special officers Of Charles his father.
lines: We arrest your word.
Boyet, you can produce acquittances
For such a sum from special officers
Of Charles his father.

speeches.speech: Satisfy me so.
lines: Satisfy me so.

speeches.speech: So please your grace, the packet is not come Where that and other specialties are bound: To-morrow you shall have a sight of them.
lines: So please your grace, the packet is not come
Where that and other specialties are bound:
To-morrow you shall have a sight of them.

speeches.speech: It shall suffice me: at which interview All liberal reason I will yield unto. Meantime receive such welcome at my hand As honour without breach of honour may Make tender of to thy true worthiness: You may not come, fair princess, in my gates; But here without you shall be so received As you shall deem yourself lodged in my heart, Though so denied fair harbour in my house. Your own good thoughts excuse me, and farewell: To-morrow shall we visit you again.
lines: It shall suffice me: at which interview
All liberal reason I will yield unto.
Meantime receive such welcome at my hand
As honour without breach of honour may
Make tender of to thy true worthiness:
You may not come, fair princess, in my gates;
But here without you shall be so received
As you shall deem yourself lodged in my heart,
Though so denied fair harbour in my house.
Your own good thoughts excuse me, and farewell:
To-morrow shall we visit you again.

speeches.speech: Sweet health and fair desires consort your grace!
lines: Sweet health and fair desires consort your grace!

speeches.speech: Thy own wish wish I thee in every place!
lines: Thy own wish wish I thee in every place!

speeches.speech: Lady, I will commend you to mine own heart.
lines: Lady, I will commend you to mine own heart.

speeches.speech: Pray you, do my commendations; I would be glad to see it.
lines: Pray you, do my commendations; I would be glad to see it.

speeches.speech: I would you heard it groan.
lines: I would you heard it groan.

speeches.speech: Is the fool sick?
lines: Is the fool sick?

speeches.speech: Sick at the heart.
lines: Sick at the heart.

speeches.speech: Alack, let it blood.
lines: Alack, let it blood.

speeches.speech: Would that do it good?
lines: Would that do it good?

speeches.speech: My physic says 'ay.'
lines: My physic says 'ay.'

speeches.speech: Will you prick't with your eye?
lines: Will you prick't with your eye?

speeches.speech: No point, with my knife.
lines: No point, with my knife.

speeches.speech: Now, God save thy life!
lines: Now, God save thy life!

speeches.speech: And yours from long living!
lines: And yours from long living!

speeches.speech: I cannot stay thanksgiving.
lines: I cannot stay thanksgiving.

speeches.speech: Sir, I pray you, a word: what lady is that same?
lines: Sir, I pray you, a word: what lady is that same?

speeches.speech: The heir of Alencon, Katharine her name.
lines: The heir of Alencon, Katharine her name.

speeches.speech: A gallant lady. Monsieur, fare you well.
lines: A gallant lady. Monsieur, fare you well.

speeches.speech: I beseech you a word: what is she in the white?
lines: I beseech you a word: what is she in the white?

speeches.speech: A woman sometimes, an you saw her in the light.
lines: A woman sometimes, an you saw her in the light.

speeches.speech: Perchance light in the light. I desire her name.
lines: Perchance light in the light. I desire her name.

speeches.speech: She hath but one for herself; to desire that were a shame.
lines: She hath but one for herself; to desire that were a shame.

speeches.speech: Pray you, sir, whose daughter?
lines: Pray you, sir, whose daughter?

speeches.speech: Her mother's, I have heard.
lines: Her mother's, I have heard.

speeches.speech: God's blessing on your beard!
lines: God's blessing on your beard!

speeches.speech: Good sir, be not offended. She is an heir of Falconbridge.
lines: Good sir, be not offended.
She is an heir of Falconbridge.

speeches.speech: Nay, my choler is ended. She is a most sweet lady.
lines: Nay, my choler is ended.
She is a most sweet lady.

speeches.speech: Not unlike, sir, that may be.
lines: Not unlike, sir, that may be.

speeches.speech: What's her name in the cap?
lines: What's her name in the cap?

speeches.speech: Rosaline, by good hap.
lines: Rosaline, by good hap.

speeches.speech: Is she wedded or no?
lines: Is she wedded or no?

speeches.speech: To her will, sir, or so.
lines: To her will, sir, or so.

speeches.speech: You are welcome, sir: adieu.
lines: You are welcome, sir: adieu.

speeches.speech: Farewell to me, sir, and welcome to you.
lines: Farewell to me, sir, and welcome to you.

speeches.speech: That last is Biron, the merry madcap lord: Not a word with him but a jest.
lines: That last is Biron, the merry madcap lord:
Not a word with him but a jest.

speeches.speech: And every jest but a word.
lines: And every jest but a word.

speeches.speech: It was well done of you to take him at his word.
lines: It was well done of you to take him at his word.

speeches.speech: I was as willing to grapple as he was to board.
lines: I was as willing to grapple as he was to board.

speeches.speech: Two hot sheeps, marry.
lines: Two hot sheeps, marry.

speeches.speech: And wherefore not ships? No sheep, sweet lamb, unless we feed on your lips.
lines: And wherefore not ships?
No sheep, sweet lamb, unless we feed on your lips.

speeches.speech: You sheep, and I pasture: shall that finish the jest?
lines: You sheep, and I pasture: shall that finish the jest?

speeches.speech: So you grant pasture for me.
lines: So you grant pasture for me.

speeches.speech: Not so, gentle beast: My lips are no common, though several they be.
lines: Not so, gentle beast:
My lips are no common, though several they be.

speeches.speech: Belonging to whom?
lines: Belonging to whom?

speeches.speech: To my fortunes and me.
lines: To my fortunes and me.

speeches.speech: Good wits will be jangling; but, gentles, agree: This civil war of wits were much better used On Navarre and his book-men; for here 'tis abused.
lines: Good wits will be jangling; but, gentles, agree:
This civil war of wits were much better used
On Navarre and his book-men; for here 'tis abused.

speeches.speech: If my observation, which very seldom lies, By the heart's still rhetoric disclosed with eyes, Deceive me not now, Navarre is infected.
lines: If my observation, which very seldom lies,
By the heart's still rhetoric disclosed with eyes,
Deceive me not now, Navarre is infected.

speeches.speech: With what?
lines: With what?

speeches.speech: With that which we lovers entitle affected.
lines: With that which we lovers entitle affected.

speeches.speech: Your reason?
lines: Your reason?

speeches.speech: Why, all his behaviors did make their retire To the court of his eye, peeping thorough desire: His heart, like an agate, with your print impress'd, Proud with his form, in his eye pride express'd: His tongue, all impatient to speak and not see, Did stumble with haste in his eyesight to be; All senses to that sense did make their repair, To feel only looking on fairest of fair: Methought all his senses were lock'd in his eye, As jewels in crystal for some prince to buy; Who, tendering their own worth from where they were glass'd, Did point you to buy them, along as you pass'd: His face's own margent did quote such amazes That all eyes saw his eyes enchanted with gazes. I'll give you Aquitaine and all that is his, An you give him for my sake but one loving kiss.
lines: Why, all his behaviors did make their retire
To the court of his eye, peeping thorough desire:
His heart, like an agate, with your print impress'd,
Proud with his form, in his eye pride express'd:
His tongue, all impatient to speak and not see,
Did stumble with haste in his eyesight to be;
All senses to that sense did make their repair,
To feel only looking on fairest of fair:
Methought all his senses were lock'd in his eye,
As jewels in crystal for some prince to buy;
Who, tendering their own worth from where they were glass'd,
Did point you to buy them, along as you pass'd:
His face's own margent did quote such amazes
That all eyes saw his eyes enchanted with gazes.
I'll give you Aquitaine and all that is his,
An you give him for my sake but one loving kiss.

speeches.speech: Come to our pavilion: Boyet is disposed.
lines: Come to our pavilion: Boyet is disposed.

speeches.speech: But to speak that in words which his eye hath disclosed. I only have made a mouth of his eye, By adding a tongue which I know will not lie.
lines: But to speak that in words which his eye hath
disclosed.
I only have made a mouth of his eye,
By adding a tongue which I know will not lie.

speeches.speech: Thou art an old love-monger and speakest skilfully.
lines: Thou art an old love-monger and speakest skilfully.

speeches.speech: He is Cupid's grandfather and learns news of him.
lines: He is Cupid's grandfather and learns news of him.

speeches.speech: Then was Venus like her mother, for her father is but grim.
lines: Then was Venus like her mother, for her father is but grim.

speeches.speech: Do you hear, my mad wenches?
lines: Do you hear, my mad wenches?

speeches.speech: No.
lines: No.

speeches.speech: What then, do you see?
lines: What then, do you see?

speeches.speech: Ay, our way to be gone.
lines: Ay, our way to be gone.

speeches.speech: You are too hard for me.
lines: You are too hard for me.

speeches.speech: Warble, child; make passionate my sense of hearing.
lines: Warble, child; make passionate my sense of hearing.

speeches.speech: Concolinel.
lines: Concolinel.

speeches.speech: Sweet air! Go, tenderness of years; take this key, give enlargement to the swain, bring him festinately hither: I must employ him in a letter to my love.
lines: Sweet air! Go, tenderness of years; take this key,
give enlargement to the swain, bring him festinately
hither: I must employ him in a letter to my love.

speeches.speech: Master, will you win your love with a French brawl?
lines: Master, will you win your love with a French brawl?

speeches.speech: How meanest thou? brawling in French?
lines: How meanest thou? brawling in French?

speeches.speech: No, my complete master: but to jig off a tune at the tongue's end, canary to it with your feet, humour it with turning up your eyelids, sigh a note and sing a note, sometime through the throat, as if you swallowed love with singing love, sometime through the nose, as if you snuffed up love by smelling love; with your hat penthouse-like o'er the shop of your eyes; with your arms crossed on your thin-belly doublet like a rabbit on a spit; or your hands in your pocket like a man after the old painting; and keep not too long in one tune, but a snip and away. These are complements, these are humours; these betray nice wenches, that would be betrayed without these; and make them men of note--do you note me?--that most are affected to these.
lines: No, my complete master: but to jig off a tune at
the tongue's end, canary to it with your feet, humour
it with turning up your eyelids, sigh a note and
sing a note, sometime through the throat, as if you
swallowed love with singing love, sometime through
the nose, as if you snuffed up love by smelling
love; with your hat penthouse-like o'er the shop of
your eyes; with your arms crossed on your thin-belly
doublet like a rabbit on a spit; or your hands in
your pocket like a man after the old painting; and
keep not too long in one tune, but a snip and away.
These are complements, these are humours; these
betray nice wenches, that would be betrayed without
these; and make them men of note--do you note
me?--that most are affected to these.

speeches.speech: How hast thou purchased this experience?
lines: How hast thou purchased this experience?

speeches.speech: By my penny of observation.
lines: By my penny of observation.

speeches.speech: But O,--but O,--
lines: But O,--but O,--

speeches.speech: 'The hobby-horse is forgot.'
lines: 'The hobby-horse is forgot.'

speeches.speech: Callest thou my love 'hobby-horse'?
lines: Callest thou my love 'hobby-horse'?

speeches.speech: No, master; the hobby-horse is but a colt, and your love perhaps a hackney. But have you forgot your love?
lines: No, master; the hobby-horse is but a colt, and your
love perhaps a hackney. But have you forgot your love?

speeches.speech: Almost I had.
lines: Almost I had.

speeches.speech: Negligent student! learn her by heart.
lines: Negligent student! learn her by heart.

speeches.speech: By heart and in heart, boy.
lines: By heart and in heart, boy.

speeches.speech: And out of heart, master: all those three I will prove.
lines: And out of heart, master: all those three I will prove.

speeches.speech: What wilt thou prove?
lines: What wilt thou prove?

speeches.speech: A man, if I live; and this, by, in, and without, upon the instant: by heart you love her, because your heart cannot come by her; in heart you love her, because your heart is in love with her; and out of heart you love her, being out of heart that you cannot enjoy her.
lines: A man, if I live; and this, by, in, and without, upon
the instant: by heart you love her, because your
heart cannot come by her; in heart you love her,
because your heart is in love with her; and out of
heart you love her, being out of heart that you
cannot enjoy her.

speeches.speech: I am all these three.
lines: I am all these three.

speeches.speech: And three times as much more, and yet nothing at all.
lines: And three times as much more, and yet nothing at
all.

speeches.speech: Fetch hither the swain: he must carry me a letter.
lines: Fetch hither the swain: he must carry me a letter.

speeches.speech: A message well sympathized; a horse to be ambassador for an ass.
lines: A message well sympathized; a horse to be ambassador
for an ass.

speeches.speech: Ha, ha! what sayest thou?
lines: Ha, ha! what sayest thou?

speeches.speech: Marry, sir, you must send the ass upon the horse, for he is very slow-gaited. But I go.
lines: Marry, sir, you must send the ass upon the horse,
for he is very slow-gaited. But I go.

speeches.speech: The way is but short: away!
lines: The way is but short: away!

speeches.speech: As swift as lead, sir.
lines: As swift as lead, sir.

speeches.speech: The meaning, pretty ingenious? Is not lead a metal heavy, dull, and slow?
lines: The meaning, pretty ingenious?
Is not lead a metal heavy, dull, and slow?

speeches.speech: Minime, honest master; or rather, master, no.
lines: Minime, honest master; or rather, master, no.

speeches.speech: I say lead is slow.
lines: I say lead is slow.

speeches.speech: You are too swift, sir, to say so: Is that lead slow which is fired from a gun?
lines: You are too swift, sir, to say so:
Is that lead slow which is fired from a gun?

speeches.speech: Sweet smoke of rhetoric! He reputes me a cannon; and the bullet, that's he: I shoot thee at the swain.
lines: Sweet smoke of rhetoric!
He reputes me a cannon; and the bullet, that's he:
I shoot thee at the swain.

speeches.speech: Thump then and I flee.
lines: Thump then and I flee.

speeches.speech: A most acute juvenal; voluble and free of grace! By thy favour, sweet welkin, I must sigh in thy face: Most rude melancholy, valour gives thee place. My herald is return'd.
lines: A most acute juvenal; voluble and free of grace!
By thy favour, sweet welkin, I must sigh in thy face:
Most rude melancholy, valour gives thee place.
My herald is return'd.

speeches.speech: A wonder, master! here's a costard broken in a shin.
lines: A wonder, master! here's a costard broken in a shin.

speeches.speech: Some enigma, some riddle: come, thy l'envoy; begin.
lines: Some enigma, some riddle: come, thy l'envoy; begin.

speeches.speech: No enigma, no riddle, no l'envoy; no salve in the mail, sir: O, sir, plantain, a plain plantain! no l'envoy, no l'envoy; no salve, sir, but a plantain!
lines: No enigma, no riddle, no l'envoy; no salve in the
mail, sir: O, sir, plantain, a plain plantain! no
l'envoy, no l'envoy; no salve, sir, but a plantain!

speeches.speech: By virtue, thou enforcest laughter; thy silly thought my spleen; the heaving of my lungs provokes me to ridiculous smiling. O, pardon me, my stars! Doth the inconsiderate take salve for l'envoy, and the word l'envoy for a salve?
lines: By virtue, thou enforcest laughter; thy silly
thought my spleen; the heaving of my lungs provokes
me to ridiculous smiling. O, pardon me, my stars!
Doth the inconsiderate take salve for l'envoy, and
the word l'envoy for a salve?

speeches.speech: Do the wise think them other? is not l'envoy a salve?
lines: Do the wise think them other? is not l'envoy a salve?

speeches.speech: No, page: it is an epilogue or discourse, to make plain Some obscure precedence that hath tofore been sain. I will example it: The fox, the ape, and the humble-bee, Were still at odds, being but three. There's the moral. Now the l'envoy.
lines: No, page: it is an epilogue or discourse, to make plain
Some obscure precedence that hath tofore been sain.
I will example it:
The fox, the ape, and the humble-bee,
Were still at odds, being but three.
There's the moral. Now the l'envoy.

speeches.speech: I will add the l'envoy. Say the moral again.
lines: I will add the l'envoy. Say the moral again.

speeches.speech: The fox, the ape, and the humble-bee, Were still at odds, being but three.
lines: The fox, the ape, and the humble-bee,
Were still at odds, being but three.

speeches.speech: Until the goose came out of door, And stay'd the odds by adding four. Now will I begin your moral, and do you follow with my l'envoy. The fox, the ape, and the humble-bee, Were still at odds, being but three.
lines: Until the goose came out of door,
And stay'd the odds by adding four.
Now will I begin your moral, and do you follow with
my l'envoy.
The fox, the ape, and the humble-bee,
Were still at odds, being but three.

speeches.speech: Until the goose came out of door, Staying the odds by adding four.
lines: Until the goose came out of door,
Staying the odds by adding four.

speeches.speech: A good l'envoy, ending in the goose: would you desire more?
lines: A good l'envoy, ending in the goose: would you
desire more?

speeches.speech: The boy hath sold him a bargain, a goose, that's flat. Sir, your pennyworth is good, an your goose be fat. To sell a bargain well is as cunning as fast and loose: Let me see; a fat l'envoy; ay, that's a fat goose.
lines: The boy hath sold him a bargain, a goose, that's flat.
Sir, your pennyworth is good, an your goose be fat.
To sell a bargain well is as cunning as fast and loose:
Let me see; a fat l'envoy; ay, that's a fat goose.

speeches.speech: Come hither, come hither. How did this argument begin?
lines: Come hither, come hither. How did this argument begin?

speeches.speech: By saying that a costard was broken in a shin. Then call'd you for the l'envoy.
lines: By saying that a costard was broken in a shin.
Then call'd you for the l'envoy.

speeches.speech: True, and I for a plantain: thus came your argument in; Then the boy's fat l'envoy, the goose that you bought; And he ended the market.
lines: True, and I for a plantain: thus came your
argument in;
Then the boy's fat l'envoy, the goose that you bought;
And he ended the market.

speeches.speech: But tell me; how was there a costard broken in a shin?
lines: But tell me; how was there a costard broken in a shin?

speeches.speech: I will tell you sensibly.
lines: I will tell you sensibly.

speeches.speech: Thou hast no feeling of it, Moth: I will speak that l'envoy: I Costard, running out, that was safely within, Fell over the threshold and broke my shin.
lines: Thou hast no feeling of it, Moth: I will speak that l'envoy:
I Costard, running out, that was safely within,
Fell over the threshold and broke my shin.

speeches.speech: We will talk no more of this matter.
lines: We will talk no more of this matter.

speeches.speech: Till there be more matter in the shin.
lines: Till there be more matter in the shin.

speeches.speech: Sirrah Costard, I will enfranchise thee.
lines: Sirrah Costard, I will enfranchise thee.

speeches.speech: O, marry me to one Frances: I smell some l'envoy, some goose, in this.
lines: O, marry me to one Frances: I smell some l'envoy,
some goose, in this.

speeches.speech: By my sweet soul, I mean setting thee at liberty, enfreedoming thy person; thou wert immured, restrained, captivated, bound.
lines: By my sweet soul, I mean setting thee at liberty,
enfreedoming thy person; thou wert immured,
restrained, captivated, bound.

speeches.speech: True, true; and now you will be my purgation and let me loose.
lines: True, true; and now you will be my purgation and let me loose.

speeches.speech: I give thee thy liberty, set thee from durance; and, in lieu thereof, impose on thee nothing but this: bear this significant [Giving a letter] to the country maid Jaquenetta: there is remuneration; for the best ward of mine honour is rewarding my dependents. Moth, follow.
lines: I give thee thy liberty, set thee from durance; and,
in lieu thereof, impose on thee nothing but this:
bear this significant
[Giving a letter]
to the country maid Jaquenetta:
there is remuneration; for the best ward of mine
honour is rewarding my dependents. Moth, follow.

speeches.speech: Like the sequel, I. Signior Costard, adieu.
lines: Like the sequel, I. Signior Costard, adieu.

speeches.speech: My sweet ounce of man's flesh! my incony Jew! [Exit MOTH] Now will I look to his remuneration. Remuneration! O, that's the Latin word for three farthings: three farthings--remuneration.--'What's the price of this inkle?'--'One penny.'--'No, I'll give you a remuneration:' why, it carries it. Remuneration! why, it is a fairer name than French crown. I will never buy and sell out of this word.
lines: My sweet ounce of man's flesh! my incony Jew!
[Exit MOTH]
Now will I look to his remuneration. Remuneration!
O, that's the Latin word for three farthings: three
farthings--remuneration.--'What's the price of this
inkle?'--'One penny.'--'No, I'll give you a
remuneration:' why, it carries it. Remuneration!
why, it is a fairer name than French crown. I will
never buy and sell out of this word.

speeches.speech: O, my good knave Costard! exceedingly well met.
lines: O, my good knave Costard! exceedingly well met.

speeches.speech: Pray you, sir, how much carnation ribbon may a man buy for a remuneration?
lines: Pray you, sir, how much carnation ribbon may a man
buy for a remuneration?

speeches.speech: What is a remuneration?
lines: What is a remuneration?

speeches.speech: Marry, sir, halfpenny farthing.
lines: Marry, sir, halfpenny farthing.

speeches.speech: Why, then, three-farthing worth of silk.
lines: Why, then, three-farthing worth of silk.

speeches.speech: I thank your worship: God be wi' you!
lines: I thank your worship: God be wi' you!

speeches.speech: Stay, slave; I must employ thee: As thou wilt win my favour, good my knave, Do one thing for me that I shall entreat.
lines: Stay, slave; I must employ thee:
As thou wilt win my favour, good my knave,
Do one thing for me that I shall entreat.

speeches.speech: When would you have it done, sir?
lines: When would you have it done, sir?

speeches.speech: This afternoon.
lines: This afternoon.

speeches.speech: Well, I will do it, sir: fare you well.
lines: Well, I will do it, sir: fare you well.

speeches.speech: Thou knowest not what it is.
lines: Thou knowest not what it is.

speeches.speech: I shall know, sir, when I have done it.
lines: I shall know, sir, when I have done it.

speeches.speech: Why, villain, thou must know first.
lines: Why, villain, thou must know first.

speeches.speech: I will come to your worship to-morrow morning.
lines: I will come to your worship to-morrow morning.

speeches.speech: It must be done this afternoon. Hark, slave, it is but this: The princess comes to hunt here in the park, And in her train there is a gentle lady; When tongues speak sweetly, then they name her name, And Rosaline they call her: ask for her; And to her white hand see thou do commend This seal'd-up counsel. There's thy guerdon; go.
lines: It must be done this afternoon.
Hark, slave, it is but this:
The princess comes to hunt here in the park,
And in her train there is a gentle lady;
When tongues speak sweetly, then they name her name,
And Rosaline they call her: ask for her;
And to her white hand see thou do commend
This seal'd-up counsel. There's thy guerdon; go.

speeches.speech: Gardon, O sweet gardon! better than remuneration, a'leven-pence farthing better: most sweet gardon! I will do it sir, in print. Gardon! Remuneration!
lines: Gardon, O sweet gardon! better than remuneration,
a'leven-pence farthing better: most sweet gardon! I
will do it sir, in print. Gardon! Remuneration!

speeches.speech: And I, forsooth, in love! I, that have been love's whip; A very beadle to a humorous sigh; A critic, nay, a night-watch constable; A domineering pedant o'er the boy; Than whom no mortal so magnificent! This whimpled, whining, purblind, wayward boy; This senior-junior, giant-dwarf, Dan Cupid; Regent of love-rhymes, lord of folded arms, The anointed sovereign of sighs and groans, Liege of all loiterers and malcontents, Dread prince of plackets, king of codpieces, Sole imperator and great general Of trotting 'paritors:--O my little heart:-- And I to be a corporal of his field, And wear his colours like a tumbler's hoop! What, I! I love! I sue! I seek a wife! A woman, that is like a German clock, Still a-repairing, ever out of frame, And never going aright, being a watch, But being watch'd that it may still go right! Nay, to be perjured, which is worst of all; And, among three, to love the worst of all; A wightly wanton with a velvet brow, With two pitch-balls stuck in her face for eyes; Ay, and by heaven, one that will do the deed Though Argus were her eunuch and her guard: And I to sigh for her! to watch for her! To pray for her! Go to; it is a plague That Cupid will impose for my neglect Of his almighty dreadful little might. Well, I will love, write, sigh, pray, sue and groan: Some men must love my lady and some Joan.
lines: And I, forsooth, in love! I, that have been love's whip;
A very beadle to a humorous sigh;
A critic, nay, a night-watch constable;
A domineering pedant o'er the boy;
Than whom no mortal so magnificent!
This whimpled, whining, purblind, wayward boy;
This senior-junior, giant-dwarf, Dan Cupid;
Regent of love-rhymes, lord of folded arms,
The anointed sovereign of sighs and groans,
Liege of all loiterers and malcontents,
Dread prince of plackets, king of codpieces,
Sole imperator and great general
Of trotting 'paritors:--O my little heart:--
And I to be a corporal of his field,
And wear his colours like a tumbler's hoop!
What, I! I love! I sue! I seek a wife!
A woman, that is like a German clock,
Still a-repairing, ever out of frame,
And never going aright, being a watch,
But being watch'd that it may still go right!
Nay, to be perjured, which is worst of all;
And, among three, to love the worst of all;
A wightly wanton with a velvet brow,
With two pitch-balls stuck in her face for eyes;
Ay, and by heaven, one that will do the deed
Though Argus were her eunuch and her guard:
And I to sigh for her! to watch for her!
To pray for her! Go to; it is a plague
That Cupid will impose for my neglect
Of his almighty dreadful little might.
Well, I will love, write, sigh, pray, sue and groan:
Some men must love my lady and some Joan.

speeches.speech: Was that the king, that spurred his horse so hard Against the steep uprising of the hill?
lines: Was that the king, that spurred his horse so hard
Against the steep uprising of the hill?

speeches.speech: I know not; but I think it was not he.
lines: I know not; but I think it was not he.

speeches.speech: Whoe'er a' was, a' show'd a mounting mind. Well, lords, to-day we shall have our dispatch: On Saturday we will return to France. Then, forester, my friend, where is the bush That we must stand and play the murderer in?
lines: Whoe'er a' was, a' show'd a mounting mind.
Well, lords, to-day we shall have our dispatch:
On Saturday we will return to France.
Then, forester, my friend, where is the bush
That we must stand and play the murderer in?

speeches.speech: Hereby, upon the edge of yonder coppice; A stand where you may make the fairest shoot.
lines: Hereby, upon the edge of yonder coppice;
A stand where you may make the fairest shoot.

speeches.speech: I thank my beauty, I am fair that shoot, And thereupon thou speak'st the fairest shoot.
lines: I thank my beauty, I am fair that shoot,
And thereupon thou speak'st the fairest shoot.

speeches.speech: Pardon me, madam, for I meant not so.
lines: Pardon me, madam, for I meant not so.

speeches.speech: What, what? first praise me and again say no? O short-lived pride! Not fair? alack for woe!
lines: What, what? first praise me and again say no?
O short-lived pride! Not fair? alack for woe!

speeches.speech: Yes, madam, fair.
lines: Yes, madam, fair.

speeches.speech: Nay, never paint me now: Where fair is not, praise cannot mend the brow. Here, good my glass, take this for telling true: Fair payment for foul words is more than due.
lines: Nay, never paint me now:
Where fair is not, praise cannot mend the brow.
Here, good my glass, take this for telling true:
Fair payment for foul words is more than due.

speeches.speech: Nothing but fair is that which you inherit.
lines: Nothing but fair is that which you inherit.

speeches.speech: See see, my beauty will be saved by merit! O heresy in fair, fit for these days! A giving hand, though foul, shall have fair praise. But come, the bow: now mercy goes to kill, And shooting well is then accounted ill. Thus will I save my credit in the shoot: Not wounding, pity would not let me do't; If wounding, then it was to show my skill, That more for praise than purpose meant to kill. And out of question so it is sometimes, Glory grows guilty of detested crimes, When, for fame's sake, for praise, an outward part, We bend to that the working of the heart; As I for praise alone now seek to spill The poor deer's blood, that my heart means no ill.
lines: See see, my beauty will be saved by merit!
O heresy in fair, fit for these days!
A giving hand, though foul, shall have fair praise.
But come, the bow: now mercy goes to kill,
And shooting well is then accounted ill.
Thus will I save my credit in the shoot:
Not wounding, pity would not let me do't;
If wounding, then it was to show my skill,
That more for praise than purpose meant to kill.
And out of question so it is sometimes,
Glory grows guilty of detested crimes,
When, for fame's sake, for praise, an outward part,
We bend to that the working of the heart;
As I for praise alone now seek to spill
The poor deer's blood, that my heart means no ill.

speeches.speech: Do not curst wives hold that self-sovereignty Only for praise sake, when they strive to be Lords o'er their lords?
lines: Do not curst wives hold that self-sovereignty
Only for praise sake, when they strive to be
Lords o'er their lords?

speeches.speech: Only for praise: and praise we may afford To any lady that subdues a lord.
lines: Only for praise: and praise we may afford
To any lady that subdues a lord.

speeches.speech: Here comes a member of the commonwealth.
lines: Here comes a member of the commonwealth.

speeches.speech: God dig-you-den all! Pray you, which is the head lady?
lines: God dig-you-den all! Pray you, which is the head lady?

speeches.speech: Thou shalt know her, fellow, by the rest that have no heads.
lines: Thou shalt know her, fellow, by the rest that have no heads.

speeches.speech: Which is the greatest lady, the highest?
lines: Which is the greatest lady, the highest?

speeches.speech: The thickest and the tallest.
lines: The thickest and the tallest.

speeches.speech: The thickest and the tallest! it is so; truth is truth. An your waist, mistress, were as slender as my wit, One o' these maids' girdles for your waist should be fit. Are not you the chief woman? you are the thickest here.
lines: The thickest and the tallest! it is so; truth is truth.
An your waist, mistress, were as slender as my wit,
One o' these maids' girdles for your waist should be fit.
Are not you the chief woman? you are the thickest here.

speeches.speech: What's your will, sir? what's your will?
lines: What's your will, sir? what's your will?

speeches.speech: I have a letter from Monsieur Biron to one Lady Rosaline.
lines: I have a letter from Monsieur Biron to one Lady Rosaline.

speeches.speech: O, thy letter, thy letter! he's a good friend of mine: Stand aside, good bearer. Boyet, you can carve; Break up this capon.
lines: O, thy letter, thy letter! he's a good friend of mine:
Stand aside, good bearer. Boyet, you can carve;
Break up this capon.

speeches.speech: I am bound to serve. This letter is mistook, it importeth none here; It is writ to Jaquenetta.
lines: I am bound to serve.
This letter is mistook, it importeth none here;
It is writ to Jaquenetta.

speeches.speech: We will read it, I swear. Break the neck of the wax, and every one give ear.
lines: We will read it, I swear.
Break the neck of the wax, and every one give ear.

speeches.speech: 'By heaven, that thou art fair, is most infallible; true, that thou art beauteous; truth itself, that thou art lovely. More fairer than fair, beautiful than beauteous, truer than truth itself, have commiseration on thy heroical vassal! The magnanimous and most illustrate king Cophetua set eye upon the pernicious and indubitate beggar Zenelophon; and he it was that might rightly say, Veni, vidi, vici; which to annothanize in the vulgar,--O base and obscure vulgar!--videlicet, He came, saw, and overcame: he came, one; saw two; overcame, three. Who came? the king: why did he come? to see: why did he see? to overcome: to whom came he? to the beggar: what saw he? the beggar: who overcame he? the beggar. The conclusion is victory: on whose side? the king's. The captive is enriched: on whose side? the beggar's. The catastrophe is a nuptial: on whose side? the king's: no, on both in one, or one in both. I am the king; for so stands the comparison: thou the beggar; for so witnesseth thy lowliness. Shall I command thy love? I may: shall I enforce thy love? I could: shall I entreat thy love? I will. What shalt thou exchange for rags? robes; for tittles? titles; for thyself? me. Thus, expecting thy reply, I profane my lips on thy foot, my eyes on thy picture. and my heart on thy every part. Thine, in the dearest design of industry, DON ADRIANO DE ARMADO.' Thus dost thou hear the Nemean lion roar 'Gainst thee, thou lamb, that standest as his prey. Submissive fall his princely feet before, And he from forage will incline to play: But if thou strive, poor soul, what art thou then? Food for his rage, repasture for his den.
lines: 'By heaven, that thou art fair, is most infallible;
true, that thou art beauteous; truth itself, that
thou art lovely. More fairer than fair, beautiful
than beauteous, truer than truth itself, have
commiseration on thy heroical vassal! The
magnanimous and most illustrate king Cophetua set
eye upon the pernicious and indubitate beggar
Zenelophon; and he it was that might rightly say,
Veni, vidi, vici; which to annothanize in the
vulgar,--O base and obscure vulgar!--videlicet, He
came, saw, and overcame: he came, one; saw two;
overcame, three. Who came? the king: why did he
come? to see: why did he see? to overcome: to
whom came he? to the beggar: what saw he? the
beggar: who overcame he? the beggar. The
conclusion is victory: on whose side? the king's.
The captive is enriched: on whose side? the
beggar's. The catastrophe is a nuptial: on whose
side? the king's: no, on both in one, or one in
both. I am the king; for so stands the comparison:
thou the beggar; for so witnesseth thy lowliness.
Shall I command thy love? I may: shall I enforce
thy love? I could: shall I entreat thy love? I
will. What shalt thou exchange for rags? robes;
for tittles? titles; for thyself? me. Thus,
expecting thy reply, I profane my lips on thy foot,
my eyes on thy picture. and my heart on thy every
part. Thine, in the dearest design of industry,
DON ADRIANO DE ARMADO.'
Thus dost thou hear the Nemean lion roar
'Gainst thee, thou lamb, that standest as his prey.
Submissive fall his princely feet before,
And he from forage will incline to play:
But if thou strive, poor soul, what art thou then?
Food for his rage, repasture for his den.

speeches.speech: What plume of feathers is he that indited this letter? What vane? what weathercock? did you ever hear better?
lines: What plume of feathers is he that indited this letter?
What vane? what weathercock? did you ever hear better?

speeches.speech: I am much deceived but I remember the style.
lines: I am much deceived but I remember the style.

speeches.speech: Else your memory is bad, going o'er it erewhile.
lines: Else your memory is bad, going o'er it erewhile.

speeches.speech: This Armado is a Spaniard, that keeps here in court; A phantasime, a Monarcho, and one that makes sport To the prince and his bookmates.
lines: This Armado is a Spaniard, that keeps here in court;
A phantasime, a Monarcho, and one that makes sport
To the prince and his bookmates.

speeches.speech: Thou fellow, a word: Who gave thee this letter?
lines: Thou fellow, a word:
Who gave thee this letter?

speeches.speech: I told you; my lord.
lines: I told you; my lord.

speeches.speech: To whom shouldst thou give it?
lines: To whom shouldst thou give it?

speeches.speech: From my lord to my lady.
lines: From my lord to my lady.

speeches.speech: From which lord to which lady?
lines: From which lord to which lady?

speeches.speech: From my lord Biron, a good master of mine, To a lady of France that he call'd Rosaline.
lines: From my lord Biron, a good master of mine,
To a lady of France that he call'd Rosaline.

speeches.speech: Thou hast mistaken his letter. Come, lords, away. [To ROSALINE] Here, sweet, put up this: 'twill be thine another day.
lines: Thou hast mistaken his letter. Come, lords, away.
[To ROSALINE]
Here, sweet, put up this: 'twill be thine another day.

speeches.speech: Who is the suitor? who is the suitor?
lines: Who is the suitor? who is the suitor?

speeches.speech: Shall I teach you to know?
lines: Shall I teach you to know?

speeches.speech: Ay, my continent of beauty.
lines: Ay, my continent of beauty.

speeches.speech: Why, she that bears the bow. Finely put off!
lines: Why, she that bears the bow.
Finely put off!

speeches.speech: My lady goes to kill horns; but, if thou marry, Hang me by the neck, if horns that year miscarry. Finely put on!
lines: My lady goes to kill horns; but, if thou marry,
Hang me by the neck, if horns that year miscarry.
Finely put on!

speeches.speech: Well, then, I am the shooter.
lines: Well, then, I am the shooter.

speeches.speech: And who is your deer?
lines: And who is your deer?

speeches.speech: If we choose by the horns, yourself come not near. Finely put on, indeed!
lines: If we choose by the horns, yourself come not near.
Finely put on, indeed!

speeches.speech: You still wrangle with her, Boyet, and she strikes at the brow.
lines: You still wrangle with her, Boyet, and she strikes
at the brow.

speeches.speech: But she herself is hit lower: have I hit her now?
lines: But she herself is hit lower: have I hit her now?

speeches.speech: Shall I come upon thee with an old saying, that was a man when King Pepin of France was a little boy, as touching the hit it?
lines: Shall I come upon thee with an old saying, that was
a man when King Pepin of France was a little boy, as
touching the hit it?

speeches.speech: So I may answer thee with one as old, that was a woman when Queen Guinover of Britain was a little wench, as touching the hit it.
lines: So I may answer thee with one as old, that was a
woman when Queen Guinover of Britain was a little
wench, as touching the hit it.

speeches.speech: Thou canst not hit it, hit it, hit it, Thou canst not hit it, my good man.
lines: Thou canst not hit it, hit it, hit it,
Thou canst not hit it, my good man.

speeches.speech: An I cannot, cannot, cannot, An I cannot, another can.
lines: An I cannot, cannot, cannot,
An I cannot, another can.

speeches.speech: By my troth, most pleasant: how both did fit it!
lines: By my troth, most pleasant: how both did fit it!

speeches.speech: A mark marvellous well shot, for they both did hit it.
lines: A mark marvellous well shot, for they both did hit it.

speeches.speech: A mark! O, mark but that mark! A mark, says my lady! Let the mark have a prick in't, to mete at, if it may be.
lines: A mark! O, mark but that mark! A mark, says my lady!
Let the mark have a prick in't, to mete at, if it may be.

speeches.speech: Wide o' the bow hand! i' faith, your hand is out.
lines: Wide o' the bow hand! i' faith, your hand is out.

speeches.speech: Indeed, a' must shoot nearer, or he'll ne'er hit the clout.
lines: Indeed, a' must shoot nearer, or he'll ne'er hit the clout.

speeches.speech: An if my hand be out, then belike your hand is in.
lines: An if my hand be out, then belike your hand is in.

speeches.speech: Then will she get the upshoot by cleaving the pin.
lines: Then will she get the upshoot by cleaving the pin.

speeches.speech: Come, come, you talk greasily; your lips grow foul.
lines: Come, come, you talk greasily; your lips grow foul.

speeches.speech: She's too hard for you at pricks, sir: challenge her to bowl.
lines: She's too hard for you at pricks, sir: challenge her to bowl.

speeches.speech: I fear too much rubbing. Good night, my good owl.
lines: I fear too much rubbing. Good night, my good owl.

speeches.speech: By my soul, a swain! a most simple clown! Lord, Lord, how the ladies and I have put him down! O' my troth, most sweet jests! most incony vulgar wit! When it comes so smoothly off, so obscenely, as it were, so fit. Armado o' th' one side,--O, a most dainty man! To see him walk before a lady and to bear her fan! To see him kiss his hand! and how most sweetly a' will swear! And his page o' t' other side, that handful of wit! Ah, heavens, it is a most pathetical nit! Sola, sola!
lines: By my soul, a swain! a most simple clown!
Lord, Lord, how the ladies and I have put him down!
O' my troth, most sweet jests! most incony
vulgar wit!
When it comes so smoothly off, so obscenely, as it
were, so fit.
Armado o' th' one side,--O, a most dainty man!
To see him walk before a lady and to bear her fan!
To see him kiss his hand! and how most sweetly a'
will swear!
And his page o' t' other side, that handful of wit!
Ah, heavens, it is a most pathetical nit!
Sola, sola!

speeches.speech: Very reverend sport, truly; and done in the testimony of a good conscience.
lines: Very reverend sport, truly; and done in the testimony
of a good conscience.

speeches.speech: The deer was, as you know, sanguis, in blood; ripe as the pomewater, who now hangeth like a jewel in the ear of caelo, the sky, the welkin, the heaven; and anon falleth like a crab on the face of terra, the soil, the land, the earth.
lines: The deer was, as you know, sanguis, in blood; ripe
as the pomewater, who now hangeth like a jewel in
the ear of caelo, the sky, the welkin, the heaven;
and anon falleth like a crab on the face of terra,
the soil, the land, the earth.

speeches.speech: Truly, Master Holofernes, the epithets are sweetly varied, like a scholar at the least: but, sir, I assure ye, it was a buck of the first head.
lines: Truly, Master Holofernes, the epithets are sweetly
varied, like a scholar at the least: but, sir, I
assure ye, it was a buck of the first head.

speeches.speech: Sir Nathaniel, haud credo.
lines: Sir Nathaniel, haud credo.

speeches.speech: 'Twas not a haud credo; 'twas a pricket.
lines: 'Twas not a haud credo; 'twas a pricket.

speeches.speech: Most barbarous intimation! yet a kind of insinuation, as it were, in via, in way, of explication; facere, as it were, replication, or rather, ostentare, to show, as it were, his inclination, after his undressed, unpolished, uneducated, unpruned, untrained, or rather, unlettered, or ratherest, unconfirmed fashion, to insert again my haud credo for a deer.
lines: Most barbarous intimation! yet a kind of
insinuation, as it were, in via, in way, of
explication; facere, as it were, replication, or
rather, ostentare, to show, as it were, his
inclination, after his undressed, unpolished,
uneducated, unpruned, untrained, or rather,
unlettered, or ratherest, unconfirmed fashion, to
insert again my haud credo for a deer.

speeches.speech: I said the deer was not a haud credo; twas a pricket.
lines: I said the deer was not a haud credo; twas a pricket.

speeches.speech: Twice-sod simplicity, his coctus! O thou monster Ignorance, how deformed dost thou look!
lines: Twice-sod simplicity, his coctus!
O thou monster Ignorance, how deformed dost thou look!

speeches.speech: Sir, he hath never fed of the dainties that are bred in a book; he hath not eat paper, as it were; he hath not drunk ink: his intellect is not replenished; he is only an animal, only sensible in the duller parts: And such barren plants are set before us, that we thankful should be, Which we of taste and feeling are, for those parts that do fructify in us more than he. For as it would ill become me to be vain, indiscreet, or a fool, So were there a patch set on learning, to see him in a school: But omne bene, say I; being of an old father's mind, Many can brook the weather that love not the wind.
lines: Sir, he hath never fed of the dainties that are bred
in a book; he hath not eat paper, as it were; he
hath not drunk ink: his intellect is not
replenished; he is only an animal, only sensible in
the duller parts:
And such barren plants are set before us, that we
thankful should be,
Which we of taste and feeling are, for those parts that
do fructify in us more than he.
For as it would ill become me to be vain, indiscreet, or a fool,
So were there a patch set on learning, to see him in a school:
But omne bene, say I; being of an old father's mind,
Many can brook the weather that love not the wind.

speeches.speech: You two are book-men: can you tell me by your wit What was a month old at Cain's birth, that's not five weeks old as yet?
lines: You two are book-men: can you tell me by your wit
What was a month old at Cain's birth, that's not five
weeks old as yet?

speeches.speech: Dictynna, goodman Dull; Dictynna, goodman Dull.
lines: Dictynna, goodman Dull; Dictynna, goodman Dull.

speeches.speech: What is Dictynna?
lines: What is Dictynna?

speeches.speech: A title to Phoebe, to Luna, to the moon.
lines: A title to Phoebe, to Luna, to the moon.

speeches.speech: The moon was a month old when Adam was no more, And raught not to five weeks when he came to five-score. The allusion holds in the exchange.
lines: The moon was a month old when Adam was no more,
And raught not to five weeks when he came to
five-score.
The allusion holds in the exchange.

speeches.speech: 'Tis true indeed; the collusion holds in the exchange.
lines: 'Tis true indeed; the collusion holds in the exchange.

speeches.speech: God comfort thy capacity! I say, the allusion holds in the exchange.
lines: God comfort thy capacity! I say, the allusion holds
in the exchange.

speeches.speech: And I say, the pollusion holds in the exchange; for the moon is never but a month old: and I say beside that, 'twas a pricket that the princess killed.
lines: And I say, the pollusion holds in the exchange; for
the moon is never but a month old: and I say beside
that, 'twas a pricket that the princess killed.

speeches.speech: Sir Nathaniel, will you hear an extemporal epitaph on the death of the deer? And, to humour the ignorant, call I the deer the princess killed a pricket.
lines: Sir Nathaniel, will you hear an extemporal epitaph
on the death of the deer? And, to humour the
ignorant, call I the deer the princess killed a pricket.

speeches.speech: Perge, good Master Holofernes, perge; so it shall please you to abrogate scurrility.
lines: Perge, good Master Holofernes, perge; so it shall
please you to abrogate scurrility.

speeches.speech: I will something affect the letter, for it argues facility. The preyful princess pierced and prick'd a pretty pleasing pricket; Some say a sore; but not a sore, till now made sore with shooting. The dogs did yell: put L to sore, then sorel jumps from thicket; Or pricket sore, or else sorel; the people fall a-hooting. If sore be sore, then L to sore makes fifty sores one sorel. Of one sore I an hundred make by adding but one more L.
lines: I will something affect the letter, for it argues facility.
The preyful princess pierced and prick'd a pretty
pleasing pricket;
Some say a sore; but not a sore, till now made
sore with shooting.
The dogs did yell: put L to sore, then sorel jumps
from thicket;
Or pricket sore, or else sorel; the people fall a-hooting.
If sore be sore, then L to sore makes fifty sores
one sorel.
Of one sore I an hundred make by adding but one more L.

speeches.speech: A rare talent!
lines: A rare talent!

speeches.speech: [Aside] If a talent be a claw, look how he claws him with a talent.
lines: [Aside] If a talent be a claw, look how he claws
him with a talent.

speeches.speech: This is a gift that I have, simple, simple; a foolish extravagant spirit, full of forms, figures, shapes, objects, ideas, apprehensions, motions, revolutions: these are begot in the ventricle of memory, nourished in the womb of pia mater, and delivered upon the mellowing of occasion. But the gift is good in those in whom it is acute, and I am thankful for it.
lines: This is a gift that I have, simple, simple; a
foolish extravagant spirit, full of forms, figures,
shapes, objects, ideas, apprehensions, motions,
revolutions: these are begot in the ventricle of
memory, nourished in the womb of pia mater, and
delivered upon the mellowing of occasion. But the
gift is good in those in whom it is acute, and I am
thankful for it.

speeches.speech: Sir, I praise the Lord for you; and so may my parishioners; for their sons are well tutored by you, and their daughters profit very greatly under you: you are a good member of the commonwealth.
lines: Sir, I praise the Lord for you; and so may my
parishioners; for their sons are well tutored by
you, and their daughters profit very greatly under
you: you are a good member of the commonwealth.

speeches.speech: Mehercle, if their sons be ingenuous, they shall want no instruction; if their daughters be capable, I will put it to them: but vir sapit qui pauca loquitur; a soul feminine saluteth us.
lines: Mehercle, if their sons be ingenuous, they shall
want no instruction; if their daughters be capable,
I will put it to them: but vir sapit qui pauca
loquitur; a soul feminine saluteth us.

speeches.speech: God give you good morrow, master Parson.
lines: God give you good morrow, master Parson.

speeches.speech: Master Parson, quasi pers-on. An if one should be pierced, which is the one?
lines: Master Parson, quasi pers-on. An if one should be
pierced, which is the one?

speeches.speech: Marry, master schoolmaster, he that is likest to a hogshead.
lines: Marry, master schoolmaster, he that is likest to a hogshead.

speeches.speech: Piercing a hogshead! a good lustre of conceit in a tuft of earth; fire enough for a flint, pearl enough for a swine: 'tis pretty; it is well.
lines: Piercing a hogshead! a good lustre of conceit in a
tuft of earth; fire enough for a flint, pearl enough
for a swine: 'tis pretty; it is well.

speeches.speech: Good master Parson, be so good as read me this letter: it was given me by Costard, and sent me from Don Armado: I beseech you, read it.
lines: Good master Parson, be so good as read me this
letter: it was given me by Costard, and sent me
from Don Armado: I beseech you, read it.

speeches.speech: Fauste, precor gelida quando pecus omne sub umbra Ruminat,--and so forth. Ah, good old Mantuan! I may speak of thee as the traveller doth of Venice; Venetia, Venetia, Chi non ti vede non ti pretia. Old Mantuan, old Mantuan! who understandeth thee not, loves thee not. Ut, re, sol, la, mi, fa. Under pardon, sir, what are the contents? or rather, as Horace says in his--What, my soul, verses?
lines: Fauste, precor gelida quando pecus omne sub umbra
Ruminat,--and so forth. Ah, good old Mantuan! I
may speak of thee as the traveller doth of Venice;
Venetia, Venetia,
Chi non ti vede non ti pretia.
Old Mantuan, old Mantuan! who understandeth thee
not, loves thee not. Ut, re, sol, la, mi, fa.
Under pardon, sir, what are the contents? or rather,
as Horace says in his--What, my soul, verses?

speeches.speech: Ay, sir, and very learned.
lines: Ay, sir, and very learned.

speeches.speech: Let me hear a staff, a stanze, a verse; lege, domine.
lines: Let me hear a staff, a stanze, a verse; lege, domine.

speeches.speech: [Reads] If love make me forsworn, how shall I swear to love? Ah, never faith could hold, if not to beauty vow'd! Though to myself forsworn, to thee I'll faithful prove: Those thoughts to me were oaks, to thee like osiers bow'd. Study his bias leaves and makes his book thine eyes, Where all those pleasures live that art would comprehend: If knowledge be the mark, to know thee shall suffice; Well learned is that tongue that well can thee commend, All ignorant that soul that sees thee without wonder; Which is to me some praise that I thy parts admire: Thy eye Jove's lightning bears, thy voice his dreadful thunder, Which not to anger bent, is music and sweet fire. Celestial as thou art, O, pardon, love, this wrong, That sings heaven's praise with such an earthly tongue.
lines: [Reads]
If love make me forsworn, how shall I swear to love?
Ah, never faith could hold, if not to beauty vow'd!
Though to myself forsworn, to thee I'll faithful prove:
Those thoughts to me were oaks, to thee like
osiers bow'd.
Study his bias leaves and makes his book thine eyes,
Where all those pleasures live that art would
comprehend:
If knowledge be the mark, to know thee shall suffice;
Well learned is that tongue that well can thee commend,
All ignorant that soul that sees thee without wonder;
Which is to me some praise that I thy parts admire:
Thy eye Jove's lightning bears, thy voice his dreadful thunder,
Which not to anger bent, is music and sweet fire.
Celestial as thou art, O, pardon, love, this wrong,
That sings heaven's praise with such an earthly tongue.

speeches.speech: You find not the apostraphas, and so miss the accent: let me supervise the canzonet. Here are only numbers ratified; but, for the elegancy, facility, and golden cadence of poesy, caret. Ovidius Naso was the man: and why, indeed, Naso, but for smelling out the odouriferous flowers of fancy, the jerks of invention? Imitari is nothing: so doth the hound his master, the ape his keeper, the tired horse his rider. But, damosella virgin, was this directed to you?

speeches.speech: Ay, sir, from one Monsieur Biron, one of the strange queen's lords.
lines: Ay, sir, from one Monsieur Biron, one of the strange
queen's lords.

speeches.speech: I will overglance the superscript: 'To the snow-white hand of the most beauteous Lady Rosaline.' I will look again on the intellect of the letter, for the nomination of the party writing to the person written unto: 'Your ladyship's in all desired employment, BIRON.' Sir Nathaniel, this Biron is one of the votaries with the king; and here he hath framed a letter to a sequent of the stranger queen's, which accidentally, or by the way of progression, hath miscarried. Trip and go, my sweet; deliver this paper into the royal hand of the king: it may concern much. Stay not thy compliment; I forgive thy duty; adieu.

compliment; I forgive thy duty; adieu.

speeches.speech: Good Costard, go with me. Sir, God save your life!
lines: Good Costard, go with me. Sir, God save your life!

speeches.speech: Have with thee, my girl.
lines: Have with thee, my girl.

speeches.speech: Sir, you have done this in the fear of God, very religiously; and, as a certain father saith,--
lines: Sir, you have done this in the fear of God, very

speeches.speech: Sir tell me not of the father; I do fear colourable colours. But to return to the verses: did they please you, Sir Nathaniel?
lines: Sir tell me not of the father; I do fear colourable
colours. But to return to the verses: did they

speeches.speech: Marvellous well for the pen.
lines: Marvellous well for the pen.

speeches.speech: I do dine to-day at the father's of a certain pupil of mine; where, if, before repast, it shall please you to gratify the table with a grace, I will, on my privilege I have with the parents of the foresaid child or pupil, undertake your ben venuto; where I will prove those verses to be very unlearned, neither savouring of poetry, wit, nor invention: I beseech your society.

of mine; where, if, before repast, it shall please
you to gratify the table with a grace, I will, on my

child or pupil, undertake your ben venuto; where I
will prove those verses to be very unlearned,
neither savouring of poetry, wit, nor invention: I

speeches.speech: And thank you too; for society, saith the text, is the happiness of life.
lines: And thank you too; for society, saith the text, is

speeches.speech: And, certes, the text most infallibly concludes it. [To DULL] Sir, I do invite you too; you shall not say me nay: pauca verba. Away! the gentles are at their game, and we will to our recreation.
lines: And, certes, the text most infallibly concludes it.
[To DULL]
Sir, I do invite you too; you shall not

speeches.speech: The king he is hunting the deer; I am coursing myself: they have pitched a toil; I am toiling in a pitch,--pitch that defiles: defile! a foul word. Well, set thee down, sorrow! for so they say the fool said, and so say I, and I the fool: well proved, wit! By the Lord, this love is as mad as Ajax: it kills sheep; it kills me, I a sheep: well proved again o' my side! I will not love: if I do, hang me; i' faith, I will not. O, but her eye,--by this light, but for her eye, I would not love her; yes, for her two eyes. Well, I do nothing in the world but lie, and lie in my throat. By heaven, I do love: and it hath taught me to rhyme and to be melancholy; and here is part of my rhyme, and here my melancholy. Well, she hath one o' my sonnets already: the clown bore it, the fool sent it, and the lady hath it: sweet clown, sweeter fool, sweetest lady! By the world, I would not care a pin, if the other three were in. Here comes one with a paper: God give him grace to groan!

well proved again o' my side! I will not love: if
I do, hang me; i' faith, I will not. O, but her

in the world but lie, and lie in my throat. By

speeches.speech: Ay me!
lines: Ay me!

speeches.speech: [Aside] Shot, by heaven! Proceed, sweet Cupid: thou hast thumped him with thy bird-bolt under the left pap. In faith, secrets!
lines: [Aside] Shot, by heaven! Proceed, sweet Cupid:
thou hast thumped him with thy bird-bolt under the
left pap. In faith, secrets!

speeches.speech: [Reads] So sweet a kiss the golden sun gives not To those fresh morning drops upon the rose, As thy eye-beams, when their fresh rays have smote The night of dew that on my cheeks down flows: Nor shines the silver moon one half so bright Through the transparent bosom of the deep, As doth thy face through tears of mine give light; Thou shinest in every tear that I do weep: No drop but as a coach doth carry thee; So ridest thou triumphing in my woe. Do but behold the tears that swell in me, And they thy glory through my grief will show: But do not love thyself; then thou wilt keep My tears for glasses, and still make me weep. O queen of queens! how far dost thou excel, No thought can think, nor tongue of mortal tell. How shall she know my griefs? I'll drop the paper: Sweet leaves, shade folly. Who is he comes here? [Steps aside] What, Longaville! and reading! listen, ear.
lines: [Reads]
So sweet a kiss the golden sun gives not
To those fresh morning drops upon the rose,
As thy eye-beams, when their fresh rays have smote
The night of dew that on my cheeks down flows:
Nor shines the silver moon one half so bright
Through the transparent bosom of the deep,
As doth thy face through tears of mine give light;
Thou shinest in every tear that I do weep:
No drop but as a coach doth carry thee;
So ridest thou triumphing in my woe.
Do but behold the tears that swell in me,
And they thy glory through my grief will show:
But do not love thyself; then thou wilt keep
My tears for glasses, and still make me weep.
O queen of queens! how far dost thou excel,
No thought can think, nor tongue of mortal tell.
How shall she know my griefs? I'll drop the paper:
Sweet leaves, shade folly. Who is he comes here?
[Steps aside]
What, Longaville! and reading! listen, ear.

speeches.speech: Now, in thy likeness, one more fool appear!
lines: Now, in thy likeness, one more fool appear!

speeches.speech: Ay me, I am forsworn!
lines: Ay me, I am forsworn!

speeches.speech: Why, he comes in like a perjure, wearing papers.
lines: Why, he comes in like a perjure, wearing papers.

speeches.speech: In love, I hope: sweet fellowship in shame!
lines: In love, I hope: sweet fellowship in shame!

speeches.speech: One drunkard loves another of the name.
lines: One drunkard loves another of the name.

speeches.speech: Am I the first that have been perjured so?
lines: Am I the first that have been perjured so?

speeches.speech: I could put thee in comfort. Not by two that I know: Thou makest the triumviry, the corner-cap of society, The shape of Love's Tyburn that hangs up simplicity.
lines: I could put thee in comfort. Not by two that I know:
Thou makest the triumviry, the corner-cap of society,
The shape of Love's Tyburn that hangs up simplicity.

speeches.speech: I fear these stubborn lines lack power to move: O sweet Maria, empress of my love! These numbers will I tear, and write in prose.
lines: I fear these stubborn lines lack power to move:
O sweet Maria, empress of my love!
These numbers will I tear, and write in prose.

speeches.speech: O, rhymes are guards on wanton Cupid's hose: Disfigure not his slop.
lines: O, rhymes are guards on wanton Cupid's hose:
Disfigure not his slop.

speeches.speech: This same shall go. [Reads] Did not the heavenly rhetoric of thine eye, 'Gainst whom the world cannot hold argument, Persuade my heart to this false perjury? Vows for thee broke deserve not punishment. A woman I forswore; but I will prove, Thou being a goddess, I forswore not thee: My vow was earthly, thou a heavenly love; Thy grace being gain'd cures all disgrace in me. Vows are but breath, and breath a vapour is: Then thou, fair sun, which on my earth dost shine, Exhalest this vapour-vow; in thee it is: If broken then, it is no fault of mine: If by me broke, what fool is not so wise To lose an oath to win a paradise?
lines: This same shall go.
[Reads]
Did not the heavenly rhetoric of thine eye,
'Gainst whom the world cannot hold argument,
Persuade my heart to this false perjury?
Vows for thee broke deserve not punishment.
A woman I forswore; but I will prove,
Thou being a goddess, I forswore not thee:
My vow was earthly, thou a heavenly love;
Thy grace being gain'd cures all disgrace in me.
Vows are but breath, and breath a vapour is:
Then thou, fair sun, which on my earth dost shine,
Exhalest this vapour-vow; in thee it is:
If broken then, it is no fault of mine:
If by me broke, what fool is not so wise
To lose an oath to win a paradise?

speeches.speech: This is the liver-vein, which makes flesh a deity, A green goose a goddess: pure, pure idolatry. God amend us, God amend! we are much out o' the way.
lines: This is the liver-vein, which makes flesh a deity,
A green goose a goddess: pure, pure idolatry.
God amend us, God amend! we are much out o' the way.

speeches.speech: By whom shall I send this?--Company! stay.
lines: By whom shall I send this?--Company! stay.

speeches.speech: All hid, all hid; an old infant play. Like a demigod here sit I in the sky. And wretched fools' secrets heedfully o'ereye. More sacks to the mill! O heavens, I have my wish! [Enter DUMAIN, with a paper] Dumain transform'd! four woodcocks in a dish!
lines: All hid, all hid; an old infant play.
Like a demigod here sit I in the sky.
And wretched fools' secrets heedfully o'ereye.
More sacks to the mill! O heavens, I have my wish!
[Enter DUMAIN, with a paper]
Dumain transform'd! four woodcocks in a dish!

speeches.speech: O most divine Kate!
lines: O most divine Kate!

speeches.speech: O most profane coxcomb!
lines: O most profane coxcomb!

speeches.speech: By heaven, the wonder in a mortal eye!
lines: By heaven, the wonder in a mortal eye!

speeches.speech: By earth, she is not, corporal, there you lie.
lines: By earth, she is not, corporal, there you lie.

speeches.speech: Her amber hair for foul hath amber quoted.
lines: Her amber hair for foul hath amber quoted.

speeches.speech: An amber-colour'd raven was well noted.
lines: An amber-colour'd raven was well noted.

speeches.speech: As upright as the cedar.
lines: As upright as the cedar.

speeches.speech: Stoop, I say; Her shoulder is with child.
lines: Stoop, I say;
Her shoulder is with child.

speeches.speech: As fair as day.
lines: As fair as day.

speeches.speech: Ay, as some days; but then no sun must shine.
lines: Ay, as some days; but then no sun must shine.

speeches.speech: O that I had my wish!
lines: O that I had my wish!

speeches.speech: And I had mine!
lines: And I had mine!

speeches.speech: And I mine too, good Lord!
lines: And I mine too, good Lord!

speeches.speech: Amen, so I had mine: is not that a good word?
lines: Amen, so I had mine: is not that a good word?

speeches.speech: I would forget her; but a fever she Reigns in my blood and will remember'd be.
lines: I would forget her; but a fever she
Reigns in my blood and will remember'd be.

speeches.speech: A fever in your blood! why, then incision Would let her out in saucers: sweet misprision!
lines: A fever in your blood! why, then incision
Would let her out in saucers: sweet misprision!

speeches.speech: Once more I'll read the ode that I have writ.
lines: Once more I'll read the ode that I have writ.

speeches.speech: Once more I'll mark how love can vary wit.
lines: Once more I'll mark how love can vary wit.

speeches.speech: [Reads] On a day--alack the day!-- Love, whose month is ever May, Spied a blossom passing fair Playing in the wanton air: Through the velvet leaves the wind, All unseen, can passage find; That the lover, sick to death, Wish himself the heaven's breath. Air, quoth he, thy cheeks may blow; Air, would I might triumph so! But, alack, my hand is sworn Ne'er to pluck thee from thy thorn; Vow, alack, for youth unmeet, Youth so apt to pluck a sweet! Do not call it sin in me, That I am forsworn for thee; Thou for whom Jove would swear Juno but an Ethiope were; And deny himself for Jove, Turning mortal for thy love. This will I send, and something else more plain, That shall express my true love's fasting pain. O, would the king, Biron, and Longaville, Were lovers too! Ill, to example ill, Would from my forehead wipe a perjured note; For none offend where all alike do dote.
lines: [Reads]
On a day--alack the day!--
Love, whose month is ever May,
Spied a blossom passing fair
Playing in the wanton air:
Through the velvet leaves the wind,
All unseen, can passage find;
That the lover, sick to death,
Wish himself the heaven's breath.
Air, quoth he, thy cheeks may blow;
Air, would I might triumph so!
But, alack, my hand is sworn
Ne'er to pluck thee from thy thorn;
Vow, alack, for youth unmeet,
Youth so apt to pluck a sweet!
Do not call it sin in me,
That I am forsworn for thee;
Thou for whom Jove would swear
Juno but an Ethiope were;
And deny himself for Jove,
Turning mortal for thy love.
This will I send, and something else more plain,
That shall express my true love's fasting pain.
O, would the king, Biron, and Longaville,
Were lovers too! Ill, to example ill,
Would from my forehead wipe a perjured note;
For none offend where all alike do dote.

speeches.speech: [Advancing] Dumain, thy love is far from charity. You may look pale, but I should blush, I know, To be o'erheard and taken napping so.
lines: [Advancing] Dumain, thy love is far from charity.
You may look pale, but I should blush, I know,
To be o'erheard and taken napping so.

speeches.speech: [Advancing] Come, sir, you blush; as his your case is such; You chide at him, offending twice as much; You do not love Maria; Longaville Did never sonnet for her sake compile, Nor never lay his wreathed arms athwart His loving bosom to keep down his heart. I have been closely shrouded in this bush And mark'd you both and for you both did blush: I heard your guilty rhymes, observed your fashion, Saw sighs reek from you, noted well your passion: Ay me! says one; O Jove! the other cries; One, her hairs were gold, crystal the other's eyes: [To LONGAVILLE] You would for paradise break faith, and troth; [To DUMAIN] And Jove, for your love, would infringe an oath. What will Biron say when that he shall hear Faith so infringed, which such zeal did swear? How will he scorn! how will he spend his wit! How will he triumph, leap and laugh at it! For all the wealth that ever I did see, I would not have him know so much by me.
lines: [Advancing] Come, sir, you blush; as his your case is such;
You chide at him, offending twice as much;
You do not love Maria; Longaville
Did never sonnet for her sake compile,
Nor never lay his wreathed arms athwart
His loving bosom to keep down his heart.
I have been closely shrouded in this bush
And mark'd you both and for you both did blush:
I heard your guilty rhymes, observed your fashion,
Saw sighs reek from you, noted well your passion:
Ay me! says one; O Jove! the other cries;
One, her hairs were gold, crystal the other's eyes:
[To LONGAVILLE]
You would for paradise break faith, and troth;
[To DUMAIN]
And Jove, for your love, would infringe an oath.
What will Biron say when that he shall hear
Faith so infringed, which such zeal did swear?
How will he scorn! how will he spend his wit!
How will he triumph, leap and laugh at it!
For all the wealth that ever I did see,
I would not have him know so much by me.

speeches.speech: Now step I forth to whip hypocrisy. [Advancing] Ah, good my liege, I pray thee, pardon me! Good heart, what grace hast thou, thus to reprove These worms for loving, that art most in love? Your eyes do make no coaches; in your tears There is no certain princess that appears; You'll not be perjured, 'tis a hateful thing; Tush, none but minstrels like of sonneting! But are you not ashamed? nay, are you not, All three of you, to be thus much o'ershot? You found his mote; the king your mote did see; But I a beam do find in each of three. O, what a scene of foolery have I seen, Of sighs, of groans, of sorrow and of teen! O me, with what strict patience have I sat, To see a king transformed to a gnat! To see great Hercules whipping a gig, And profound Solomon to tune a jig, And Nestor play at push-pin with the boys, And critic Timon laugh at idle toys! Where lies thy grief, O, tell me, good Dumain? And gentle Longaville, where lies thy pain? And where my liege's? all about the breast: A caudle, ho!
lines: Now step I forth to whip hypocrisy.
[Advancing]
Ah, good my liege, I pray thee, pardon me!
Good heart, what grace hast thou, thus to reprove
These worms for loving, that art most in love?
Your eyes do make no coaches; in your tears
There is no certain princess that appears;
You'll not be perjured, 'tis a hateful thing;
Tush, none but minstrels like of sonneting!
But are you not ashamed? nay, are you not,
All three of you, to be thus much o'ershot?
You found his mote; the king your mote did see;
But I a beam do find in each of three.
O, what a scene of foolery have I seen,
Of sighs, of groans, of sorrow and of teen!
O me, with what strict patience have I sat,
To see a king transformed to a gnat!
To see great Hercules whipping a gig,
And profound Solomon to tune a jig,
And Nestor play at push-pin with the boys,
And critic Timon laugh at idle toys!
Where lies thy grief, O, tell me, good Dumain?
And gentle Longaville, where lies thy pain?
And where my liege's? all about the breast:
A caudle, ho!

speeches.speech: Too bitter is thy jest. Are we betray'd thus to thy over-view?
lines: Too bitter is thy jest.
Are we betray'd thus to thy over-view?

speeches.speech: Not you to me, but I betray'd by you: I, that am honest; I, that hold it sin To break the vow I am engaged in; I am betray'd, by keeping company With men like men of inconstancy. When shall you see me write a thing in rhyme? Or groan for love? or spend a minute's time In pruning me? When shall you hear that I Will praise a hand, a foot, a face, an eye, A gait, a state, a brow, a breast, a waist, A leg, a limb?
lines: Not you to me, but I betray'd by you:
I, that am honest; I, that hold it sin
To break the vow I am engaged in;
I am betray'd, by keeping company
With men like men of inconstancy.
When shall you see me write a thing in rhyme?
Or groan for love? or spend a minute's time
In pruning me? When shall you hear that I
Will praise a hand, a foot, a face, an eye,
A gait, a state, a brow, a breast, a waist,
A leg, a limb?

speeches.speech: Soft! whither away so fast? A true man or a thief that gallops so?
lines: Soft! whither away so fast?
A true man or a thief that gallops so?

speeches.speech: I post from love: good lover, let me go.
lines: I post from love: good lover, let me go.

speeches.speech: God bless the king!
lines: God bless the king!

speeches.speech: What present hast thou there?
lines: What present hast thou there?

speeches.speech: Some certain treason.
lines: Some certain treason.

speeches.speech: What makes treason here?
lines: What makes treason here?

speeches.speech: Nay, it makes nothing, sir.
lines: Nay, it makes nothing, sir.

speeches.speech: If it mar nothing neither, The treason and you go in peace away together.
lines: If it mar nothing neither,
The treason and you go in peace away together.

speeches.speech: I beseech your grace, let this letter be read: Our parson misdoubts it; 'twas treason, he said.
lines: I beseech your grace, let this letter be read:
Our parson misdoubts it; 'twas treason, he said.

speeches.speech: Biron, read it over. [Giving him the paper] Where hadst thou it?
lines: Biron, read it over.
[Giving him the paper]
Where hadst thou it?

speeches.speech: Of Costard.
lines: Of Costard.

speeches.speech: Where hadst thou it?
lines: Where hadst thou it?

speeches.speech: Of Dun Adramadio, Dun Adramadio.
lines: Of Dun Adramadio, Dun Adramadio.

speeches.speech: How now! what is in you? why dost thou tear it?
lines: How now! what is in you? why dost thou tear it?

speeches.speech: A toy, my liege, a toy: your grace needs not fear it.
lines: A toy, my liege, a toy: your grace needs not fear it.

lines: It did move him to passion, and therefore let's hear it.

lines: It is Biron's writing, and here is his name.

speeches.speech: [To COSTARD] Ah, you whoreson loggerhead! you were born to do me shame. Guilty, my lord, guilty! I confess, I confess.
lines: [To COSTARD] Ah, you whoreson loggerhead! you were

Guilty, my lord, guilty! I confess, I confess.

speeches.speech: What?
lines: What?

speeches.speech: That you three fools lack'd me fool to make up the mess: He, he, and you, and you, my liege, and I, Are pick-purses in love, and we deserve to die. O, dismiss this audience, and I shall tell you more.
lines: That you three fools lack'd me fool to make up the mess:
He, he, and you, and you, my liege, and I,
