O, dismiss this audience, and I shall tell you more.

speeches.speech: Now the number is even.
lines: Now the number is even.

speeches.speech: True, true; we are four. Will these turtles be gone?
lines: True, true; we are four.
Will these turtles be gone?

speeches.speech: Hence, sirs; away!
lines: Hence, sirs; away!

speeches.speech: Walk aside the true folk, and let the traitors stay.
lines: Walk aside the true folk, and let the traitors stay.

speeches.speech: Sweet lords, sweet lovers, O, let us embrace! As true we are as flesh and blood can be: The sea will ebb and flow, heaven show his face; Young blood doth not obey an old decree: We cannot cross the cause why we were born; Therefore of all hands must we be forsworn.
lines: Sweet lords, sweet lovers, O, let us embrace!
As true we are as flesh and blood can be:
The sea will ebb and flow, heaven show his face;
Young blood doth not obey an old decree:
We cannot cross the cause why we were born;
Therefore of all hands must we be forsworn.

speeches.speech: What, did these rent lines show some love of thine?
lines: What, did these rent lines show some love of thine?

speeches.speech: Did they, quoth you? Who sees the heavenly Rosaline, That, like a rude and savage man of Inde, At the first opening of the gorgeous east, Bows not his vassal head and strucken blind Kisses the base ground with obedient breast? What peremptory eagle-sighted eye Dares look upon the heaven of her brow, That is not blinded by her majesty?
lines: Did they, quoth you? Who sees the heavenly Rosaline,
That, like a rude and savage man of Inde,
At the first opening of the gorgeous east,
Bows not his vassal head and strucken blind
Kisses the base ground with obedient breast?
What peremptory eagle-sighted eye
Dares look upon the heaven of her brow,
That is not blinded by her majesty?

speeches.speech: What zeal, what fury hath inspired thee now? My love, her mistress, is a gracious moon; She an attending star, scarce seen a light.
lines: What zeal, what fury hath inspired thee now?
My love, her mistress, is a gracious moon;
She an attending star, scarce seen a light.

speeches.speech: My eyes are then no eyes, nor I Biron: O, but for my love, day would turn to night! Of all complexions the cull'd sovereignty Do meet, as at a fair, in her fair cheek, Where several worthies make one dignity, Where nothing wants that want itself doth seek. Lend me the flourish of all gentle tongues,-- Fie, painted rhetoric! O, she needs it not: To things of sale a seller's praise belongs, She passes praise; then praise too short doth blot. A wither'd hermit, five-score winters worn, Might shake off fifty, looking in her eye: Beauty doth varnish age, as if new-born, And gives the crutch the cradle's infancy: O, 'tis the sun that maketh all things shine.
lines: My eyes are then no eyes, nor I Biron:
O, but for my love, day would turn to night!
Of all complexions the cull'd sovereignty
Do meet, as at a fair, in her fair cheek,
Where several worthies make one dignity,
Where nothing wants that want itself doth seek.
Lend me the flourish of all gentle tongues,--
Fie, painted rhetoric! O, she needs it not:
To things of sale a seller's praise belongs,
She passes praise; then praise too short doth blot.
A wither'd hermit, five-score winters worn,
Might shake off fifty, looking in her eye:
Beauty doth varnish age, as if new-born,
And gives the crutch the cradle's infancy:
O, 'tis the sun that maketh all things shine.

speeches.speech: By heaven, thy love is black as ebony.
lines: By heaven, thy love is black as ebony.

speeches.speech: Is ebony like her? O wood divine! A wife of such wood were felicity. O, who can give an oath? where is a book? That I may swear beauty doth beauty lack, If that she learn not of her eye to look: No face is fair that is not full so black.
lines: Is ebony like her? O wood divine!
A wife of such wood were felicity.
O, who can give an oath? where is a book?
That I may swear beauty doth beauty lack,
If that she learn not of her eye to look:
No face is fair that is not full so black.

speeches.speech: O paradox! Black is the badge of hell, The hue of dungeons and the suit of night; And beauty's crest becomes the heavens well.
lines: O paradox! Black is the badge of hell,
The hue of dungeons and the suit of night;
And beauty's crest becomes the heavens well.

speeches.speech: Devils soonest tempt, resembling spirits of light. O, if in black my lady's brows be deck'd, It mourns that painting and usurping hair Should ravish doters with a false aspect; And therefore is she born to make black fair. Her favour turns the fashion of the days, For native blood is counted painting now; And therefore red, that would avoid dispraise, Paints itself black, to imitate her brow.
lines: Devils soonest tempt, resembling spirits of light.
O, if in black my lady's brows be deck'd,
It mourns that painting and usurping hair
Should ravish doters with a false aspect;
And therefore is she born to make black fair.
Her favour turns the fashion of the days,
For native blood is counted painting now;
And therefore red, that would avoid dispraise,
Paints itself black, to imitate her brow.

speeches.speech: To look like her are chimney-sweepers black.
lines: To look like her are chimney-sweepers black.

speeches.speech: And since her time are colliers counted bright.
lines: And since her time are colliers counted bright.

speeches.speech: And Ethiopes of their sweet complexion crack.
lines: And Ethiopes of their sweet complexion crack.

speeches.speech: Dark needs no candles now, for dark is light.
lines: Dark needs no candles now, for dark is light.

speeches.speech: Your mistresses dare never come in rain, For fear their colours should be wash'd away.
lines: Your mistresses dare never come in rain,
For fear their colours should be wash'd away.

speeches.speech: 'Twere good, yours did; for, sir, to tell you plain, I'll find a fairer face not wash'd to-day.
lines: 'Twere good, yours did; for, sir, to tell you plain,
I'll find a fairer face not wash'd to-day.

speeches.speech: I'll prove her fair, or talk till doomsday here.
lines: I'll prove her fair, or talk till doomsday here.

speeches.speech: No devil will fright thee then so much as she.
lines: No devil will fright thee then so much as she.

speeches.speech: I never knew man hold vile stuff so dear.
lines: I never knew man hold vile stuff so dear.

speeches.speech: Look, here's thy love: my foot and her face see.
lines: Look, here's thy love: my foot and her face see.

speeches.speech: O, if the streets were paved with thine eyes, Her feet were much too dainty for such tread!
lines: O, if the streets were paved with thine eyes,
Her feet were much too dainty for such tread!

speeches.speech: O, vile! then, as she goes, what upward lies The street should see as she walk'd overhead.
lines: O, vile! then, as she goes, what upward lies
The street should see as she walk'd overhead.

speeches.speech: But what of this? are we not all in love?
lines: But what of this? are we not all in love?

speeches.speech: Nothing so sure; and thereby all forsworn.
lines: Nothing so sure; and thereby all forsworn.

speeches.speech: Then leave this chat; and, good Biron, now prove Our loving lawful, and our faith not torn.
lines: Then leave this chat; and, good Biron, now prove
Our loving lawful, and our faith not torn.

speeches.speech: Ay, marry, there; some flattery for this evil.
lines: Ay, marry, there; some flattery for this evil.

speeches.speech: O, some authority how to proceed; Some tricks, some quillets, how to cheat the devil.
lines: O, some authority how to proceed;
Some tricks, some quillets, how to cheat the devil.

speeches.speech: Some salve for perjury.
lines: Some salve for perjury.

speeches.speech: 'Tis more than need. Have at you, then, affection's men at arms. Consider what you first did swear unto, To fast, to study, and to see no woman; Flat treason 'gainst the kingly state of youth. Say, can you fast? your stomachs are too young; And abstinence engenders maladies. And where that you have vow'd to study, lords, In that each of you have forsworn his book, Can you still dream and pore and thereon look? For when would you, my lord, or you, or you, Have found the ground of study's excellence Without the beauty of a woman's face? [From women's eyes this doctrine I derive;] They are the ground, the books, the academes From whence doth spring the true Promethean fire] Why, universal plodding poisons up The nimble spirits in the arteries, As motion and long-during action tires The sinewy vigour of the traveller. Now, for not looking on a woman's face, You have in that forsworn the use of eyes And study too, the causer of your vow; For where is any author in the world Teaches such beauty as a woman's eye? Learning is but an adjunct to ourself And where we are our learning likewise is: Then when ourselves we see in ladies' eyes, Do we not likewise see our learning there? O, we have made a vow to study, lords, And in that vow we have forsworn our books. For when would you, my liege, or you, or you, In leaden contemplation have found out Such fiery numbers as the prompting eyes Of beauty's tutors have enrich'd you with? Other slow arts entirely keep the brain; And therefore, finding barren practisers, Scarce show a harvest of their heavy toil: But love, first learned in a lady's eyes, Lives not alone immured in the brain; But, with the motion of all elements, Courses as swift as thought in every power, And gives to every power a double power, Above their functions and their offices. It adds a precious seeing to the eye; A lover's eyes will gaze an eagle blind; A lover's ear will hear the lowest sound, When the suspicious head of theft is stopp'd: Love's feeling is more soft and sensible Than are the tender horns of cockl'd snails; Love's tongue proves dainty Bacchus gross in taste: For valour, is not Love a Hercules, Still climbing trees in the Hesperides? Subtle as Sphinx; as sweet and musical As bright Apollo's lute, strung with his hair: And when Love speaks, the voice of all the gods Makes heaven drowsy with the harmony. Never durst poet touch a pen to write Until his ink were temper'd with Love's sighs; O, then his lines would ravish savage ears And plant in tyrants mild humility. From women's eyes this doctrine I derive: They sparkle still the right Promethean fire; They are the books, the arts, the academes, That show, contain and nourish all the world: Else none at all in ought proves excellent. Then fools you were these women to forswear, Or keeping what is sworn, you will prove fools. For wisdom's sake, a word that all men love, Or for love's sake, a word that loves all men, Or for men's sake, the authors of these women, Or women's sake, by whom we men are men, Let us once lose our oaths to find ourselves, Or else we lose ourselves to keep our oaths. It is religion to be thus forsworn, For charity itself fulfills the law, And who can sever love from charity?
lines: 'Tis more than need.
Have at you, then, affection's men at arms.
Consider what you first did swear unto,
To fast, to study, and to see no woman;
Flat treason 'gainst the kingly state of youth.
Say, can you fast? your stomachs are too young;
And abstinence engenders maladies.
And where that you have vow'd to study, lords,
In that each of you have forsworn his book,
Can you still dream and pore and thereon look?
For when would you, my lord, or you, or you,
Have found the ground of study's excellence
Without the beauty of a woman's face?
[From women's eyes this doctrine I derive;]
They are the ground, the books, the academes
From whence doth spring the true Promethean fire]
Why, universal plodding poisons up
The nimble spirits in the arteries,
As motion and long-during action tires
The sinewy vigour of the traveller.
Now, for not looking on a woman's face,
You have in that forsworn the use of eyes
And study too, the causer of your vow;
For where is any author in the world
Teaches such beauty as a woman's eye?
Learning is but an adjunct to ourself
And where we are our learning likewise is:
Then when ourselves we see in ladies' eyes,
Do we not likewise see our learning there?
O, we have made a vow to study, lords,
And in that vow we have forsworn our books.
For when would you, my liege, or you, or you,
In leaden contemplation have found out
Such fiery numbers as the prompting eyes
Of beauty's tutors have enrich'd you with?
Other slow arts entirely keep the brain;
And therefore, finding barren practisers,
Scarce show a harvest of their heavy toil:
But love, first learned in a lady's eyes,
Lives not alone immured in the brain;
But, with the motion of all elements,
Courses as swift as thought in every power,
And gives to every power a double power,
Above their functions and their offices.
It adds a precious seeing to the eye;
A lover's eyes will gaze an eagle blind;
A lover's ear will hear the lowest sound,
When the suspicious head of theft is stopp'd:
Love's feeling is more soft and sensible
Than are the tender horns of cockl'd snails;
Love's tongue proves dainty Bacchus gross in taste:
For valour, is not Love a Hercules,
Still climbing trees in the Hesperides?
Subtle as Sphinx; as sweet and musical
As bright Apollo's lute, strung with his hair:
And when Love speaks, the voice of all the gods
Makes heaven drowsy with the harmony.
Never durst poet touch a pen to write
Until his ink were temper'd with Love's sighs;
O, then his lines would ravish savage ears
And plant in tyrants mild humility.
From women's eyes this doctrine I derive:
They sparkle still the right Promethean fire;
They are the books, the arts, the academes,
That show, contain and nourish all the world:
Else none at all in ought proves excellent.
Then fools you were these women to forswear,
Or keeping what is sworn, you will prove fools.
For wisdom's sake, a word that all men love,
Or for love's sake, a word that loves all men,
Or for men's sake, the authors of these women,
Or women's sake, by whom we men are men,
Let us once lose our oaths to find ourselves,
Or else we lose ourselves to keep our oaths.
It is religion to be thus forsworn,
For charity itself fulfills the law,
And who can sever love from charity?

speeches.speech: Saint Cupid, then! and, soldiers, to the field!
lines: Saint Cupid, then! and, soldiers, to the field!

speeches.speech: Advance your standards, and upon them, lords; Pell-mell, down with them! but be first advised, In conflict that you get the sun of them.
lines: Advance your standards, and upon them, lords;
Pell-mell, down with them! but be first advised,
In conflict that you get the sun of them.

speeches.speech: Now to plain-dealing; lay these glozes by: Shall we resolve to woo these girls of France?
lines: Now to plain-dealing; lay these glozes by:
Shall we resolve to woo these girls of France?

speeches.speech: And win them too: therefore let us devise Some entertainment for them in their tents.
lines: And win them too: therefore let us devise
Some entertainment for them in their tents.

speeches.speech: First, from the park let us conduct them thither; Then homeward every man attach the hand Of his fair mistress: in the afternoon We will with some strange pastime solace them, Such as the shortness of the time can shape; For revels, dances, masks and merry hours Forerun fair Love, strewing her way with flowers.
lines: First, from the park let us conduct them thither;
Then homeward every man attach the hand
Of his fair mistress: in the afternoon
We will with some strange pastime solace them,
Such as the shortness of the time can shape;
For revels, dances, masks and merry hours
Forerun fair Love, strewing her way with flowers.

speeches.speech: Away, away! no time shall be omitted That will betime, and may by us be fitted.
lines: Away, away! no time shall be omitted
That will betime, and may by us be fitted.

speeches.speech: Allons! allons! Sow'd cockle reap'd no corn; And justice always whirls in equal measure: Light wenches may prove plagues to men forsworn; If so, our copper buys no better treasure.
lines: Allons! allons! Sow'd cockle reap'd no corn;
And justice always whirls in equal measure:
Light wenches may prove plagues to men forsworn;
If so, our copper buys no better treasure.

speeches.speech: Satis quod sufficit.
lines: Satis quod sufficit.

speeches.speech: I praise God for you, sir: your reasons at dinner have been sharp and sententious; pleasant without scurrility, witty without affection, audacious without impudency, learned without opinion, and strange with- out heresy. I did converse this quondam day with a companion of the king's, who is intituled, nomi- nated, or called, Don Adriano de Armado.
lines: I praise God for you, sir: your reasons at dinner
have been sharp and sententious; pleasant without
scurrility, witty without affection, audacious without
impudency, learned without opinion, and strange with-
out heresy. I did converse this quondam day with
a companion of the king's, who is intituled, nomi-
nated, or called, Don Adriano de Armado.

speeches.speech: Novi hominem tanquam te: his humour is lofty, his discourse peremptory, his tongue filed, his eye ambitious, his gait majestical, and his general behavior vain, ridiculous, and thrasonical. He is too picked, too spruce, too affected, too odd, as it were, too peregrinate, as I may call it.
lines: Novi hominem tanquam te: his humour is lofty, his
discourse peremptory, his tongue filed, his eye
ambitious, his gait majestical, and his general
behavior vain, ridiculous, and thrasonical. He is
too picked, too spruce, too affected, too odd, as it
were, too peregrinate, as I may call it.

speeches.speech: A most singular and choice epithet.
lines: A most singular and choice epithet.

speeches.speech: He draweth out the thread of his verbosity finer than the staple of his argument. I abhor such fanatical phantasimes, such insociable and point-devise companions; such rackers of orthography, as to speak dout, fine, when he should say doubt; det, when he should pronounce debt,--d, e, b, t, not d, e, t: he clepeth a calf, cauf; half, hauf; neighbour vocatur nebor; neigh abbreviated ne. This is abhominable,--which he would call abbominable: it insinuateth me of insanie: anne intelligis, domine? to make frantic, lunatic.

than the staple of his argument. I abhor such

speeches.speech: Laus Deo, bene intelligo.
lines: Laus Deo, bene intelligo.

speeches.speech: Bon, bon, fort bon, Priscian! a little scratch'd, 'twill serve.
lines: Bon, bon, fort bon, Priscian! a little scratch'd,
'twill serve.

speeches.speech: Videsne quis venit?
lines: Videsne quis venit?

speeches.speech: Video, et gaudeo.
lines: Video, et gaudeo.

speeches.speech: Chirrah!
lines: Chirrah!

speeches.speech: Quare chirrah, not sirrah?
lines: Quare chirrah, not sirrah?

speeches.speech: Men of peace, well encountered.
lines: Men of peace, well encountered.

speeches.speech: Most military sir, salutation.
lines: Most military sir, salutation.

speeches.speech: [Aside to COSTARD] They have been at a great feast of languages, and stolen the scraps.
lines: [Aside to COSTARD] They have been at a great feast
of languages, and stolen the scraps.

speeches.speech: O, they have lived long on the alms-basket of words. I marvel thy master hath not eaten thee for a word; for thou art not so long by the head as honorificabilitudinitatibus: thou art easier swallowed than a flap-dragon.
lines: O, they have lived long on the alms-basket of words.
I marvel thy master hath not eaten thee for a word;
for thou art not so long by the head as
honorificabilitudinitatibus: thou art easier
swallowed than a flap-dragon.

speeches.speech: Peace! the peal begins.
lines: Peace! the peal begins.

speeches.speech: [To HOLOFERNES] Monsieur, are you not lettered?
lines: [To HOLOFERNES] Monsieur, are you not lettered?

speeches.speech: Yes, yes; he teaches boys the hornbook. What is a, b, spelt backward, with the horn on his head?
lines: Yes, yes; he teaches boys the hornbook. What is a,
b, spelt backward, with the horn on his head?

speeches.speech: Ba, pueritia, with a horn added.
lines: Ba, pueritia, with a horn added.

speeches.speech: Ba, most silly sheep with a horn. You hear his learning.
lines: Ba, most silly sheep with a horn. You hear his learning.

speeches.speech: Quis, quis, thou consonant?
lines: Quis, quis, thou consonant?

speeches.speech: The third of the five vowels, if you repeat them; or the fifth, if I.
lines: The third of the five vowels, if you repeat them; or
the fifth, if I.

speeches.speech: I will repeat them,--a, e, i,--
lines: I will repeat them,--a, e, i,--

speeches.speech: The sheep: the other two concludes it,--o, u.
lines: The sheep: the other two concludes it,--o, u.

speeches.speech: Now, by the salt wave of the Mediterraneum, a sweet touch, a quick venue of wit! snip, snap, quick and home! it rejoiceth my intellect: true wit!
lines: Now, by the salt wave of the Mediterraneum, a sweet
touch, a quick venue of wit! snip, snap, quick and
home! it rejoiceth my intellect: true wit!

speeches.speech: Offered by a child to an old man; which is wit-old.
lines: Offered by a child to an old man; which is wit-old.

speeches.speech: What is the figure? what is the figure?
lines: What is the figure? what is the figure?

speeches.speech: Horns.
lines: Horns.

speeches.speech: Thou disputest like an infant: go, whip thy gig.
lines: Thou disputest like an infant: go, whip thy gig.

speeches.speech: Lend me your horn to make one, and I will whip about your infamy circum circa,--a gig of a cuckold's horn.
lines: Lend me your horn to make one, and I will whip about
your infamy circum circa,--a gig of a cuckold's horn.

speeches.speech: An I had but one penny in the world, thou shouldst have it to buy gingerbread: hold, there is the very remuneration I had of thy master, thou halfpenny purse of wit, thou pigeon-egg of discretion. O, an the heavens were so pleased that thou wert but my bastard, what a joyful father wouldst thou make me! Go to; thou hast it ad dunghill, at the fingers' ends, as they say.

speeches.speech: O, I smell false Latin; dunghill for unguem.
lines: O, I smell false Latin; dunghill for unguem.

speeches.speech: Arts-man, preambulate, we will be singled from the barbarous. Do you not educate youth at the charge-house on the top of the mountain?
lines: Arts-man, preambulate, we will be singled from the
barbarous. Do you not educate youth at the
charge-house on the top of the mountain?

speeches.speech: Or mons, the hill.
lines: Or mons, the hill.

speeches.speech: At your sweet pleasure, for the mountain.
lines: At your sweet pleasure, for the mountain.

speeches.speech: I do, sans question.
lines: I do, sans question.

speeches.speech: Sir, it is the king's most sweet pleasure and affection to congratulate the princess at her pavilion in the posteriors of this day, which the rude multitude call the afternoon.
lines: Sir, it is the king's most sweet pleasure and
affection to congratulate the princess at her
pavilion in the posteriors of this day, which the
rude multitude call the afternoon.

speeches.speech: The posterior of the day, most generous sir, is liable, congruent and measurable for the afternoon: the word is well culled, chose, sweet and apt, I do assure you, sir, I do assure.
lines: The posterior of the day, most generous sir, is
liable, congruent and measurable for the afternoon:
the word is well culled, chose, sweet and apt, I do
assure you, sir, I do assure.

speeches.speech: Sir, the king is a noble gentleman, and my familiar, I do assure ye, very good friend: for what is inward between us, let it pass. I do beseech thee, remember thy courtesy; I beseech thee, apparel thy head: and among other important and most serious designs, and of great import indeed, too, but let that pass: for I must tell thee, it will please his grace, by the world, sometime to lean upon my poor shoulder, and with his royal finger, thus, dally with my excrement, with my mustachio; but, sweet heart, let that pass. By the world, I recount no fable: some certain special honours it pleaseth his greatness to impart to Armado, a soldier, a man of travel, that hath seen the world; but let that pass. The very all of all is,--but, sweet heart, I do implore secrecy,--that the king would have me present the princess, sweet chuck, with some delightful ostentation, or show, or pageant, or antique, or firework. Now, understanding that the curate and your sweet self are good at such eruptions and sudden breaking out of mirth, as it were, I have acquainted you withal, to the end to crave your assistance.

speeches.speech: Sir, you shall present before her the Nine Worthies. Sir, as concerning some entertainment of time, some show in the posterior of this day, to be rendered by our assistants, at the king's command, and this most gallant, illustrate, and learned gentleman, before the princess; I say none so fit as to present the Nine Worthies.

speeches.speech: Where will you find men worthy enough to present them?
lines: Where will you find men worthy enough to present them?

speeches.speech: Joshua, yourself; myself and this gallant gentleman, Judas Maccabaeus; this swain, because of his great limb or joint, shall pass Pompey the Great; the page, Hercules,--
lines: Joshua, yourself; myself and this gallant gentleman,
Judas Maccabaeus; this swain, because of his great
limb or joint, shall pass Pompey the Great; the
page, Hercules,--

speeches.speech: Pardon, sir; error: he is not quantity enough for that Worthy's thumb: he is not so big as the end of his club.
lines: Pardon, sir; error: he is not quantity enough for
that Worthy's thumb: he is not so big as the end of his club.

speeches.speech: Shall I have audience? he shall present Hercules in minority: his enter and exit shall be strangling a snake; and I will have an apology for that purpose.
lines: Shall I have audience? he shall present Hercules in
minority: his enter and exit shall be strangling a
snake; and I will have an apology for that purpose.

speeches.speech: An excellent device! so, if any of the audience hiss, you may cry 'Well done, Hercules! now thou crushest the snake!' that is the way to make an offence gracious, though few have the grace to do it.
lines: An excellent device! so, if any of the audience
hiss, you may cry 'Well done, Hercules! now thou
crushest the snake!' that is the way to make an
offence gracious, though few have the grace to do it.

speeches.speech: For the rest of the Worthies?--
lines: For the rest of the Worthies?--

speeches.speech: I will play three myself.
lines: I will play three myself.

speeches.speech: Thrice-worthy gentleman!
lines: Thrice-worthy gentleman!

speeches.speech: Shall I tell you a thing?
lines: Shall I tell you a thing?

speeches.speech: We attend.
lines: We attend.

speeches.speech: We will have, if this fadge not, an antique. I beseech you, follow.
lines: We will have, if this fadge not, an antique. I
beseech you, follow.

speeches.speech: Via, goodman Dull! thou hast spoken no word all this while.
lines: Via, goodman Dull! thou hast spoken no word all this while.

speeches.speech: Nor understood none neither, sir.
lines: Nor understood none neither, sir.

speeches.speech: Allons! we will employ thee.
lines: Allons! we will employ thee.

speeches.speech: I'll make one in a dance, or so; or I will play On the tabour to the Worthies, and let them dance the hay.
lines: I'll make one in a dance, or so; or I will play
On the tabour to the Worthies, and let them dance the hay.

speeches.speech: Most dull, honest Dull! To our sport, away!
lines: Most dull, honest Dull! To our sport, away!

speeches.speech: Sweet hearts, we shall be rich ere we depart, If fairings come thus plentifully in: A lady wall'd about with diamonds! Look you what I have from the loving king.
lines: Sweet hearts, we shall be rich ere we depart,
If fairings come thus plentifully in:
A lady wall'd about with diamonds!
Look you what I have from the loving king.

speeches.speech: Madame, came nothing else along with that?
lines: Madame, came nothing else along with that?

speeches.speech: Nothing but this! yes, as much love in rhyme As would be cramm'd up in a sheet of paper, Writ o' both sides the leaf, margent and all, That he was fain to seal on Cupid's name.
lines: Nothing but this! yes, as much love in rhyme
As would be cramm'd up in a sheet of paper,
Writ o' both sides the leaf, margent and all,
That he was fain to seal on Cupid's name.

speeches.speech: That was the way to make his godhead wax, For he hath been five thousand years a boy.
lines: That was the way to make his godhead wax,
For he hath been five thousand years a boy.

speeches.speech: Ay, and a shrewd unhappy gallows too.
lines: Ay, and a shrewd unhappy gallows too.

speeches.speech: You'll ne'er be friends with him; a' kill'd your sister.
lines: You'll ne'er be friends with him; a' kill'd your sister.

speeches.speech: He made her melancholy, sad, and heavy; And so she died: had she been light, like you, Of such a merry, nimble, stirring spirit, She might ha' been a grandam ere she died: And so may you; for a light heart lives long.
lines: He made her melancholy, sad, and heavy;
And so she died: had she been light, like you,
Of such a merry, nimble, stirring spirit,
She might ha' been a grandam ere she died:
And so may you; for a light heart lives long.

speeches.speech: What's your dark meaning, mouse, of this light word?
lines: What's your dark meaning, mouse, of this light word?

speeches.speech: A light condition in a beauty dark.
lines: A light condition in a beauty dark.

speeches.speech: We need more light to find your meaning out.
lines: We need more light to find your meaning out.

speeches.speech: You'll mar the light by taking it in snuff; Therefore I'll darkly end the argument.
lines: You'll mar the light by taking it in snuff;
Therefore I'll darkly end the argument.

speeches.speech: Look what you do, you do it still i' the dark.
lines: Look what you do, you do it still i' the dark.

speeches.speech: So do not you, for you are a light wench.
lines: So do not you, for you are a light wench.

speeches.speech: Indeed I weigh not you, and therefore light.
lines: Indeed I weigh not you, and therefore light.

speeches.speech: You weigh me not? O, that's you care not for me.
lines: You weigh me not? O, that's you care not for me.

speeches.speech: Great reason; for 'past cure is still past care.'
lines: Great reason; for 'past cure is still past care.'

speeches.speech: Well bandied both; a set of wit well play'd. But Rosaline, you have a favour too: Who sent it? and what is it?
lines: Well bandied both; a set of wit well play'd.
But Rosaline, you have a favour too:
Who sent it? and what is it?

speeches.speech: I would you knew: An if my face were but as fair as yours, My favour were as great; be witness this. Nay, I have verses too, I thank Biron: The numbers true; and, were the numbering too, I were the fairest goddess on the ground: I am compared to twenty thousand fairs. O, he hath drawn my picture in his letter!
lines: I would you knew:
An if my face were but as fair as yours,
My favour were as great; be witness this.
Nay, I have verses too, I thank Biron:
The numbers true; and, were the numbering too,
I were the fairest goddess on the ground:
I am compared to twenty thousand fairs.
O, he hath drawn my picture in his letter!

speeches.speech: Any thing like?
lines: Any thing like?

speeches.speech: Much in the letters; nothing in the praise.
lines: Much in the letters; nothing in the praise.

speeches.speech: Beauteous as ink; a good conclusion.
lines: Beauteous as ink; a good conclusion.

speeches.speech: Fair as a text B in a copy-book.
lines: Fair as a text B in a copy-book.

speeches.speech: 'Ware pencils, ho! let me not die your debtor, My red dominical, my golden letter: O, that your face were not so full of O's!
lines: 'Ware pencils, ho! let me not die your debtor,
My red dominical, my golden letter:
O, that your face were not so full of O's!

speeches.speech: A pox of that jest! and I beshrew all shrows.
lines: A pox of that jest! and I beshrew all shrows.

speeches.speech: But, Katharine, what was sent to you from fair Dumain?
lines: But, Katharine, what was sent to you from fair Dumain?

speeches.speech: Madam, this glove.
lines: Madam, this glove.

speeches.speech: Did he not send you twain?
lines: Did he not send you twain?

speeches.speech: Yes, madam, and moreover Some thousand verses of a faithful lover, A huge translation of hypocrisy, Vilely compiled, profound simplicity.
lines: Yes, madam, and moreover
Some thousand verses of a faithful lover,
A huge translation of hypocrisy,
Vilely compiled, profound simplicity.

speeches.speech: This and these pearls to me sent Longaville: The letter is too long by half a mile.
lines: This and these pearls to me sent Longaville:
The letter is too long by half a mile.

speeches.speech: I think no less. Dost thou not wish in heart The chain were longer and the letter short?
lines: I think no less. Dost thou not wish in heart
The chain were longer and the letter short?

speeches.speech: Ay, or I would these hands might never part.
lines: Ay, or I would these hands might never part.

speeches.speech: We are wise girls to mock our lovers so.
lines: We are wise girls to mock our lovers so.

speeches.speech: They are worse fools to purchase mocking so. That same Biron I'll torture ere I go: O that I knew he were but in by the week! How I would make him fawn and beg and seek And wait the season and observe the times And spend his prodigal wits in bootless rhymes And shape his service wholly to my hests And make him proud to make me proud that jests! So perttaunt-like would I o'ersway his state That he should be my fool and I his fate.
lines: They are worse fools to purchase mocking so.
That same Biron I'll torture ere I go:
O that I knew he were but in by the week!
How I would make him fawn and beg and seek
And wait the season and observe the times
And spend his prodigal wits in bootless rhymes
And shape his service wholly to my hests
And make him proud to make me proud that jests!
So perttaunt-like would I o'ersway his state
That he should be my fool and I his fate.

speeches.speech: None are so surely caught, when they are catch'd, As wit turn'd fool: folly, in wisdom hatch'd, Hath wisdom's warrant and the help of school And wit's own grace to grace a learned fool.
lines: None are so surely caught, when they are catch'd,
As wit turn'd fool: folly, in wisdom hatch'd,
Hath wisdom's warrant and the help of school
And wit's own grace to grace a learned fool.

speeches.speech: The blood of youth burns not with such excess As gravity's revolt to wantonness.
lines: The blood of youth burns not with such excess
As gravity's revolt to wantonness.

speeches.speech: Folly in fools bears not so strong a note As foolery in the wise, when wit doth dote; Since all the power thereof it doth apply To prove, by wit, worth in simplicity.
lines: Folly in fools bears not so strong a note
As foolery in the wise, when wit doth dote;
Since all the power thereof it doth apply
To prove, by wit, worth in simplicity.

speeches.speech: Here comes Boyet, and mirth is in his face.
lines: Here comes Boyet, and mirth is in his face.

speeches.speech: O, I am stabb'd with laughter! Where's her grace?
lines: O, I am stabb'd with laughter! Where's her grace?

speeches.speech: Thy news Boyet?
lines: Thy news Boyet?

speeches.speech: Prepare, madam, prepare! Arm, wenches, arm! encounters mounted are Against your peace: Love doth approach disguised, Armed in arguments; you'll be surprised: Muster your wits; stand in your own defence; Or hide your heads like cowards, and fly hence.
lines: Prepare, madam, prepare!
Arm, wenches, arm! encounters mounted are
Against your peace: Love doth approach disguised,
Armed in arguments; you'll be surprised:
Muster your wits; stand in your own defence;
Or hide your heads like cowards, and fly hence.

speeches.speech: Saint Denis to Saint Cupid! What are they That charge their breath against us? say, scout, say.
lines: Saint Denis to Saint Cupid! What are they
That charge their breath against us? say, scout, say.

speeches.speech: Under the cool shade of a sycamore I thought to close mine eyes some half an hour; When, lo! to interrupt my purposed rest, Toward that shade I might behold addrest The king and his companions: warily I stole into a neighbour thicket by, And overheard what you shall overhear, That, by and by, disguised they will be here. Their herald is a pretty knavish page, That well by heart hath conn'd his embassage: Action and accent did they teach him there; 'Thus must thou speak,' and 'thus thy body bear:' And ever and anon they made a doubt Presence majestical would put him out, 'For,' quoth the king, 'an angel shalt thou see; Yet fear not thou, but speak audaciously.' The boy replied, 'An angel is not evil; I should have fear'd her had she been a devil.' With that, all laugh'd and clapp'd him on the shoulder, Making the bold wag by their praises bolder: One rubb'd his elbow thus, and fleer'd and swore A better speech was never spoke before; Another, with his finger and his thumb, Cried, 'Via! we will do't, come what will come;' The third he caper'd, and cried, 'All goes well;' The fourth turn'd on the toe, and down he fell. With that, they all did tumble on the ground, With such a zealous laughter, so profound, That in this spleen ridiculous appears, To cheque their folly, passion's solemn tears.
lines: Under the cool shade of a sycamore
I thought to close mine eyes some half an hour;
When, lo! to interrupt my purposed rest,
Toward that shade I might behold addrest
The king and his companions: warily
I stole into a neighbour thicket by,
And overheard what you shall overhear,
That, by and by, disguised they will be here.
Their herald is a pretty knavish page,
That well by heart hath conn'd his embassage:
Action and accent did they teach him there;
'Thus must thou speak,' and 'thus thy body bear:'
And ever and anon they made a doubt
Presence majestical would put him out,
'For,' quoth the king, 'an angel shalt thou see;
Yet fear not thou, but speak audaciously.'
The boy replied, 'An angel is not evil;
I should have fear'd her had she been a devil.'
With that, all laugh'd and clapp'd him on the shoulder,
Making the bold wag by their praises bolder:
One rubb'd his elbow thus, and fleer'd and swore
A better speech was never spoke before;
Another, with his finger and his thumb,
Cried, 'Via! we will do't, come what will come;'
The third he caper'd, and cried, 'All goes well;'
The fourth turn'd on the toe, and down he fell.
With that, they all did tumble on the ground,
With such a zealous laughter, so profound,
That in this spleen ridiculous appears,
To cheque their folly, passion's solemn tears.

speeches.speech: But what, but what, come they to visit us?
lines: But what, but what, come they to visit us?

speeches.speech: They do, they do: and are apparell'd thus. Like Muscovites or Russians, as I guess. Their purpose is to parle, to court and dance; And every one his love-feat will advance Unto his several mistress, which they'll know By favours several which they did bestow.
lines: They do, they do: and are apparell'd thus.
Like Muscovites or Russians, as I guess.
Their purpose is to parle, to court and dance;
And every one his love-feat will advance
Unto his several mistress, which they'll know
By favours several which they did bestow.

speeches.speech: And will they so? the gallants shall be task'd; For, ladies, we shall every one be mask'd; And not a man of them shall have the grace, Despite of suit, to see a lady's face. Hold, Rosaline, this favour thou shalt wear, And then the king will court thee for his dear; Hold, take thou this, my sweet, and give me thine, So shall Biron take me for Rosaline. And change your favours too; so shall your loves Woo contrary, deceived by these removes.
lines: And will they so? the gallants shall be task'd;
For, ladies, we shall every one be mask'd;
And not a man of them shall have the grace,
Despite of suit, to see a lady's face.
Hold, Rosaline, this favour thou shalt wear,
And then the king will court thee for his dear;
Hold, take thou this, my sweet, and give me thine,
So shall Biron take me for Rosaline.
And change your favours too; so shall your loves
Woo contrary, deceived by these removes.

speeches.speech: Come on, then; wear the favours most in sight.
lines: Come on, then; wear the favours most in sight.

speeches.speech: But in this changing what is your intent?
lines: But in this changing what is your intent?

speeches.speech: The effect of my intent is to cross theirs: They do it but in mocking merriment; And mock for mock is only my intent. Their several counsels they unbosom shall To loves mistook, and so be mock'd withal Upon the next occasion that we meet, With visages displayed, to talk and greet.
lines: The effect of my intent is to cross theirs:
They do it but in mocking merriment;
And mock for mock is only my intent.
Their several counsels they unbosom shall
To loves mistook, and so be mock'd withal
Upon the next occasion that we meet,
With visages displayed, to talk and greet.

speeches.speech: But shall we dance, if they desire to't?
lines: But shall we dance, if they desire to't?

speeches.speech: No, to the death, we will not move a foot; Nor to their penn'd speech render we no grace, But while 'tis spoke each turn away her face.
lines: No, to the death, we will not move a foot;
Nor to their penn'd speech render we no grace,
But while 'tis spoke each turn away her face.

speeches.speech: Why, that contempt will kill the speaker's heart, And quite divorce his memory from his part.
lines: Why, that contempt will kill the speaker's heart,
And quite divorce his memory from his part.

speeches.speech: Therefore I do it; and I make no doubt The rest will ne'er come in, if he be out There's no such sport as sport by sport o'erthrown, To make theirs ours and ours none but our own: So shall we stay, mocking intended game, And they, well mock'd, depart away with shame.
lines: Therefore I do it; and I make no doubt
The rest will ne'er come in, if he be out
There's no such sport as sport by sport o'erthrown,
To make theirs ours and ours none but our own:
So shall we stay, mocking intended game,
And they, well mock'd, depart away with shame.

speeches.speech: The trumpet sounds: be mask'd; the maskers come. [The Ladies mask] [Enter Blackamoors with music; MOTH; FERDINAND,] BIRON, LONGAVILLE, and DUMAIN, in Russian habits, and masked]
lines: The trumpet sounds: be mask'd; the maskers come.
[The Ladies mask]
[Enter Blackamoors with music; MOTH; FERDINAND,]
BIRON, LONGAVILLE, and DUMAIN, in Russian habits,
and masked]

speeches.speech: All hail, the richest beauties on the earth!--
lines: All hail, the richest beauties on the earth!--

speeches.speech: Beauties no richer than rich taffeta.
lines: Beauties no richer than rich taffeta.

speeches.speech: A holy parcel of the fairest dames. [The Ladies turn their backs to him] That ever turn'd their--backs--to mortal views!
lines: A holy parcel of the fairest dames.
[The Ladies turn their backs to him]
That ever turn'd their--backs--to mortal views!

speeches.speech: [Aside to MOTH] Their eyes, villain, their eyes!
lines: [Aside to MOTH] Their eyes, villain, their eyes!

speeches.speech: That ever turn'd their eyes to mortal views!--Out--
lines: That ever turn'd their eyes to mortal views!--Out--

speeches.speech: True; out indeed.
lines: True; out indeed.

speeches.speech: Out of your favours, heavenly spirits, vouchsafe Not to behold--
lines: Out of your favours, heavenly spirits, vouchsafe
Not to behold--

speeches.speech: [Aside to MOTH] Once to behold, rogue.
lines: [Aside to MOTH] Once to behold, rogue.

speeches.speech: Once to behold with your sun-beamed eyes, --with your sun-beamed eyes--
lines: Once to behold with your sun-beamed eyes,
--with your sun-beamed eyes--

speeches.speech: They will not answer to that epithet; You were best call it 'daughter-beamed eyes.'
lines: They will not answer to that epithet;
You were best call it 'daughter-beamed eyes.'

speeches.speech: They do not mark me, and that brings me out.
lines: They do not mark me, and that brings me out.

speeches.speech: Is this your perfectness? be gone, you rogue!
lines: Is this your perfectness? be gone, you rogue!

speeches.speech: What would these strangers? know their minds, Boyet: If they do speak our language, 'tis our will: That some plain man recount their purposes Know what they would.
lines: What would these strangers? know their minds, Boyet:
If they do speak our language, 'tis our will:
That some plain man recount their purposes
Know what they would.

speeches.speech: What would you with the princess?
lines: What would you with the princess?

speeches.speech: Nothing but peace and gentle visitation.
lines: Nothing but peace and gentle visitation.

speeches.speech: What would they, say they?
lines: What would they, say they?

speeches.speech: Nothing but peace and gentle visitation.
lines: Nothing but peace and gentle visitation.

speeches.speech: Why, that they have; and bid them so be gone.
lines: Why, that they have; and bid them so be gone.

speeches.speech: She says, you have it, and you may be gone.
lines: She says, you have it, and you may be gone.

speeches.speech: Say to her, we have measured many miles To tread a measure with her on this grass.
lines: Say to her, we have measured many miles
To tread a measure with her on this grass.

speeches.speech: They say, that they have measured many a mile To tread a measure with you on this grass.
lines: They say, that they have measured many a mile
To tread a measure with you on this grass.

speeches.speech: It is not so. Ask them how many inches Is in one mile: if they have measured many, The measure then of one is easily told.
lines: It is not so. Ask them how many inches
Is in one mile: if they have measured many,
The measure then of one is easily told.

speeches.speech: If to come hither you have measured miles, And many miles, the princess bids you tell How many inches doth fill up one mile.
lines: If to come hither you have measured miles,
And many miles, the princess bids you tell
How many inches doth fill up one mile.

speeches.speech: Tell her, we measure them by weary steps.
lines: Tell her, we measure them by weary steps.

speeches.speech: She hears herself.
lines: She hears herself.

speeches.speech: How many weary steps, Of many weary miles you have o'ergone, Are number'd in the travel of one mile?
lines: How many weary steps,
Of many weary miles you have o'ergone,
Are number'd in the travel of one mile?

speeches.speech: We number nothing that we spend for you: Our duty is so rich, so infinite, That we may do it still without accompt. Vouchsafe to show the sunshine of your face, That we, like savages, may worship it.
lines: We number nothing that we spend for you:
Our duty is so rich, so infinite,
That we may do it still without accompt.
Vouchsafe to show the sunshine of your face,
That we, like savages, may worship it.

speeches.speech: My face is but a moon, and clouded too.
lines: My face is but a moon, and clouded too.

speeches.speech: Blessed are clouds, to do as such clouds do! Vouchsafe, bright moon, and these thy stars, to shine, Those clouds removed, upon our watery eyne.
lines: Blessed are clouds, to do as such clouds do!
Vouchsafe, bright moon, and these thy stars, to shine,
Those clouds removed, upon our watery eyne.

speeches.speech: O vain petitioner! beg a greater matter; Thou now request'st but moonshine in the water.
lines: O vain petitioner! beg a greater matter;
Thou now request'st but moonshine in the water.

speeches.speech: Then, in our measure do but vouchsafe one change. Thou bid'st me beg: this begging is not strange.
lines: Then, in our measure do but vouchsafe one change.
Thou bid'st me beg: this begging is not strange.

speeches.speech: Play, music, then! Nay, you must do it soon. [Music plays] Not yet! no dance! Thus change I like the moon.
lines: Play, music, then! Nay, you must do it soon.
[Music plays]
Not yet! no dance! Thus change I like the moon.

speeches.speech: Will you not dance? How come you thus estranged?
lines: Will you not dance? How come you thus estranged?

speeches.speech: You took the moon at full, but now she's changed.
lines: You took the moon at full, but now she's changed.

speeches.speech: Yet still she is the moon, and I the man. The music plays; vouchsafe some motion to it.
lines: Yet still she is the moon, and I the man.
The music plays; vouchsafe some motion to it.

speeches.speech: Our ears vouchsafe it.
lines: Our ears vouchsafe it.

speeches.speech: But your legs should do it.
lines: But your legs should do it.

speeches.speech: Since you are strangers and come here by chance, We'll not be nice: take hands. We will not dance.
lines: Since you are strangers and come here by chance,
We'll not be nice: take hands. We will not dance.

speeches.speech: Why take we hands, then?
lines: Why take we hands, then?

speeches.speech: Only to part friends: Curtsy, sweet hearts; and so the measure ends.
lines: Only to part friends:
Curtsy, sweet hearts; and so the measure ends.

speeches.speech: More measure of this measure; be not nice.
lines: More measure of this measure; be not nice.

speeches.speech: We can afford no more at such a price.
lines: We can afford no more at such a price.

speeches.speech: Prize you yourselves: what buys your company?
lines: Prize you yourselves: what buys your company?

speeches.speech: Your absence only.
lines: Your absence only.

speeches.speech: That can never be.
lines: That can never be.

speeches.speech: Then cannot we be bought: and so, adieu; Twice to your visor, and half once to you.
lines: Then cannot we be bought: and so, adieu;
Twice to your visor, and half once to you.

speeches.speech: If you deny to dance, let's hold more chat.
lines: If you deny to dance, let's hold more chat.

speeches.speech: In private, then.
lines: In private, then.

speeches.speech: I am best pleased with that.
lines: I am best pleased with that.

speeches.speech: White-handed mistress, one sweet word with thee.
lines: White-handed mistress, one sweet word with thee.

speeches.speech: Honey, and milk, and sugar; there is three.
lines: Honey, and milk, and sugar; there is three.

speeches.speech: Nay then, two treys, and if you grow so nice, Metheglin, wort, and malmsey: well run, dice! There's half-a-dozen sweets.
lines: Nay then, two treys, and if you grow so nice,
Metheglin, wort, and malmsey: well run, dice!
There's half-a-dozen sweets.

speeches.speech: Seventh sweet, adieu: Since you can cog, I'll play no more with you.
lines: Seventh sweet, adieu:
Since you can cog, I'll play no more with you.

speeches.speech: One word in secret.
lines: One word in secret.

speeches.speech: Let it not be sweet.
lines: Let it not be sweet.

speeches.speech: Thou grievest my gall.
lines: Thou grievest my gall.

speeches.speech: Gall! bitter.
lines: Gall! bitter.

speeches.speech: Therefore meet.
lines: Therefore meet.

speeches.speech: Will you vouchsafe with me to change a word?
lines: Will you vouchsafe with me to change a word?

speeches.speech: Name it.
lines: Name it.

speeches.speech: Fair lady,--
lines: Fair lady,--

speeches.speech: Say you so? Fair lord,-- Take that for your fair lady.
lines: Say you so? Fair lord,--
Take that for your fair lady.

speeches.speech: Please it you, As much in private, and I'll bid adieu.
lines: Please it you,
As much in private, and I'll bid adieu.

speeches.speech: What, was your vizard made without a tongue?
lines: What, was your vizard made without a tongue?

speeches.speech: I know the reason, lady, why you ask.
lines: I know the reason, lady, why you ask.

speeches.speech: O for your reason! quickly, sir; I long.
lines: O for your reason! quickly, sir; I long.

speeches.speech: You have a double tongue within your mask, And would afford my speechless vizard half.
lines: You have a double tongue within your mask,
And would afford my speechless vizard half.

speeches.speech: Veal, quoth the Dutchman. Is not 'veal' a calf?
lines: Veal, quoth the Dutchman. Is not 'veal' a calf?

speeches.speech: A calf, fair lady!
lines: A calf, fair lady!

speeches.speech: No, a fair lord calf.
lines: No, a fair lord calf.

speeches.speech: Let's part the word.
lines: Let's part the word.

speeches.speech: No, I'll not be your half Take all, and wean it; it may prove an ox.
lines: No, I'll not be your half
Take all, and wean it; it may prove an ox.

speeches.speech: Look, how you butt yourself in these sharp mocks! Will you give horns, chaste lady? do not so.
lines: Look, how you butt yourself in these sharp mocks!
Will you give horns, chaste lady? do not so.

speeches.speech: Then die a calf, before your horns do grow.
lines: Then die a calf, before your horns do grow.

speeches.speech: One word in private with you, ere I die.
lines: One word in private with you, ere I die.

speeches.speech: Bleat softly then; the butcher hears you cry.
lines: Bleat softly then; the butcher hears you cry.

speeches.speech: The tongues of mocking wenches are as keen As is the razor's edge invisible, Cutting a smaller hair than may be seen, Above the sense of sense; so sensible Seemeth their conference; their conceits have wings Fleeter than arrows, bullets, wind, thought, swifter things.
lines: The tongues of mocking wenches are as keen
As is the razor's edge invisible,
Cutting a smaller hair than may be seen,
Above the sense of sense; so sensible
Seemeth their conference; their conceits have wings
Fleeter than arrows, bullets, wind, thought, swifter things.

speeches.speech: Not one word more, my maids; break off, break off.
lines: Not one word more, my maids; break off, break off.

speeches.speech: By heaven, all dry-beaten with pure scoff!
lines: By heaven, all dry-beaten with pure scoff!

speeches.speech: Farewell, mad wenches; you have simple wits.
lines: Farewell, mad wenches; you have simple wits.

speeches.speech: Twenty adieus, my frozen Muscovits. [Exeunt FERDINAND, Lords, and Blackamoors] Are these the breed of wits so wonder'd at?
lines: Twenty adieus, my frozen Muscovits.
[Exeunt FERDINAND, Lords, and Blackamoors]
Are these the breed of wits so wonder'd at?

speeches.speech: Tapers they are, with your sweet breaths puff'd out.
lines: Tapers they are, with your sweet breaths puff'd out.

speeches.speech: Well-liking wits they have; gross, gross; fat, fat.
lines: Well-liking wits they have; gross, gross; fat, fat.

speeches.speech: O poverty in wit, kingly-poor flout! Will they not, think you, hang themselves tonight? Or ever, but in vizards, show their faces? This pert Biron was out of countenance quite.
lines: O poverty in wit, kingly-poor flout!
Will they not, think you, hang themselves tonight?
Or ever, but in vizards, show their faces?
This pert Biron was out of countenance quite.

speeches.speech: O, they were all in lamentable cases! The king was weeping-ripe for a good word.
lines: O, they were all in lamentable cases!
The king was weeping-ripe for a good word.

speeches.speech: Biron did swear himself out of all suit.
lines: Biron did swear himself out of all suit.

speeches.speech: Dumain was at my service, and his sword: No point, quoth I; my servant straight was mute.
lines: Dumain was at my service, and his sword:
No point, quoth I; my servant straight was mute.

speeches.speech: Lord Longaville said, I came o'er his heart; And trow you what he called me?
lines: Lord Longaville said, I came o'er his heart;
And trow you what he called me?

speeches.speech: Qualm, perhaps.
lines: Qualm, perhaps.

speeches.speech: Yes, in good faith.
lines: Yes, in good faith.

speeches.speech: Go, sickness as thou art!
lines: Go, sickness as thou art!

speeches.speech: Well, better wits have worn plain statute-caps. But will you hear? the king is my love sworn.
lines: Well, better wits have worn plain statute-caps.
But will you hear? the king is my love sworn.

speeches.speech: And quick Biron hath plighted faith to me.
lines: And quick Biron hath plighted faith to me.

speeches.speech: And Longaville was for my service born.
lines: And Longaville was for my service born.

speeches.speech: Dumain is mine, as sure as bark on tree.
lines: Dumain is mine, as sure as bark on tree.

speeches.speech: Madam, and pretty mistresses, give ear: Immediately they will again be here In their own shapes; for it can never be They will digest this harsh indignity.
lines: Madam, and pretty mistresses, give ear:
Immediately they will again be here
In their own shapes; for it can never be
They will digest this harsh indignity.

speeches.speech: Will they return?
lines: Will they return?

speeches.speech: They will, they will, God knows, And leap for joy, though they are lame with blows: Therefore change favours; and, when they repair, Blow like sweet roses in this summer air.
lines: They will, they will, God knows,
And leap for joy, though they are lame with blows:
Therefore change favours; and, when they repair,
Blow like sweet roses in this summer air.

speeches.speech: How blow? how blow? speak to be understood.
lines: How blow? how blow? speak to be understood.

speeches.speech: Fair ladies mask'd are roses in their bud; Dismask'd, their damask sweet commixture shown, Are angels vailing clouds, or roses blown.
lines: Fair ladies mask'd are roses in their bud;
Dismask'd, their damask sweet commixture shown,
Are angels vailing clouds, or roses blown.

speeches.speech: Avaunt, perplexity! What shall we do, If they return in their own shapes to woo?
lines: Avaunt, perplexity! What shall we do,
If they return in their own shapes to woo?

speeches.speech: Good madam, if by me you'll be advised, Let's, mock them still, as well known as disguised: Let us complain to them what fools were here, Disguised like Muscovites, in shapeless gear; And wonder what they were and to what end Their shallow shows and prologue vilely penn'd And their rough carriage so ridiculous, Should be presented at our tent to us.
lines: Good madam, if by me you'll be advised,
Let's, mock them still, as well known as disguised:
Let us complain to them what fools were here,
Disguised like Muscovites, in shapeless gear;
And wonder what they were and to what end
Their shallow shows and prologue vilely penn'd
And their rough carriage so ridiculous,
Should be presented at our tent to us.

speeches.speech: Ladies, withdraw: the gallants are at hand.
lines: Ladies, withdraw: the gallants are at hand.

speeches.speech: Whip to our tents, as roes run o'er land. [Exeunt PRINCESS, ROSALINE, KATHARINE, and MARIA] [Re-enter FERDINAND, BIRON, LONGAVILLE, and DUMAIN,] in their proper habits]
lines: Whip to our tents, as roes run o'er land.
[Exeunt PRINCESS, ROSALINE, KATHARINE, and MARIA]
[Re-enter FERDINAND, BIRON, LONGAVILLE, and DUMAIN,]
in their proper habits]

speeches.speech: Fair sir, God save you! Where's the princess?
lines: Fair sir, God save you! Where's the princess?

speeches.speech: Gone to her tent. Please it your majesty Command me any service to her thither?
lines: Gone to her tent. Please it your majesty
Command me any service to her thither?

speeches.speech: That she vouchsafe me audience for one word.
lines: That she vouchsafe me audience for one word.

speeches.speech: I will; and so will she, I know, my lord.
lines: I will; and so will she, I know, my lord.

speeches.speech: This fellow pecks up wit as pigeons pease, And utters it again when God doth please: He is wit's pedler, and retails his wares At wakes and wassails, meetings, markets, fairs; And we that sell by gross, the Lord doth know, Have not the grace to grace it with such show. This gallant pins the wenches on his sleeve; Had he been Adam, he had tempted Eve; A' can carve too, and lisp: why, this is he That kiss'd his hand away in courtesy; This is the ape of form, monsieur the nice, That, when he plays at tables, chides the dice In honourable terms: nay, he can sing A mean most meanly; and in ushering Mend him who can: the ladies call him sweet; The stairs, as he treads on them, kiss his feet: This is the flower that smiles on every one, To show his teeth as white as whale's bone; And consciences, that will not die in debt, Pay him the due of honey-tongued Boyet.
lines: This fellow pecks up wit as pigeons pease,
And utters it again when God doth please:
He is wit's pedler, and retails his wares
At wakes and wassails, meetings, markets, fairs;
And we that sell by gross, the Lord doth know,
Have not the grace to grace it with such show.
This gallant pins the wenches on his sleeve;
Had he been Adam, he had tempted Eve;
A' can carve too, and lisp: why, this is he
That kiss'd his hand away in courtesy;
This is the ape of form, monsieur the nice,
That, when he plays at tables, chides the dice
In honourable terms: nay, he can sing
A mean most meanly; and in ushering
Mend him who can: the ladies call him sweet;
The stairs, as he treads on them, kiss his feet:
This is the flower that smiles on every one,
To show his teeth as white as whale's bone;
And consciences, that will not die in debt,
Pay him the due of honey-tongued Boyet.

speeches.speech: A blister on his sweet tongue, with my heart, That put Armado's page out of his part!
lines: A blister on his sweet tongue, with my heart,
That put Armado's page out of his part!

speeches.speech: See where it comes! Behavior, what wert thou Till this madman show'd thee? and what art thou now? [Re-enter the PRINCESS, ushered by BOYET, ROSALINE,] MARIA, and KATHARINE]
lines: See where it comes! Behavior, what wert thou
Till this madman show'd thee? and what art thou now?
[Re-enter the PRINCESS, ushered by BOYET, ROSALINE,]
MARIA, and KATHARINE]

speeches.speech: All hail, sweet madam, and fair time of day!
lines: All hail, sweet madam, and fair time of day!

speeches.speech: 'Fair' in 'all hail' is foul, as I conceive.
lines: 'Fair' in 'all hail' is foul, as I conceive.

speeches.speech: Construe my speeches better, if you may.
lines: Construe my speeches better, if you may.

speeches.speech: Then wish me better; I will give you leave.
lines: Then wish me better; I will give you leave.

speeches.speech: We came to visit you, and purpose now To lead you to our court; vouchsafe it then.
lines: We came to visit you, and purpose now
To lead you to our court; vouchsafe it then.

speeches.speech: This field shall hold me; and so hold your vow: Nor God, nor I, delights in perjured men.
lines: This field shall hold me; and so hold your vow:
Nor God, nor I, delights in perjured men.

speeches.speech: Rebuke me not for that which you provoke: The virtue of your eye must break my oath.
lines: Rebuke me not for that which you provoke:
The virtue of your eye must break my oath.

speeches.speech: You nickname virtue; vice you should have spoke; For virtue's office never breaks men's troth. Now by my maiden honour, yet as pure As the unsullied lily, I protest, A world of torments though I should endure, I would not yield to be your house's guest; So much I hate a breaking cause to be Of heavenly oaths, vow'd with integrity.
lines: You nickname virtue; vice you should have spoke;
For virtue's office never breaks men's troth.
Now by my maiden honour, yet as pure
As the unsullied lily, I protest,
A world of torments though I should endure,
I would not yield to be your house's guest;
So much I hate a breaking cause to be
Of heavenly oaths, vow'd with integrity.

speeches.speech: O, you have lived in desolation here, Unseen, unvisited, much to our shame.
lines: O, you have lived in desolation here,
Unseen, unvisited, much to our shame.

speeches.speech: Not so, my lord; it is not so, I swear; We have had pastimes here and pleasant game: A mess of Russians left us but of late.
lines: Not so, my lord; it is not so, I swear;
We have had pastimes here and pleasant game:
A mess of Russians left us but of late.

speeches.speech: How, madam! Russians!
lines: How, madam! Russians!

speeches.speech: Ay, in truth, my lord; Trim gallants, full of courtship and of state.
lines: Ay, in truth, my lord;
Trim gallants, full of courtship and of state.

speeches.speech: Madam, speak true. It is not so, my lord: My lady, to the manner of the days, In courtesy gives undeserving praise. We four indeed confronted were with four In Russian habit: here they stay'd an hour, And talk'd apace; and in that hour, my lord, They did not bless us with one happy word. I dare not call them fools; but this I think, When they are thirsty, fools would fain have drink.
lines: Madam, speak true. It is not so, my lord:
My lady, to the manner of the days,
In courtesy gives undeserving praise.
We four indeed confronted were with four
In Russian habit: here they stay'd an hour,
And talk'd apace; and in that hour, my lord,
They did not bless us with one happy word.
I dare not call them fools; but this I think,
When they are thirsty, fools would fain have drink.

speeches.speech: This jest is dry to me. Fair gentle sweet, Your wit makes wise things foolish: when we greet, With eyes best seeing, heaven's fiery eye, By light we lose light: your capacity Is of that nature that to your huge store Wise things seem foolish and rich things but poor.
lines: This jest is dry to me. Fair gentle sweet,
Your wit makes wise things foolish: when we greet,
With eyes best seeing, heaven's fiery eye,
By light we lose light: your capacity
Is of that nature that to your huge store
Wise things seem foolish and rich things but poor.

speeches.speech: This proves you wise and rich, for in my eye,--
lines: This proves you wise and rich, for in my eye,--

speeches.speech: I am a fool, and full of poverty.
lines: I am a fool, and full of poverty.

speeches.speech: But that you take what doth to you belong, It were a fault to snatch words from my tongue.
lines: But that you take what doth to you belong,
It were a fault to snatch words from my tongue.

speeches.speech: O, I am yours, and all that I possess!
lines: O, I am yours, and all that I possess!

speeches.speech: All the fool mine?
lines: All the fool mine?

speeches.speech: I cannot give you less.
lines: I cannot give you less.

speeches.speech: Which of the vizards was it that you wore?
lines: Which of the vizards was it that you wore?

speeches.speech: Where? when? what vizard? why demand you this?
lines: Where? when? what vizard? why demand you this?

speeches.speech: There, then, that vizard; that superfluous case That hid the worse and show'd the better face.
lines: There, then, that vizard; that superfluous case
That hid the worse and show'd the better face.

speeches.speech: We are descried; they'll mock us now downright.
lines: We are descried; they'll mock us now downright.

speeches.speech: Let us confess and turn it to a jest.
lines: Let us confess and turn it to a jest.

speeches.speech: Amazed, my lord? why looks your highness sad?
lines: Amazed, my lord? why looks your highness sad?

speeches.speech: Help, hold his brows! he'll swoon! Why look you pale? Sea-sick, I think, coming from Muscovy.
lines: Help, hold his brows! he'll swoon! Why look you pale?
Sea-sick, I think, coming from Muscovy.

speeches.speech: Thus pour the stars down plagues for perjury. Can any face of brass hold longer out? Here stand I. lady, dart thy skill at me; Bruise me with scorn, confound me with a flout; Thrust thy sharp wit quite through my ignorance; Cut me to pieces with thy keen conceit; And I will wish thee never more to dance, Nor never more in Russian habit wait. O, never will I trust to speeches penn'd, Nor to the motion of a schoolboy's tongue, Nor never come in vizard to my friend, Nor woo in rhyme, like a blind harper's song! Taffeta phrases, silken terms precise, Three-piled hyperboles, spruce affectation, Figures pedantical; these summer-flies Have blown me full of maggot ostentation: I do forswear them; and I here protest, By this white glove;--how white the hand, God knows!-- Henceforth my wooing mind shall be express'd In russet yeas and honest kersey noes: And, to begin, wench,--so God help me, la!-- My love to thee is sound, sans crack or flaw.
lines: Thus pour the stars down plagues for perjury.
Can any face of brass hold longer out?
Here stand I. lady, dart thy skill at me;
Bruise me with scorn, confound me with a flout;
Thrust thy sharp wit quite through my ignorance;
Cut me to pieces with thy keen conceit;
And I will wish thee never more to dance,
Nor never more in Russian habit wait.
O, never will I trust to speeches penn'd,
Nor to the motion of a schoolboy's tongue,
Nor never come in vizard to my friend,
Nor woo in rhyme, like a blind harper's song!
Taffeta phrases, silken terms precise,
Three-piled hyperboles, spruce affectation,
Figures pedantical; these summer-flies
Have blown me full of maggot ostentation:
I do forswear them; and I here protest,
By this white glove;--how white the hand, God knows!--
Henceforth my wooing mind shall be express'd
In russet yeas and honest kersey noes:
And, to begin, wench,--so God help me, la!--
My love to thee is sound, sans crack or flaw.

speeches.speech: Sans sans, I pray you.
lines: Sans sans, I pray you.

speeches.speech: Yet I have a trick Of the old rage: bear with me, I am sick; I'll leave it by degrees. Soft, let us see: Write, 'Lord have mercy on us' on those three; They are infected; in their hearts it lies; They have the plague, and caught it of your eyes; These lords are visited; you are not free, For the Lord's tokens on you do I see.
lines: Yet I have a trick
Of the old rage: bear with me, I am sick;
I'll leave it by degrees. Soft, let us see:
Write, 'Lord have mercy on us' on those three;
They are infected; in their hearts it lies;
They have the plague, and caught it of your eyes;
These lords are visited; you are not free,
For the Lord's tokens on you do I see.

speeches.speech: No, they are free that gave these tokens to us.
lines: No, they are free that gave these tokens to us.

speeches.speech: Our states are forfeit: seek not to undo us.
lines: Our states are forfeit: seek not to undo us.

speeches.speech: It is not so; for how can this be true, That you stand forfeit, being those that sue?
lines: It is not so; for how can this be true,
That you stand forfeit, being those that sue?

speeches.speech: Peace! for I will not have to do with you.
lines: Peace! for I will not have to do with you.

speeches.speech: Nor shall not, if I do as I intend.
lines: Nor shall not, if I do as I intend.

speeches.speech: Speak for yourselves; my wit is at an end.
lines: Speak for yourselves; my wit is at an end.

speeches.speech: Teach us, sweet madam, for our rude transgression Some fair excuse.
lines: Teach us, sweet madam, for our rude transgression
Some fair excuse.

speeches.speech: The fairest is confession. Were not you here but even now disguised?
lines: The fairest is confession.
Were not you here but even now disguised?

speeches.speech: Madam, I was.
lines: Madam, I was.

speeches.speech: And were you well advised?
lines: And were you well advised?

speeches.speech: I was, fair madam.
lines: I was, fair madam.

speeches.speech: When you then were here, What did you whisper in your lady's ear?
lines: When you then were here,
What did you whisper in your lady's ear?

speeches.speech: That more than all the world I did respect her.
lines: That more than all the world I did respect her.

speeches.speech: When she shall challenge this, you will reject her.
lines: When she shall challenge this, you will reject her.

speeches.speech: Upon mine honour, no.
lines: Upon mine honour, no.

speeches.speech: Peace, peace! forbear: Your oath once broke, you force not to forswear.
lines: Peace, peace! forbear:
Your oath once broke, you force not to forswear.

speeches.speech: Despise me, when I break this oath of mine.
lines: Despise me, when I break this oath of mine.

speeches.speech: I will: and therefore keep it. Rosaline, What did the Russian whisper in your ear?
lines: I will: and therefore keep it. Rosaline,
What did the Russian whisper in your ear?

speeches.speech: Madam, he swore that he did hold me dear As precious eyesight, and did value me Above this world; adding thereto moreover That he would wed me, or else die my lover.
lines: Madam, he swore that he did hold me dear
As precious eyesight, and did value me
Above this world; adding thereto moreover
That he would wed me, or else die my lover.

speeches.speech: God give thee joy of him! the noble lord Most honourably doth unhold his word.
lines: God give thee joy of him! the noble lord
Most honourably doth unhold his word.

speeches.speech: What mean you, madam? by my life, my troth, I never swore this lady such an oath.
lines: What mean you, madam? by my life, my troth,
I never swore this lady such an oath.

speeches.speech: By heaven, you did; and to confirm it plain, You gave me this: but take it, sir, again.
lines: By heaven, you did; and to confirm it plain,
You gave me this: but take it, sir, again.

speeches.speech: My faith and this the princess I did give: I knew her by this jewel on her sleeve.
lines: My faith and this the princess I did give:
I knew her by this jewel on her sleeve.

speeches.speech: Pardon me, sir, this jewel did she wear; And Lord Biron, I thank him, is my dear. What, will you have me, or your pearl again?
lines: Pardon me, sir, this jewel did she wear;
And Lord Biron, I thank him, is my dear.
What, will you have me, or your pearl again?

speeches.speech: Neither of either; I remit both twain. I see the trick on't: here was a consent, Knowing aforehand of our merriment, To dash it like a Christmas comedy: Some carry-tale, some please-man, some slight zany, Some mumble-news, some trencher-knight, some Dick, That smiles his cheek in years and knows the trick To make my lady laugh when she's disposed, Told our intents before; which once disclosed, The ladies did change favours: and then we, Following the signs, woo'd but the sign of she. Now, to our perjury to add more terror, We are again forsworn, in will and error. Much upon this it is: and might not you [To BOYET] Forestall our sport, to make us thus untrue? Do not you know my lady's foot by the squier, And laugh upon the apple of her eye? And stand between her back, sir, and the fire, Holding a trencher, jesting merrily? You put our page out: go, you are allow'd; Die when you will, a smock shall be your shroud. You leer upon me, do you? there's an eye Wounds like a leaden sword.
lines: Neither of either; I remit both twain.
I see the trick on't: here was a consent,
Knowing aforehand of our merriment,
To dash it like a Christmas comedy:
Some carry-tale, some please-man, some slight zany,
Some mumble-news, some trencher-knight, some Dick,
That smiles his cheek in years and knows the trick
To make my lady laugh when she's disposed,
Told our intents before; which once disclosed,
The ladies did change favours: and then we,
Following the signs, woo'd but the sign of she.
Now, to our perjury to add more terror,
We are again forsworn, in will and error.
Much upon this it is: and might not you
[To BOYET]
Forestall our sport, to make us thus untrue?
Do not you know my lady's foot by the squier,
And laugh upon the apple of her eye?
And stand between her back, sir, and the fire,
Holding a trencher, jesting merrily?
You put our page out: go, you are allow'd;
Die when you will, a smock shall be your shroud.
You leer upon me, do you? there's an eye
Wounds like a leaden sword.

speeches.speech: Full merrily Hath this brave manage, this career, been run.
lines: Full merrily
Hath this brave manage, this career, been run.

speeches.speech: Lo, he is tilting straight! Peace! I have done. [Enter COSTARD] Welcome, pure wit! thou partest a fair fray.
lines: Lo, he is tilting straight! Peace! I have done.
[Enter COSTARD]
Welcome, pure wit! thou partest a fair fray.

speeches.speech: O Lord, sir, they would know Whether the three Worthies shall come in or no.
lines: O Lord, sir, they would know
Whether the three Worthies shall come in or no.

speeches.speech: What, are there but three?
lines: What, are there but three?

speeches.speech: No, sir; but it is vara fine, For every one pursents three.
lines: No, sir; but it is vara fine,
For every one pursents three.

speeches.speech: And three times thrice is nine.
lines: And three times thrice is nine.

speeches.speech: Not so, sir; under correction, sir; I hope it is not so. You cannot beg us, sir, I can assure you, sir we know what we know: I hope, sir, three times thrice, sir,--
lines: Not so, sir; under correction, sir; I hope it is not so.
You cannot beg us, sir, I can assure you, sir we know
what we know:
I hope, sir, three times thrice, sir,--

speeches.speech: Is not nine.
lines: Is not nine.

speeches.speech: Under correction, sir, we know whereuntil it doth amount.
lines: Under correction, sir, we know whereuntil it doth amount.

speeches.speech: By Jove, I always took three threes for nine.
lines: By Jove, I always took three threes for nine.

speeches.speech: O Lord, sir, it were pity you should get your living by reckoning, sir.
lines: O Lord, sir, it were pity you should get your living
by reckoning, sir.

speeches.speech: How much is it?
lines: How much is it?

speeches.speech: O Lord, sir, the parties themselves, the actors, sir, will show whereuntil it doth amount: for mine own part, I am, as they say, but to parfect one man in one poor man, Pompion the Great, sir.
lines: O Lord, sir, the parties themselves, the actors,
sir, will show whereuntil it doth amount: for mine
own part, I am, as they say, but to parfect one man
in one poor man, Pompion the Great, sir.

speeches.speech: Art thou one of the Worthies?
lines: Art thou one of the Worthies?

speeches.speech: It pleased them to think me worthy of Pompion the Great: for mine own part, I know not the degree of the Worthy, but I am to stand for him.
lines: It pleased them to think me worthy of Pompion the
Great: for mine own part, I know not the degree of
the Worthy, but I am to stand for him.

speeches.speech: Go, bid them prepare.
lines: Go, bid them prepare.

speeches.speech: We will turn it finely off, sir; we will take some care.
lines: We will turn it finely off, sir; we will take
some care.

speeches.speech: Biron, they will shame us: let them not approach.
lines: Biron, they will shame us: let them not approach.

speeches.speech: We are shame-proof, my lord: and tis some policy To have one show worse than the king's and his company.
lines: We are shame-proof, my lord: and tis some policy
To have one show worse than the king's and his company.

speeches.speech: I say they shall not come.
lines: I say they shall not come.

speeches.speech: Nay, my good lord, let me o'errule you now: That sport best pleases that doth least know how: Where zeal strives to content, and the contents Dies in the zeal of that which it presents: Their form confounded makes most form in mirth, When great things labouring perish in their birth.
lines: Nay, my good lord, let me o'errule you now:
That sport best pleases that doth least know how:
Where zeal strives to content, and the contents
Dies in the zeal of that which it presents:
Their form confounded makes most form in mirth,
When great things labouring perish in their birth.

speeches.speech: A right description of our sport, my lord.
lines: A right description of our sport, my lord.

speeches.speech: Anointed, I implore so much expense of thy royal sweet breath as will utter a brace of words.
lines: Anointed, I implore so much expense of thy royal
sweet breath as will utter a brace of words.

speeches.speech: Doth this man serve God?
lines: Doth this man serve God?

speeches.speech: Why ask you?
