lines: Why ask you?

speeches.speech: He speaks not like a man of God's making.
lines: He speaks not like a man of God's making.

speeches.speech: That is all one, my fair, sweet, honey monarch; for, I protest, the schoolmaster is exceeding fantastical; too, too vain, too too vain: but we will put it, as they say, to fortuna de la guerra. I wish you the peace of mind, most royal couplement!
lines: That is all one, my fair, sweet, honey monarch; for,
I protest, the schoolmaster is exceeding
fantastical; too, too vain, too too vain: but we
will put it, as they say, to fortuna de la guerra.
I wish you the peace of mind, most royal couplement!

speeches.speech: Here is like to be a good presence of Worthies. He presents Hector of Troy; the swain, Pompey the Great; the parish curate, Alexander; Armado's page, Hercules; the pedant, Judas Maccabaeus: And if these four Worthies in their first show thrive, These four will change habits, and present the other five.
lines: Here is like to be a good presence of Worthies. He
presents Hector of Troy; the swain, Pompey the
Great; the parish curate, Alexander; Armado's page,
Hercules; the pedant, Judas Maccabaeus: And if
these four Worthies in their first show thrive,
These four will change habits, and present the other five.

speeches.speech: There is five in the first show.
lines: There is five in the first show.

speeches.speech: You are deceived; 'tis not so.
lines: You are deceived; 'tis not so.

speeches.speech: The pedant, the braggart, the hedge-priest, the fool and the boy:-- Abate throw at novum, and the whole world again Cannot pick out five such, take each one in his vein.
lines: The pedant, the braggart, the hedge-priest, the fool
and the boy:--
Abate throw at novum, and the whole world again
Cannot pick out five such, take each one in his vein.

speeches.speech: The ship is under sail, and here she comes amain.
lines: The ship is under sail, and here she comes amain.

speeches.speech: I Pompey am,--
lines: I Pompey am,--

speeches.speech: You lie, you are not he.
lines: You lie, you are not he.

speeches.speech: I Pompey am,--
lines: I Pompey am,--

speeches.speech: With libbard's head on knee.
lines: With libbard's head on knee.

speeches.speech: Well said, old mocker: I must needs be friends with thee.
lines: Well said, old mocker: I must needs be friends
with thee.

speeches.speech: I Pompey am, Pompey surnamed the Big--
lines: I Pompey am, Pompey surnamed the Big--

speeches.speech: The Great.
lines: The Great.

speeches.speech: It is, 'Great,' sir:-- Pompey surnamed the Great; That oft in field, with targe and shield, did make my foe to sweat: And travelling along this coast, I here am come by chance, And lay my arms before the legs of this sweet lass of France, If your ladyship would say, 'Thanks, Pompey,' I had done.
lines: It is, 'Great,' sir:--
Pompey surnamed the Great;
That oft in field, with targe and shield, did make
my foe to sweat:
And travelling along this coast, I here am come by chance,
And lay my arms before the legs of this sweet lass of France,
If your ladyship would say, 'Thanks, Pompey,' I had done.

speeches.speech: Great thanks, great Pompey.
lines: Great thanks, great Pompey.

speeches.speech: 'Tis not so much worth; but I hope I was perfect: I made a little fault in 'Great.'
lines: 'Tis not so much worth; but I hope I was perfect: I
made a little fault in 'Great.'

speeches.speech: My hat to a halfpenny, Pompey proves the best Worthy.
lines: My hat to a halfpenny, Pompey proves the best Worthy.

speeches.speech: When in the world I lived, I was the world's commander; By east, west, north, and south, I spread my conquering might: My scutcheon plain declares that I am Alisander,--
lines: When in the world I lived, I was the world's
commander;
By east, west, north, and south, I spread my
conquering might:
My scutcheon plain declares that I am Alisander,--

speeches.speech: Your nose says, no, you are not for it stands too right.
lines: Your nose says, no, you are not for it stands too right.

speeches.speech: Your nose smells 'no' in this, most tender-smelling knight.
lines: Your nose smells 'no' in this, most tender-smelling knight.

speeches.speech: The conqueror is dismay'd. Proceed, good Alexander.
lines: The conqueror is dismay'd. Proceed, good Alexander.

speeches.speech: When in the world I lived, I was the world's commander,--
lines: When in the world I lived, I was the world's
commander,--

speeches.speech: Most true, 'tis right; you were so, Alisander.
lines: Most true, 'tis right; you were so, Alisander.

speeches.speech: Pompey the Great,--
lines: Pompey the Great,--

speeches.speech: Your servant, and Costard.
lines: Your servant, and Costard.

speeches.speech: Take away the conqueror, take away Alisander.
lines: Take away the conqueror, take away Alisander.

speeches.speech: [To SIR NATHANIEL] O, sir, you have overthrown Alisander the conqueror! You will be scraped out of the painted cloth for this: your lion, that holds his poll-axe sitting on a close-stool, will be given to Ajax: he will be the ninth Worthy. A conqueror, and afeard to speak! run away for shame, Alisander. [SIR NATHANIEL retires] There, an't shall please you; a foolish mild man; an honest man, look you, and soon dashed. He is a marvellous good neighbour, faith, and a very good bowler: but, for Alisander,--alas, you see how 'tis,--a little o'erparted. But there are Worthies a-coming will speak their mind in some other sort.
lines: [To SIR NATHANIEL] O, sir, you have overthrown
Alisander the conqueror! You will be scraped out of
the painted cloth for this: your lion, that holds
his poll-axe sitting on a close-stool, will be given
to Ajax: he will be the ninth Worthy. A conqueror,
and afeard to speak! run away for shame, Alisander.
[SIR NATHANIEL retires]
There, an't shall please you; a foolish mild man; an
honest man, look you, and soon dashed. He is a
marvellous good neighbour, faith, and a very good
bowler: but, for Alisander,--alas, you see how
'tis,--a little o'erparted. But there are Worthies
a-coming will speak their mind in some other sort.

speeches.speech: Great Hercules is presented by this imp, Whose club kill'd Cerberus, that three-headed canis; And when he was a babe, a child, a shrimp, Thus did he strangle serpents in his manus. Quoniam he seemeth in minority, Ergo I come with this apology. Keep some state in thy exit, and vanish. [MOTH retires] Judas I am,--
lines: Great Hercules is presented by this imp,
Whose club kill'd Cerberus, that three-headed canis;
And when he was a babe, a child, a shrimp,
Thus did he strangle serpents in his manus.
Quoniam he seemeth in minority,
Ergo I come with this apology.
Keep some state in thy exit, and vanish.
[MOTH retires]
Judas I am,--

speeches.speech: A Judas!
lines: A Judas!

speeches.speech: Not Iscariot, sir. Judas I am, ycliped Maccabaeus.
lines: Not Iscariot, sir.
Judas I am, ycliped Maccabaeus.

speeches.speech: Judas Maccabaeus clipt is plain Judas.
lines: Judas Maccabaeus clipt is plain Judas.

speeches.speech: A kissing traitor. How art thou proved Judas?
lines: A kissing traitor. How art thou proved Judas?

speeches.speech: Judas I am,--
lines: Judas I am,--

speeches.speech: The more shame for you, Judas.
lines: The more shame for you, Judas.

speeches.speech: What mean you, sir?
lines: What mean you, sir?

speeches.speech: To make Judas hang himself.
lines: To make Judas hang himself.

speeches.speech: Begin, sir; you are my elder.
lines: Begin, sir; you are my elder.

speeches.speech: Well followed: Judas was hanged on an elder.
lines: Well followed: Judas was hanged on an elder.

speeches.speech: I will not be put out of countenance.
lines: I will not be put out of countenance.

speeches.speech: Because thou hast no face.
lines: Because thou hast no face.

speeches.speech: What is this?
lines: What is this?

speeches.speech: A cittern-head.
lines: A cittern-head.

speeches.speech: The head of a bodkin.
lines: The head of a bodkin.

speeches.speech: A Death's face in a ring.
lines: A Death's face in a ring.

speeches.speech: The face of an old Roman coin, scarce seen.
lines: The face of an old Roman coin, scarce seen.

speeches.speech: The pommel of Caesar's falchion.
lines: The pommel of Caesar's falchion.

speeches.speech: The carved-bone face on a flask.
lines: The carved-bone face on a flask.

speeches.speech: Saint George's half-cheek in a brooch.
lines: Saint George's half-cheek in a brooch.

speeches.speech: Ay, and in a brooch of lead.
lines: Ay, and in a brooch of lead.

speeches.speech: Ay, and worn in the cap of a tooth-drawer. And now forward; for we have put thee in countenance.
lines: Ay, and worn in the cap of a tooth-drawer.
And now forward; for we have put thee in countenance.

speeches.speech: You have put me out of countenance.
lines: You have put me out of countenance.

speeches.speech: False; we have given thee faces.
lines: False; we have given thee faces.

speeches.speech: But you have out-faced them all.
lines: But you have out-faced them all.

speeches.speech: An thou wert a lion, we would do so.
lines: An thou wert a lion, we would do so.

speeches.speech: Therefore, as he is an ass, let him go. And so adieu, sweet Jude! nay, why dost thou stay?
lines: Therefore, as he is an ass, let him go.
And so adieu, sweet Jude! nay, why dost thou stay?

speeches.speech: For the latter end of his name.
lines: For the latter end of his name.

speeches.speech: For the ass to the Jude; give it him:--Jud-as, away!
lines: For the ass to the Jude; give it him:--Jud-as, away!

speeches.speech: This is not generous, not gentle, not humble.
lines: This is not generous, not gentle, not humble.

speeches.speech: A light for Monsieur Judas! it grows dark, he may stumble.
lines: A light for Monsieur Judas! it grows dark, he may stumble.

speeches.speech: Alas, poor Maccabaeus, how hath he been baited!
lines: Alas, poor Maccabaeus, how hath he been baited!

speeches.speech: Hide thy head, Achilles: here comes Hector in arms.
lines: Hide thy head, Achilles: here comes Hector in arms.

speeches.speech: Though my mocks come home by me, I will now be merry.
lines: Though my mocks come home by me, I will now be merry.

speeches.speech: Hector was but a Troyan in respect of this.
lines: Hector was but a Troyan in respect of this.

speeches.speech: But is this Hector?
lines: But is this Hector?

speeches.speech: I think Hector was not so clean-timbered.
lines: I think Hector was not so clean-timbered.

speeches.speech: His leg is too big for Hector's.
lines: His leg is too big for Hector's.

speeches.speech: More calf, certain.
lines: More calf, certain.

speeches.speech: No; he is best endued in the small.
lines: No; he is best endued in the small.

speeches.speech: This cannot be Hector.
lines: This cannot be Hector.

speeches.speech: He's a god or a painter; for he makes faces.
lines: He's a god or a painter; for he makes faces.

speeches.speech: The armipotent Mars, of lances the almighty, Gave Hector a gift,--
lines: The armipotent Mars, of lances the almighty,
Gave Hector a gift,--

speeches.speech: A gilt nutmeg.
lines: A gilt nutmeg.

speeches.speech: A lemon.
lines: A lemon.

speeches.speech: Stuck with cloves.
lines: Stuck with cloves.

speeches.speech: No, cloven.
lines: No, cloven.

speeches.speech: Peace!-- The armipotent Mars, of lances the almighty Gave Hector a gift, the heir of Ilion; A man so breathed, that certain he would fight; yea From morn till night, out of his pavilion. I am that flower,--
lines: Peace!--
The armipotent Mars, of lances the almighty
Gave Hector a gift, the heir of Ilion;
A man so breathed, that certain he would fight; yea
From morn till night, out of his pavilion.
I am that flower,--

speeches.speech: That mint.
lines: That mint.

speeches.speech: That columbine.
lines: That columbine.

speeches.speech: Sweet Lord Longaville, rein thy tongue.
lines: Sweet Lord Longaville, rein thy tongue.

speeches.speech: I must rather give it the rein, for it runs against Hector.
lines: I must rather give it the rein, for it runs against Hector.

speeches.speech: Ay, and Hector's a greyhound.
lines: Ay, and Hector's a greyhound.

speeches.speech: The sweet war-man is dead and rotten; sweet chucks, beat not the bones of the buried: when he breathed, he was a man. But I will forward with my device. [To the PRINCESS] Sweet royalty, bestow on me the sense of hearing.
lines: The sweet war-man is dead and rotten; sweet chucks,
beat not the bones of the buried: when he breathed,
he was a man. But I will forward with my device.
[To the PRINCESS]
Sweet royalty, bestow on me the sense of hearing.

speeches.speech: Speak, brave Hector: we are much delighted.
lines: Speak, brave Hector: we are much delighted.

speeches.speech: I do adore thy sweet grace's slipper.
lines: I do adore thy sweet grace's slipper.

speeches.speech: [Aside to DUMAIN] Loves her by the foot,--
lines: [Aside to DUMAIN] Loves her by the foot,--

speeches.speech: [Aside to BOYET] He may not by the yard.
lines: [Aside to BOYET] He may not by the yard.

speeches.speech: This Hector far surmounted Hannibal,--
lines: This Hector far surmounted Hannibal,--

speeches.speech: The party is gone, fellow Hector, she is gone; she is two months on her way.
lines: The party is gone, fellow Hector, she is gone; she
is two months on her way.

speeches.speech: What meanest thou?
lines: What meanest thou?

speeches.speech: Faith, unless you play the honest Troyan, the poor wench is cast away: she's quick; the child brags in her belly already: tis yours.
lines: Faith, unless you play the honest Troyan, the poor
wench is cast away: she's quick; the child brags in
her belly already: tis yours.

speeches.speech: Dost thou infamonize me among potentates? thou shalt die.
lines: Dost thou infamonize me among potentates? thou shalt
die.

speeches.speech: Then shall Hector be whipped for Jaquenetta that is quick by him and hanged for Pompey that is dead by him.
lines: Then shall Hector be whipped for Jaquenetta that is
quick by him and hanged for Pompey that is dead by
him.

speeches.speech: Most rare Pompey!
lines: Most rare Pompey!

speeches.speech: Renowned Pompey!
lines: Renowned Pompey!

speeches.speech: Greater than great, great, great, great Pompey! Pompey the Huge!
lines: Greater than great, great, great, great Pompey!
Pompey the Huge!

speeches.speech: Hector trembles.
lines: Hector trembles.

speeches.speech: Pompey is moved. More Ates, more Ates! stir them on! stir them on!
lines: Pompey is moved. More Ates, more Ates! stir them
on! stir them on!

speeches.speech: Hector will challenge him.
lines: Hector will challenge him.

speeches.speech: Ay, if a' have no man's blood in's belly than will sup a flea.
lines: Ay, if a' have no man's blood in's belly than will
sup a flea.

speeches.speech: By the north pole, I do challenge thee.
lines: By the north pole, I do challenge thee.

speeches.speech: I will not fight with a pole, like a northern man: I'll slash; I'll do it by the sword. I bepray you, let me borrow my arms again.
lines: I will not fight with a pole, like a northern man:
I'll slash; I'll do it by the sword. I bepray you,
let me borrow my arms again.

speeches.speech: Room for the incensed Worthies!
lines: Room for the incensed Worthies!

speeches.speech: I'll do it in my shirt.
lines: I'll do it in my shirt.

speeches.speech: Most resolute Pompey!
lines: Most resolute Pompey!

speeches.speech: Master, let me take you a buttonhole lower. Do you not see Pompey is uncasing for the combat? What mean you? You will lose your reputation.
lines: Master, let me take you a buttonhole lower. Do you
not see Pompey is uncasing for the combat? What mean
you? You will lose your reputation.

speeches.speech: Gentlemen and soldiers, pardon me; I will not combat in my shirt.
lines: Gentlemen and soldiers, pardon me; I will not combat
in my shirt.

speeches.speech: You may not deny it: Pompey hath made the challenge.
lines: You may not deny it: Pompey hath made the challenge.

speeches.speech: Sweet bloods, I both may and will.
lines: Sweet bloods, I both may and will.

speeches.speech: What reason have you for't?
lines: What reason have you for't?

speeches.speech: The naked truth of it is, I have no shirt; I go woolward for penance.
lines: The naked truth of it is, I have no shirt; I go
woolward for penance.

speeches.speech: True, and it was enjoined him in Rome for want of linen: since when, I'll be sworn, he wore none but a dishclout of Jaquenetta's, and that a' wears next his heart for a favour.
lines: True, and it was enjoined him in Rome for want of
linen: since when, I'll be sworn, he wore none but
a dishclout of Jaquenetta's, and that a' wears next
his heart for a favour.

speeches.speech: God save you, madam!
lines: God save you, madam!

speeches.speech: Welcome, Mercade; But that thou interrupt'st our merriment.
lines: Welcome, Mercade;
But that thou interrupt'st our merriment.

speeches.speech: I am sorry, madam; for the news I bring Is heavy in my tongue. The king your father--
lines: I am sorry, madam; for the news I bring
Is heavy in my tongue. The king your father--

speeches.speech: Dead, for my life!
lines: Dead, for my life!

speeches.speech: Even so; my tale is told.
lines: Even so; my tale is told.

speeches.speech: Worthies, away! the scene begins to cloud.
lines: Worthies, away! the scene begins to cloud.

speeches.speech: For mine own part, I breathe free breath. I have seen the day of wrong through the little hole of discretion, and I will right myself like a soldier.
lines: For mine own part, I breathe free breath. I have
seen the day of wrong through the little hole of
discretion, and I will right myself like a soldier.

speeches.speech: How fares your majesty?
lines: How fares your majesty?

speeches.speech: Boyet, prepare; I will away tonight.
lines: Boyet, prepare; I will away tonight.

speeches.speech: Madam, not so; I do beseech you, stay.
lines: Madam, not so; I do beseech you, stay.

speeches.speech: Prepare, I say. I thank you, gracious lords, For all your fair endeavors; and entreat, Out of a new-sad soul, that you vouchsafe In your rich wisdom to excuse or hide The liberal opposition of our spirits, If over-boldly we have borne ourselves In the converse of breath: your gentleness Was guilty of it. Farewell worthy lord! A heavy heart bears not a nimble tongue: Excuse me so, coming too short of thanks For my great suit so easily obtain'd.
lines: Prepare, I say. I thank you, gracious lords,
For all your fair endeavors; and entreat,
Out of a new-sad soul, that you vouchsafe
In your rich wisdom to excuse or hide
The liberal opposition of our spirits,
If over-boldly we have borne ourselves
In the converse of breath: your gentleness
Was guilty of it. Farewell worthy lord!
A heavy heart bears not a nimble tongue:
Excuse me so, coming too short of thanks
For my great suit so easily obtain'd.

speeches.speech: The extreme parts of time extremely forms All causes to the purpose of his speed, And often at his very loose decides That which long process could not arbitrate: And though the mourning brow of progeny Forbid the smiling courtesy of love The holy suit which fain it would convince, Yet, since love's argument was first on foot, Let not the cloud of sorrow justle it From what it purposed; since, to wail friends lost Is not by much so wholesome-profitable As to rejoice at friends but newly found.PRINCESS. I understand you not: my griefs are double.
lines: The extreme parts of time extremely forms
All causes to the purpose of his speed,
And often at his very loose decides
That which long process could not arbitrate:
And though the mourning brow of progeny
Forbid the smiling courtesy of love
The holy suit which fain it would convince,
Yet, since love's argument was first on foot,
Let not the cloud of sorrow justle it
From what it purposed; since, to wail friends lost
Is not by much so wholesome-profitable
As to rejoice at friends but newly found.PRINCESS. I understand you not: my griefs are double.

speeches.speech: Honest plain words best pierce the ear of grief; And by these badges understand the king. For your fair sakes have we neglected time, Play'd foul play with our oaths: your beauty, ladies, Hath much deform'd us, fashioning our humours Even to the opposed end of our intents: And what in us hath seem'd ridiculous,-- As love is full of unbefitting strains, All wanton as a child, skipping and vain, Form'd by the eye and therefore, like the eye, Full of strange shapes, of habits and of forms, Varying in subjects as the eye doth roll To every varied object in his glance: Which parti-coated presence of loose love Put on by us, if, in your heavenly eyes, Have misbecomed our oaths and gravities, Those heavenly eyes, that look into these faults, Suggested us to make. Therefore, ladies, Our love being yours, the error that love makes Is likewise yours: we to ourselves prove false, By being once false for ever to be true To those that make us both,--fair ladies, you: And even that falsehood, in itself a sin, Thus purifies itself and turns to grace.
lines: Honest plain words best pierce the ear of grief;
And by these badges understand the king.
For your fair sakes have we neglected time,
Play'd foul play with our oaths: your beauty, ladies,
Hath much deform'd us, fashioning our humours
Even to the opposed end of our intents:
And what in us hath seem'd ridiculous,--
As love is full of unbefitting strains,
All wanton as a child, skipping and vain,
Form'd by the eye and therefore, like the eye,
Full of strange shapes, of habits and of forms,
Varying in subjects as the eye doth roll
To every varied object in his glance:
Which parti-coated presence of loose love
Put on by us, if, in your heavenly eyes,
Have misbecomed our oaths and gravities,
Those heavenly eyes, that look into these faults,
Suggested us to make. Therefore, ladies,
Our love being yours, the error that love makes
Is likewise yours: we to ourselves prove false,
By being once false for ever to be true
To those that make us both,--fair ladies, you:
And even that falsehood, in itself a sin,
Thus purifies itself and turns to grace.

speeches.speech: We have received your letters full of love; Your favours, the ambassadors of love; And, in our maiden council, rated them At courtship, pleasant jest and courtesy, As bombast and as lining to the time: But more devout than this in our respects Have we not been; and therefore met your loves In their own fashion, like a merriment.
lines: We have received your letters full of love;
Your favours, the ambassadors of love;
And, in our maiden council, rated them
At courtship, pleasant jest and courtesy,
As bombast and as lining to the time:
But more devout than this in our respects
Have we not been; and therefore met your loves
In their own fashion, like a merriment.

speeches.speech: Our letters, madam, show'd much more than jest.
lines: Our letters, madam, show'd much more than jest.

speeches.speech: So did our looks.
lines: So did our looks.

speeches.speech: We did not quote them so.
lines: We did not quote them so.

speeches.speech: Now, at the latest minute of the hour, Grant us your loves.
lines: Now, at the latest minute of the hour,
Grant us your loves.

speeches.speech: A time, methinks, too short To make a world-without-end bargain in. No, no, my lord, your grace is perjured much, Full of dear guiltiness; and therefore this: If for my love, as there is no such cause, You will do aught, this shall you do for me: Your oath I will not trust; but go with speed To some forlorn and naked hermitage, Remote from all the pleasures of the world; There stay until the twelve celestial signs Have brought about the annual reckoning. If this austere insociable life Change not your offer made in heat of blood; If frosts and fasts, hard lodging and thin weeds Nip not the gaudy blossoms of your love, But that it bear this trial and last love; Then, at the expiration of the year, Come challenge me, challenge me by these deserts, And, by this virgin palm now kissing thine I will be thine; and till that instant shut My woeful self up in a mourning house, Raining the tears of lamentation For the remembrance of my father's death. If this thou do deny, let our hands part, Neither entitled in the other's heart.
lines: A time, methinks, too short
To make a world-without-end bargain in.
No, no, my lord, your grace is perjured much,
Full of dear guiltiness; and therefore this:
If for my love, as there is no such cause,
You will do aught, this shall you do for me:
Your oath I will not trust; but go with speed
To some forlorn and naked hermitage,
Remote from all the pleasures of the world;
There stay until the twelve celestial signs
Have brought about the annual reckoning.
If this austere insociable life
Change not your offer made in heat of blood;
If frosts and fasts, hard lodging and thin weeds
Nip not the gaudy blossoms of your love,
But that it bear this trial and last love;
Then, at the expiration of the year,
Come challenge me, challenge me by these deserts,
And, by this virgin palm now kissing thine
I will be thine; and till that instant shut
My woeful self up in a mourning house,
Raining the tears of lamentation
For the remembrance of my father's death.
If this thou do deny, let our hands part,
Neither entitled in the other's heart.

speeches.speech: If this, or more than this, I would deny, To flatter up these powers of mine with rest, The sudden hand of death close up mine eye! Hence ever then my heart is in thy breast.
lines: If this, or more than this, I would deny,
To flatter up these powers of mine with rest,
The sudden hand of death close up mine eye!
Hence ever then my heart is in thy breast.

speeches.speech: [And what to me, my love? and what to me?
lines: [And what to me, my love? and what to me?

speeches.speech: You must be purged too, your sins are rack'd, You are attaint with faults and perjury: Therefore if you my favour mean to get, A twelvemonth shall you spend, and never rest, But seek the weary beds of people sick]
lines: You must be purged too, your sins are rack'd,
You are attaint with faults and perjury:
Therefore if you my favour mean to get,
A twelvemonth shall you spend, and never rest,
But seek the weary beds of people sick]

speeches.speech: But what to me, my love? but what to me? A wife?
lines: But what to me, my love? but what to me? A wife?

speeches.speech: A beard, fair health, and honesty; With three-fold love I wish you all these three.
lines: A beard, fair health, and honesty;
With three-fold love I wish you all these three.

speeches.speech: O, shall I say, I thank you, gentle wife?
lines: O, shall I say, I thank you, gentle wife?

speeches.speech: Not so, my lord; a twelvemonth and a day I'll mark no words that smooth-faced wooers say: Come when the king doth to my lady come; Then, if I have much love, I'll give you some.
lines: Not so, my lord; a twelvemonth and a day
I'll mark no words that smooth-faced wooers say:
Come when the king doth to my lady come;
Then, if I have much love, I'll give you some.

speeches.speech: I'll serve thee true and faithfully till then.
lines: I'll serve thee true and faithfully till then.

speeches.speech: Yet swear not, lest ye be forsworn again.
lines: Yet swear not, lest ye be forsworn again.

speeches.speech: What says Maria?
lines: What says Maria?

speeches.speech: At the twelvemonth's end I'll change my black gown for a faithful friend.
lines: At the twelvemonth's end
I'll change my black gown for a faithful friend.

speeches.speech: I'll stay with patience; but the time is long.
lines: I'll stay with patience; but the time is long.

speeches.speech: The liker you; few taller are so young.
lines: The liker you; few taller are so young.

speeches.speech: Studies my lady? mistress, look on me; Behold the window of my heart, mine eye, What humble suit attends thy answer there: Impose some service on me for thy love.
lines: Studies my lady? mistress, look on me;
Behold the window of my heart, mine eye,
What humble suit attends thy answer there:
Impose some service on me for thy love.

speeches.speech: Oft have I heard of you, my Lord Biron, Before I saw you; and the world's large tongue Proclaims you for a man replete with mocks, Full of comparisons and wounding flouts, Which you on all estates will execute That lie within the mercy of your wit. To weed this wormwood from your fruitful brain, And therewithal to win me, if you please, Without the which I am not to be won, You shall this twelvemonth term from day to day Visit the speechless sick and still converse With groaning wretches; and your task shall be, With all the fierce endeavor of your wit To enforce the pained impotent to smile.
lines: Oft have I heard of you, my Lord Biron,
Before I saw you; and the world's large tongue
Proclaims you for a man replete with mocks,
Full of comparisons and wounding flouts,
Which you on all estates will execute
That lie within the mercy of your wit.
To weed this wormwood from your fruitful brain,
And therewithal to win me, if you please,
Without the which I am not to be won,
You shall this twelvemonth term from day to day
Visit the speechless sick and still converse
With groaning wretches; and your task shall be,
With all the fierce endeavor of your wit
To enforce the pained impotent to smile.

speeches.speech: To move wild laughter in the throat of death? It cannot be; it is impossible: Mirth cannot move a soul in agony.
lines: To move wild laughter in the throat of death?
It cannot be; it is impossible:
Mirth cannot move a soul in agony.

speeches.speech: Why, that's the way to choke a gibing spirit, Whose influence is begot of that loose grace Which shallow laughing hearers give to fools: A jest's prosperity lies in the ear Of him that hears it, never in the tongue Of him that makes it: then, if sickly ears, Deaf'd with the clamours of their own dear groans, Will hear your idle scorns, continue then, And I will have you and that fault withal; But if they will not, throw away that spirit, And I shall find you empty of that fault, Right joyful of your reformation.
lines: Why, that's the way to choke a gibing spirit,
Whose influence is begot of that loose grace
Which shallow laughing hearers give to fools:
A jest's prosperity lies in the ear
Of him that hears it, never in the tongue
Of him that makes it: then, if sickly ears,
Deaf'd with the clamours of their own dear groans,
Will hear your idle scorns, continue then,
And I will have you and that fault withal;
But if they will not, throw away that spirit,
And I shall find you empty of that fault,
Right joyful of your reformation.

speeches.speech: A twelvemonth! well; befall what will befall, I'll jest a twelvemonth in an hospital.
lines: A twelvemonth! well; befall what will befall,
I'll jest a twelvemonth in an hospital.

speeches.speech: [To FERDINAND] Ay, sweet my lord; and so I take my leave.
lines: [To FERDINAND] Ay, sweet my lord; and so I take my leave.

speeches.speech: No, madam; we will bring you on your way.
lines: No, madam; we will bring you on your way.

speeches.speech: Our wooing doth not end like an old play; Jack hath not Jill: these ladies' courtesy Might well have made our sport a comedy.
lines: Our wooing doth not end like an old play;
Jack hath not Jill: these ladies' courtesy
Might well have made our sport a comedy.

speeches.speech: Come, sir, it wants a twelvemonth and a day, And then 'twill end.
lines: Come, sir, it wants a twelvemonth and a day,
And then 'twill end.

speeches.speech: That's too long for a play.
lines: That's too long for a play.

speeches.speech: Sweet majesty, vouchsafe me,--
lines: Sweet majesty, vouchsafe me,--

speeches.speech: Was not that Hector?
lines: Was not that Hector?

speeches.speech: The worthy knight of Troy.
lines: The worthy knight of Troy.

speeches.speech: I will kiss thy royal finger, and take leave. I am a votary; I have vowed to Jaquenetta to hold the plough for her sweet love three years. But, most esteemed greatness, will you hear the dialogue that the two learned men have compiled in praise of the owl and the cuckoo? It should have followed in the end of our show.
lines: I will kiss thy royal finger, and take leave. I am
a votary; I have vowed to Jaquenetta to hold the
plough for her sweet love three years. But, most
esteemed greatness, will you hear the dialogue that
the two learned men have compiled in praise of the
owl and the cuckoo? It should have followed in the
end of our show.

speeches.speech: Call them forth quickly; we will do so.
lines: Call them forth quickly; we will do so.

speeches.speech: Holla! approach. [Re-enter HOLOFERNES, SIR NATHANIEL, MOTH, COSTARD,] and others] This side is Hiems, Winter, this Ver, the Spring; the one maintained by the owl, the other by the cuckoo. Ver, begin. [THE SONG] SPRING. When daisies pied and violets blue And lady-smocks all silver-white And cuckoo-buds of yellow hue Do paint the meadows with delight, The cuckoo then, on every tree, Mocks married men; for thus sings he, . Cuckoo; Cuckoo, cuckoo: O word of fear, Unpleasing to a married ear! When shepherds pipe on oaten straws And merry larks are ploughmen's clocks, When turtles tread, and rooks, and daws, And maidens bleach their summer smocks The cuckoo then, on every tree, Mocks married men; for thus sings he, . Cuckoo; Cuckoo, cuckoo: O word of fear, Unpleasing to a married ear! WINTER. When icicles hang by the wall And Dick the shepherd blows his nail And Tom bears logs into the hall And milk comes frozen home in pail, When blood is nipp'd and ways be foul, Then nightly sings the staring owl, . Tu-whit; Tu-who, a merry note, While greasy Joan doth keel the pot. When all aloud the wind doth blow And coughing drowns the parson's saw And birds sit brooding in the snow And Marian's nose looks red and raw, When roasted crabs hiss in the bowl, Then nightly sings the staring owl, . Tu-whit; Tu-who, a merry note, While greasy Joan doth keel the pot.

[Re-enter HOLOFERNES, SIR NATHANIEL, MOTH, COSTARD,]
and others]

the one maintained by the owl, the other by the

[THE SONG]
SPRING.
When daisies pied and violets blue
And lady-smocks all silver-white
And cuckoo-buds of yellow hue
Do paint the meadows with delight,
The cuckoo then, on every tree,
Mocks married men; for thus sings he, . Cuckoo;
Cuckoo, cuckoo: O word of fear,
Unpleasing to a married ear!
When shepherds pipe on oaten straws
And merry larks are ploughmen's clocks,
When turtles tread, and rooks, and daws,
And maidens bleach their summer smocks
The cuckoo then, on every tree,
Mocks married men; for thus sings he, . Cuckoo;
Cuckoo, cuckoo: O word of fear,
Unpleasing to a married ear!
WINTER.
When icicles hang by the wall
And Dick the shepherd blows his nail
And Tom bears logs into the hall
And milk comes frozen home in pail,
When blood is nipp'd and ways be foul,
Then nightly sings the staring owl, . Tu-whit;
Tu-who, a merry note,
While greasy Joan doth keel the pot.
When all aloud the wind doth blow
And coughing drowns the parson's saw
And birds sit brooding in the snow
And Marian's nose looks red and raw,
When roasted crabs hiss in the bowl,
Then nightly sings the staring owl, . Tu-whit;
Tu-who, a merry note,
While greasy Joan doth keel the pot.

speeches.speech: The words of Mercury are harsh after the songs of Apollo. You that way: we this way.
lines: The words of Mercury are harsh after the songs of
Apollo. You that way: we this way.
